5 seconds

---

# Chapter 98 — Redis Pub/Sub Channels

```text
messages

presence

typing

notifications

groups

server_events

announcements
```

---

# Chapter 99 — File Storage Layout

```text
uploads/

year/

month/

conversation_uuid/

message_uuid/

attachment_uuid.bin
```

Benefits

Efficient backups

Directory scalability

Fast lookup

---

# Chapter 100 — Attachment Encryption

Every attachment shall be encrypted using AES-256-GCM.

Each attachment has:

Random key

Random nonce

Authentication tag

SHA-256 checksum

Stored separately.

---

# Chapter 101 — Database Indexing

Indexes shall exist on

users.username

users.email

messages.conversation_id

messages.created_at

attachments.message_id

contacts.owner_id

sessions.user_id

audit_logs.timestamp

conversation_members.user_id

---

# Chapter 102 — Database Transactions

Transactions required for

Message send

Group creation

User creation

Attachment upload

Permission changes

Audit insertion

Rollback on failure.

---

# Chapter 103 — Migration Order

Database migrations shall execute in this order.

1 Users

2 Roles

3 Permissions

4 RolePermissions

5 UserKeys

6 Conversations

7 ConversationMembers

8 Messages

9 MessageKeys

10 Attachments

11 Contacts

12 Sessions

13 ReadReceipts

14 Reactions

15 AuditLogs

16 Announcements

17 Settings

---

# Chapter 104 — Backup Strategy

Daily PostgreSQL dump

Hourly Redis snapshot

Incremental attachment backup

Weekly full backup

Monthly archive

Quarterly verification restore

---

# Chapter 105 — Data Retention

Audit Logs

7 years

Messages

Unlimited unless deleted

Deleted Messages

Soft delete for 30 days

Attachments

Deleted after retention expires

Sessions

30 days

Typing Indicators

5 seconds

Presence

60 seconds

---

# Chapter 106 — Future Scalability

The schema shall support future features without requiring redesign.

Reserved support for:

Voice Calls

Video Calls

Screen Sharing

Bots

Plugins

Multiple Organisations

Federation

Message Threads

Polls

Pinned Messages

Scheduled Messages

Remote Desktop Sessions

AI Assistant Integration

---

# End of Part 5

Part 6 will define the complete cryptographic architecture. This will be one of the most important sections of the specification and will include:

- Threat model
- End-to-end encryption design
- X25519 key exchange
- Ed25519 digital signatures
- AES-256-GCM message encryption
- Secure key storage
- Forward secrecy considerations
- Key rotation
- Recovery keys
- Complete encryption/decryption sequence diagrams
- Exact responsibilities of every cryptographic class

# Part 6 — Cryptographic Architecture & Security Model

---

# Chapter 107 — Security Philosophy

BlueBubbles is designed under the assumption that **the network cannot be trusted**.

Although the application operates entirely within a Local Area Network (LAN), the following assumptions shall always be made:

- Any workstation may become compromised.
- Network traffic may be intercepted.
- Attackers may gain access to the server.
- Attackers may obtain database backups.
- Attackers may obtain encrypted attachment files.
- Attackers may attempt replay attacks.
- Attackers may impersonate users.

Therefore:

**Every message shall remain encrypted from the moment it leaves the sender until it is decrypted by the intended recipient.**

The server shall never possess plaintext messages.

---

# Chapter 108 — Threat Model

BlueBubbles is designed to defend against:

✔ Packet sniffing

✔ Database theft

✔ Attachment theft

✔ Replay attacks

✔ Message tampering

✔ User impersonation

✔ Session hijacking

✔ Stolen backups

✔ Network interception

✔ Malicious insiders without cryptographic keys

BlueBubbles does **not** attempt to defend against:

- Malware running on the user's own machine.
- Physical theft of an unlocked workstation.
- Keyloggers.
- Memory scraping while the client is running.

These risks fall outside the scope of Version 1.0.

---

# Chapter 109 — Cryptographic Algorithms

The following algorithms shall be used.

| Purpose | Algorithm |
|----------|-----------|
| Message Encryption | AES-256-GCM |
| File Encryption | AES-256-GCM |
| Public Key Encryption | X25519 |
| Digital Signatures | Ed25519 |
| Password Hashing | Argon2id |
| File Integrity | SHA-256 |
| Random Number Generation | OS Secure Random |

Only modern, well-established cryptographic algorithms shall be used.

No custom cryptography shall ever be implemented.

---

# Chapter 110 — Cryptographic Libraries

The implementation shall use only trusted Python libraries.

Required libraries:

```text
cryptography

PyNaCl

argon2-cffi

hashlib

secrets
```

The implementation shall never:

- Implement AES manually.
- Implement hashing manually.
- Implement key exchange manually.
- Generate random numbers using the random module.

---

# Chapter 111 — Cryptographic Objects

The client shall manage the following objects.

```text
Identity Key Pair

Encryption Key Pair

Signing Key Pair

Session Key

File Encryption Key

Nonce

Authentication Tag

Digital Signature
```

Each object has exactly one responsibility.

---

# Chapter 112 — User Identity

Every user shall possess a permanent identity.

Each identity consists of:

```text
User UUID

Username

Display Name

Encryption Public Key

Encryption Private Key

Signing Public Key

Signing Private Key
```

Public keys may be stored on the server.

Private keys shall always remain encrypted.

---

# Chapter 113 — Key Generation

When a new user account is created:

```text
Generate Encryption Key Pair

↓

Generate Signing Key Pair

↓

Encrypt Private Keys

↓

Upload Public Keys

↓

Store Encrypted Private Keys
```

Private keys shall never exist unencrypted on persistent storage.

---

# Chapter 114 — Private Key Protection

Private keys shall be encrypted before being written to disk.

Protection Process

```text
User Password

↓

Argon2id

↓

256-bit Key

↓

AES-256-GCM

↓

Encrypted Private Key
```

Only the authenticated user can decrypt their own private keys.

---

# Chapter 115 — Public Keys

Public keys are stored by the server.

Purpose

Allow other users to encrypt messages.

Allow signature verification.

Public keys are not confidential.

They are authenticated.

---

# Chapter 116 — Message Encryption

Every message follows the same process.

```text
User Types Message

↓

Generate Random AES Key

↓

Generate Random Nonce

↓

Encrypt Message

↓

Encrypt AES Key using Recipient Public Key

↓

Sign Message

↓

Upload
```

Only recipients can decrypt the AES key.

---

# Chapter 117 — Message Structure

Each message consists of:

```text
Encrypted Payload

Encrypted AES Key

Nonce

Authentication Tag

Digital Signature

Sender ID

Timestamp

Protocol Version
```

The plaintext message is never transmitted.

---

# Chapter 118 — Message Decryption

Recipient Process

```text
Receive Message

↓

Locate Encrypted AES Key

↓

Decrypt AES Key

↓

Decrypt Payload

↓

Verify Signature

↓

Display Message
```

If verification fails:

The message shall not be displayed.

---

# Chapter 119 — File Encryption

Attachments shall use independent encryption keys.

Process

```text
Generate Random AES Key

↓

Encrypt File

↓

Encrypt AES Key

↓

Store Encrypted File

↓

Upload
```

Each attachment receives a unique key.

---

# Chapter 120 — Nonce Generation

AES-GCM requires a unique nonce.

Every encryption operation generates a new nonce.

Nonces shall never be reused.

The nonce shall be stored alongside ciphertext.

---

# Chapter 121 — Authentication Tags

AES-GCM automatically produces an authentication tag.

Purpose

Detect tampering.

If authentication fails:

Reject message.

Log event.

Notify user.

---

# Chapter 122 — Digital Signatures

Every outgoing message shall be digitally signed.

Purpose

Verify sender identity.

Prevent forgery.

Prevent modification.

Signature Process

```text
Ciphertext

↓

Ed25519

↓

Signature

↓

Transmit
```

---

# Chapter 123 — Signature Verification

Recipient Process

```text
Receive Ciphertext

↓

Retrieve Sender Public Key

↓

Verify Signature

↓

Accept or Reject
```

Modified messages automatically fail verification.

---

# Chapter 124 — Replay Protection

Every message shall include:

```text
UUID

Timestamp

Sequence Number
```

Duplicate messages shall be rejected.

---

# Chapter 125 — Secure Random Generation

Random values shall be generated using:

```python
secrets.token_bytes()
```

Never

```python
random.random()
```

Never

```python
numpy.random
```

---

# Chapter 126 — Password Storage

Passwords shall never be stored.

Only Argon2id password hashes.

The original password is unrecoverable.

---

# Chapter 127 — Session Tokens

After login

```text
Username

↓

Password

↓

LDAP Authentication

↓

JWT Access Token

↓

Refresh Token

↓

Encrypted Local Storage
```

Tokens expire automatically.

---

# Chapter 128 — Local Encryption

Client-side cache shall also be encrypted.

Encrypted

Recent Messages

Settings

Cached Files

Session Data

Private Keys

Nothing sensitive is stored unencrypted.

---

# Chapter 129 — Secure Memory

Sensitive values should remain in memory for as little time as practical.

Examples

Private Keys

Passwords

AES Keys

Session Secrets

These objects should be discarded immediately after use.

---

# Chapter 130 — Key Rotation

Users may rotate keys.

Process

```text
Generate New Keys

↓

Upload Public Keys

↓

Retain Previous Keys

↓

Re-encrypt Future Messages

↓

Archive Old Keys
```

Old messages remain decryptable.

---

# Chapter 131 — Forward Compatibility

The protocol shall support:

Future Algorithms

Algorithm Replacement

Version Negotiation

Additional Key Types

Without requiring a database redesign.

---

# Chapter 132 — Recovery Keys

Administrators shall **not** possess universal decryption keys.

Version 1.0 intentionally avoids organisational backdoors.

This simplifies implementation while providing genuine end-to-end encryption.

Future versions may optionally support escrow keys if required.

---

# Chapter 133 — Cryptographic Classes

```text
EncryptionManager

KeyManager

SignatureManager

NonceGenerator

PasswordManager

HashManager

SecureRandom

KeyStorage

SessionCipher
```

Each class has exactly one responsibility.

---

# Chapter 134 — EncryptionManager

Responsibilities

Encrypt messages

Decrypt messages

Generate AES keys

Generate nonces

Validate authentication tags

No storage responsibilities.

---

# Chapter 135 — KeyManager

Responsibilities

Generate key pairs

Load keys

Rotate keys

Import keys

Export keys

Encrypt private keys

Decrypt private keys

---

# Chapter 136 — SignatureManager

Responsibilities

Create signatures

Verify signatures

Load signing keys

Reject invalid signatures

---

# Chapter 137 — PasswordManager

Responsibilities

Hash passwords

Verify passwords

Derive encryption keys

Never store passwords.

---

# Chapter 138 — Security Rules

The following rules shall never be violated.

✔ Never store plaintext messages.

✔ Never store plaintext files.

✔ Never reuse AES keys.

✔ Never reuse nonces.

✔ Never transmit private keys.

✔ Never log passwords.

✔ Never log private keys.

✔ Never disable authentication.

✔ Never bypass signature verification.

✔ Never trust client input.

---

# Chapter 139 — Future Cryptographic Features

The architecture shall support future implementation of:

- Multi-device synchronisation
- Hardware security keys
- TPM integration
- Windows Hello authentication
- Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)
- Secure encrypted backups
- Cryptographic key escrow (optional)
- Certificate-based authentication

These features are outside the scope of Version 1.0 but shall be considered during architectural design.

---

# Chapter 140 — Summary

The cryptographic architecture is designed to remain understandable while following modern security practices.

It prioritises:

- Simplicity
- Correctness
- Maintainability
- Performance
- End-to-end encryption
- Strong authentication
- Integrity verification

No proprietary algorithms are used.

All cryptographic operations rely on industry-standard, well-tested libraries.

---

# End of Part 6

Part 7 will define the **complete networking architecture**, including:

- FastAPI server structure
- REST API design
- WebSocket protocol
- Custom packet formats
- JSON schemas
- Request/response lifecycle
- Connection management
- Heartbeats
- Automatic reconnection
- Message delivery guarantees
- Rate limiting
- Network error recovery

This will become the blueprint for every interaction between the client and the server before any networking code is written.

# Part 7 — Networking Architecture & Communication Protocol

---

# Chapter 141 — Networking Philosophy

BlueBubbles is designed around a client-server architecture.

Clients never communicate directly with one another.

Every interaction passes through the application server.

This allows:

- Centralised authentication
- Consistent permissions
- Reliable message delivery
- Audit logging
- Easier administration
- Simplified deployment
- Reduced client complexity

---

# Chapter 142 — Communication Overview

All communication follows the same architecture.

```text
User

↓

Desktop Client

↓

HTTPS / WebSocket

↓

FastAPI Server

↓

Application Services

↓

Repositories

↓

PostgreSQL / Redis

↓

Response

↓

Desktop Client
```

No client is ever aware of another client's IP address.

---

# Chapter 143 — Communication Types

BlueBubbles uses two communication mechanisms.

## REST API

Used for:

Authentication

Downloading history

Uploading files

Searching

Administration

Settings

User management

---

## WebSockets

Used for:

Incoming messages

Typing indicators

Presence updates

Notifications

Read receipts

File transfer progress

Announcements

Real-time events

---

# Chapter 144 — Protocol Selection

REST is chosen because it provides:

Reliable request-response communication

Easy debugging

Simple authentication

Clear API documentation

Standard HTTP status codes

WebSockets are chosen because they provide:

Persistent connections

Low latency

Reduced overhead

Instant notifications

Real-time communication

---

# Chapter 145 — HTTPS Requirements

All HTTP communication shall use HTTPS.

Even inside a LAN.

Reasons

Prevent packet sniffing

Prevent credential theft

Prevent session hijacking

Support future deployment

TLS Version

TLS 1.3 preferred

TLS 1.2 minimum

---

# Chapter 146 — Connection Lifecycle

```text
Launch Client

↓

Load Configuration

↓

Connect HTTPS

↓

Authenticate

↓

Receive JWT

↓

Open WebSocket

↓

Authenticate WebSocket

↓

Synchronise Data

↓

Ready
```

---

# Chapter 147 — FastAPI Application Structure

```text
FastAPI

│

├── Authentication

├── Users

├── Conversations

├── Messages

├── Files

├── Presence

├── Groups

├── Administration

├── Settings

├── Search

└── Health
```

Each module shall expose its own router.

---

# Chapter 148 — API Versioning

All REST endpoints shall include version numbers.

Example

```text
/api/v1/auth/login

/api/v1/messages/send

/api/v1/groups/create

/api/v1/files/upload
```

Future versions shall not break older clients.

---

# Chapter 149 — REST Endpoint Categories

Authentication

```text
POST /login

POST /logout

POST /refresh

GET /me
```

---

Users

```text
GET /users

GET /user/{id}

PATCH /user

GET /contacts
```

---

Messages

```text
POST /message

GET /conversation

DELETE /message

PATCH /message
```

---

Groups

```text
POST /group

PATCH /group

DELETE /group

POST /group/join

POST /group/leave
```

---

Files

```text
POST /upload

GET /download

DELETE /attachment
```

---

Administration

```text
GET /statistics

GET /audit

GET /users

PATCH /users

POST /announcement
```

---

# Chapter 150 — HTTP Status Codes

Use standard HTTP responses.

```text
200 OK

201 Created

204 No Content

400 Bad Request

401 Unauthorized

403 Forbidden

404 Not Found

409 Conflict

413 Payload Too Large

422 Validation Error

429 Too Many Requests

500 Internal Server Error
```

No custom status codes.

---

# Chapter 151 — JSON Standards

Every request and response shall use JSON.

Example

```json
{
    "conversation_id": "...",
    "message": "...",
    "timestamp": "...",
    "protocol_version": 1
}
```

Property names use snake_case.

---

# Chapter 152 — API Response Format

Every response shall follow the same structure.

Successful response

```json
{
    "success": true,
    "data": {},
    "message": null
}
```

Error response

```json
{
    "success": false,
    "error": {
        "code": "INVALID_TOKEN",
        "message": "Session has expired."
    }
}
```

---

# Chapter 153 — Authentication Headers

Every authenticated request includes

```text
Authorization

Bearer <JWT>
```

Tokens shall never appear in URLs.

---

# Chapter 154 — WebSocket Authentication

Immediately after connecting

```text
Client

↓

Connect

↓

Authenticate

↓

Receive Success

↓

Subscribe

↓

Receive Events
```

Unauthenticated sockets shall be disconnected.

---

# Chapter 155 — WebSocket Event Types

Supported events

```text
MESSAGE_RECEIVED

MESSAGE_UPDATED

MESSAGE_DELETED

MESSAGE_READ

USER_TYPING

USER_STOPPED_TYPING

PRESENCE_CHANGED

FILE_UPLOAD_PROGRESS

FILE_DOWNLOAD_PROGRESS

GROUP_CREATED

GROUP_UPDATED

GROUP_DELETED

ANNOUNCEMENT

SERVER_NOTIFICATION
```

Each event has its own DTO.

---

# Chapter 156 — Heartbeats

Heartbeat interval

30 seconds

Process

```text
Client

↓

Heartbeat

↓

Server

↓

Heartbeat Response

↓

Reset Timeout
```

If three heartbeats are missed

Connection considered lost.

Reconnect begins.

---

# Chapter 157 — Automatic Reconnection

When disconnected

```text
Attempt 1

↓

2 seconds

↓

Attempt 2

↓

4 seconds

↓

Attempt 3

↓

8 seconds

↓

Attempt 4

↓

16 seconds

↓

Maximum 30 seconds
```

Uses exponential backoff.

---

# Chapter 158 — Offline Queue

If connection is unavailable

Outgoing requests are queued locally.

Queue contents

Messages

Reactions

Read receipts

Presence updates

When connection returns

Queue processes automatically.

---

# Chapter 159 — Delivery Guarantees

Every message has a delivery state.

```text
Pending

Uploading

Delivered to Server

Delivered to Recipient

Read

Failed
```

Messages shall never silently disappear.

---

# Chapter 160 — Message Acknowledgements

Workflow

```text
Client

↓

Send Message

↓

Server Stores Message

↓

Server Returns ACK

↓

Client Updates Status
```

If ACK not received

Retry.

---

# Chapter 161 — Typing Indicators

Typing updates shall not be stored permanently.

Process

```text
User Begins Typing

↓

Send Event

↓

Redis

↓

Other Clients

↓

Display Typing
```

Automatically expires.

---

# Chapter 162 — Presence Updates

Presence uses Redis Pub/Sub.

Workflow

```text
Heartbeat

↓

Redis

↓

Subscribers

↓

Update Presence
```

No SQL queries required.

---

# Chapter 163 — File Upload Protocol

```text
Client

↓

Split File

↓

Encrypt

↓

Chunk Upload

↓

Verify

↓

Complete Upload

↓

Notify Recipient
```

Large files shall upload in parallel chunks.

---

# Chapter 164 — File Download Protocol

```text
Recipient

↓

Download Chunks

↓

Verify Hash

↓

Decrypt

↓

Merge

↓

Save
```

Interrupted downloads resume automatically.

---

# Chapter 165 — Rate Limiting

Protect server resources.

Limits

Authentication

10 requests/minute

Messages

120/minute

Search

60/minute

Administration

30/minute

Uploads

Concurrent uploads configurable.

---

# Chapter 166 — Compression

Compression shall occur before encryption.

Supported

Images

Documents

Logs

Compression skipped for:

Already compressed media

Encrypted archives

Videos

---

# Chapter 167 — Request Validation

Every request shall be validated.

Validation includes

Authentication

Permissions

Required fields

Length

Data types

Conversation membership

Rate limits

Requests failing validation never reach business logic.

---

# Chapter 168 — Error Recovery

Recoverable failures

Temporary disconnect

Timeout

Redis restart

Server restart

Retry automatically.

Fatal failures

Invalid credentials

Invalid JWT

Permission denied

Invalid protocol version

Return structured error.

---

# Chapter 169 — Protocol Versioning

Every request contains

```text
protocol_version
```

Server rejects unsupported versions.

Future versions remain backwards compatible where practical.

---

# Chapter 170 — Network Logging

Log

Authentication

Disconnects

Reconnects

Failed Requests

Rate Limits

Server Errors

API Exceptions

Never log

Passwords

Private Keys

Plaintext Messages

JWT Tokens

---

# Chapter 171 — Network Security Rules

The networking layer shall never

Access SQL directly

Decrypt messages

Modify permissions

Store passwords

Read private keys

It only transports data.

---

# Chapter 172 — Future Networking Features

Architecture shall support

IPv6

QUIC

HTTP/3

Push Notifications

Multi-server deployment

Load balancing

Reverse proxy support

Clustering

Federation

Without redesigning the networking layer.

---

# End of Part 7

Part 8 will define the **complete REST API specification**, including every endpoint, request DTO, response DTO, validation rule, authentication requirement, permissions matrix, pagination standard, filtering, searching, and OpenAPI documentation. This will serve as the definitive API contract between the client and server before implementation begins.

# Part 8 — Complete REST API Specification

---

# Chapter 173 — API Philosophy

The REST API forms the contract between the desktop client and the BlueBubbles server.

No client shall ever communicate directly with the database.

No client shall bypass the API layer.

The API shall remain:

- Stateless
- Predictable
- Versioned
- Secure
- Fully documented
- Backwards compatible where practical

All business logic shall reside within the service layer—not inside endpoint handlers.

---

# Chapter 174 — API Architecture

```text
Desktop Client

↓

API Client

↓

HTTPS

↓

FastAPI Router

↓

Validation

↓

Authentication

↓

Permission Checks

↓

Application Service

↓

Repository

↓

Database

↓

Response DTO

↓

Client
```

Every endpoint shall follow this flow.

---

# Chapter 175 — Endpoint Standards

All endpoints shall:

- Require authentication unless explicitly public.
- Return JSON.
- Validate request bodies.
- Validate permissions.
- Return standard HTTP status codes.
- Never expose internal exceptions.

---

# Chapter 176 — Authentication Endpoints

### Login

```http
POST /api/v1/auth/login
```

Request

```json
{
    "username": "j.smith",
    "password": "********"
}
```

Response

```json
{
    "success": true,
    "access_token": "...",
    "refresh_token": "...",
    "expires_in": 3600,
    "user": {}
}
```

---

### Logout

```http
POST /api/v1/auth/logout
```

Requires

JWT

Response

204 No Content

---

### Refresh Token

```http
POST /api/v1/auth/refresh
```

Returns

New access token.

---

### Current User

```http
GET /api/v1/auth/me
```

Returns

Current authenticated profile.

---

# Chapter 177 — User Endpoints

### Get User

```http
GET /api/v1/users/{id}
```

Returns

```text
Display Name

Department

Status

Presence

Role

Avatar
```

---

### Update Profile

```http
PATCH /api/v1/users/profile
```

Allows

Display Name

Status Message

Avatar

---

### Search Users

```http
GET /api/v1/users/search
```

Query Parameters

```text
query

department

limit

offset
```

---

# Chapter 178 — Contact Endpoints

### List Contacts

```http
GET /api/v1/contacts
```

Returns

Sorted contacts.

---

### Add Contact

```http
POST /api/v1/contacts
```

Request

```json
{
    "username": "john.smith"
}
```

---

### Remove Contact

```http
DELETE /api/v1/contacts/{id}
```

---

### Favourite Contact

```http
PATCH /api/v1/contacts/{id}/favourite
```

---

### Block Contact

```http
PATCH /api/v1/contacts/{id}/block
```

---

# Chapter 179 — Conversation Endpoints

### List Conversations

```http
GET /api/v1/conversations
```

Returns

Recent conversations.

---

### Create Direct Conversation

```http
POST /api/v1/conversations/direct
```

---

### Create Group

```http
POST /api/v1/conversations/group
```

Request

```json
{
    "name": "IT Support",
    "members": []
}
```

---

### Rename Conversation

```http
PATCH /api/v1/conversations/{id}
```

---

### Archive Conversation

```http
POST /api/v1/conversations/{id}/archive
```

---

# Chapter 180 — Messaging Endpoints

### Send Message

```http
POST /api/v1/messages
```

Request DTO

```json
{
    "conversation_id": "...",
    "ciphertext": "...",
    "nonce": "...",
    "signature": "...",
    "encrypted_keys": []
}
```

The server shall never receive plaintext.

---

### Edit Message

```http
PATCH /api/v1/messages/{id}
```

Only sender may edit.

---

### Delete Message

```http
DELETE /api/v1/messages/{id}
```

Soft delete only.

---

### Load Messages

```http
GET /api/v1/messages
```

Parameters

```text
conversation_id

limit

before

after
```

Supports infinite scrolling.

---

### Search Messages

```http
GET /api/v1/messages/search
```

Parameters

```text
query

conversation

sender

date_from

date_to
```

---

# Chapter 181 — Attachment Endpoints

### Upload Attachment

```http
POST /api/v1/files/upload
```

Multipart upload.

Encrypted client-side.

---

### Download Attachment

```http
GET /api/v1/files/{id}
```

Returns encrypted file.

---

### Delete Attachment

```http
DELETE /api/v1/files/{id}
```

Soft delete.

---

# Chapter 182 — Group Endpoints

### Add Member

```http
POST /api/v1/groups/{id}/members
```

---

### Remove Member

```http
DELETE /api/v1/groups/{id}/members/{user}
```

---

### Promote Moderator

```http
PATCH /api/v1/groups/{id}/moderators
```

---

### Leave Group

```http
POST /api/v1/groups/{id}/leave
```

---

# Chapter 183 — Presence Endpoints

### Update Presence

```http
PATCH /api/v1/presence
```

Values

Online

Away

Busy

Offline

Invisible

---

### Get Presence

```http
GET /api/v1/presence/{user}
```

---

# Chapter 184 — Notification Endpoints

### Mark Read

```http
POST /api/v1/notifications/read
```

---

### Clear Notifications

```http
DELETE /api/v1/notifications
```

---

# Chapter 185 — Administration Endpoints

Administrator role required.

### List Users

```http
GET /api/v1/admin/users
```

---

### Disable User

```http
PATCH /api/v1/admin/users/{id}/disable
```

---

### Enable User

```http
PATCH /api/v1/admin/users/{id}/enable
```

---

### View Audit Log

```http
GET /api/v1/admin/audit
```

Supports filtering.

---

### Server Statistics

```http
GET /api/v1/admin/statistics
```

Returns

CPU

RAM

Disk

Connected Users

Messages Today

Uploads

Downloads

Redis Health

Database Health

---

### Broadcast Announcement

```http
POST /api/v1/admin/announcements
```

---

# Chapter 186 — Health Endpoints

### Server Status

```http
GET /api/v1/health
```

Returns

```json
{
    "status": "healthy"
}
```

---

### Detailed Health

Administrator only.

```http
GET /api/v1/health/details
```

---

# Chapter 187 — Pagination Standard

Large datasets shall never be returned in one response.

Parameters

```text
limit

offset

before

after
```

Default

50 records

Maximum

250 records

---

# Chapter 188 — Filtering Standard

Filtering uses query parameters.

Example

```http
/messages?sender=123

/messages?date_from=...

/users?department=IT
```

---

# Chapter 189 — Sorting Standard

Allowed values

```text
ascending

descending

alphabetical

recent

oldest
```

---

# Chapter 190 — Validation Rules

Every request shall validate:

Authentication

Permissions

Required Fields

Maximum Length

Minimum Length

Allowed Characters

UUID Format

Date Format

Conversation Membership

Rate Limit

Validation occurs before business logic.

---

# Chapter 191 — Error Codes

Application errors shall use stable identifiers.

Examples

```text
INVALID_LOGIN

INVALID_TOKEN

SESSION_EXPIRED

USER_DISABLED

GROUP_NOT_FOUND

MESSAGE_TOO_LONG

ATTACHMENT_TOO_LARGE

NOT_GROUP_MEMBER

INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS

RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED
```

These remain stable between releases.

---

# Chapter 192 — Permission Matrix

| Endpoint | Employee | Helpdesk | HR | Admin |
|-----------|----------|----------|----|-------|
| Send Message | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Upload File | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Create Group | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Disable User | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| View Audit | ✘ | Limited | ✔ | ✔ |
| Server Statistics | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Broadcast Announcement | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Manage Roles | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ |

---

# Chapter 193 — DTO Standards

Every endpoint shall have dedicated DTOs.

Never reuse database models.

Example

```text
LoginRequest

LoginResponse

SendMessageRequest

SendMessageResponse

ConversationSummary

AttachmentMetadata

UserProfile

NotificationDTO
```

DTOs contain no business logic.

---

# Chapter 194 — OpenAPI Documentation

Every endpoint shall include:

- Summary
- Description
- Parameters
- Request Schema
- Response Schema
- Error Codes
- Required Permissions
- Example Requests
- Example Responses

The FastAPI-generated OpenAPI specification shall always remain in sync with the implementation.

---

# Chapter 195 — API Security Rules

The API shall never:

- Accept plaintext private keys.
- Return database IDs unnecessarily.
- Leak stack traces.
- Return SQL errors.
- Trust client-supplied permissions.
- Trust client timestamps.
- Accept unsigned administrative actions.

All permissions shall be verified server-side.

---

# Chapter 196 — API Version Lifecycle

Versioning strategy

```text
v1

↓

v2

↓

v3
```

Older clients should continue to function where practical.

Breaking changes require a new major API version.

---

# End of Part 8

Part 9 will define the **complete desktop client architecture**, including:

- Main window layout
- Navigation system
- MVVM/MVC controller structure
- Chat interface
- Settings pages
- Notification system
- Theme engine
- Local cache
- Background workers
- Drag-and-drop file uploads
- Keyboard shortcuts
- Every GUI component and interaction

This will be the complete blueprint for the PySide6 desktop application.

# Part 9 — Desktop Client Architecture and PySide6 Interface

---

# Chapter 197 — Client Application Purpose

The BlueBubbles desktop client is the application installed on each employee’s Windows computer.

It is responsible for:

* Displaying the graphical user interface.
* Authenticating the user.
* Maintaining a connection to the BlueBubbles server.
* Encrypting outgoing messages and files.
* Decrypting incoming messages and files.
* Managing local application settings.
* Caching recently accessed data.
* Displaying notifications.
* Recovering from temporary network failures.

The desktop client shall be written using:

```text
Python 3.13+
PySide6
Qt 6
httpx
websockets
cryptography
SQLCipher or encrypted SQLite
```

The client shall support Windows 10 and Windows 11.

---

# Chapter 198 — Client Architectural Pattern

The client shall use the Model-View-ViewModel pattern.

```text
Model

↓

ViewModel

↓

View
```

This pattern is chosen because PySide6 uses signals and slots, which fit naturally with ViewModel-based state management.

---

## Model

Models represent application data.

Examples:

```text
UserModel
MessageModel
ConversationModel
AttachmentModel
ContactModel
NotificationModel
SettingsModel
```

Models shall not contain GUI code.

---

## View

Views are PySide6 windows, pages, dialogs and widgets.

Examples:

```text
LoginView
MainWindow
ChatView
SettingsView
ContactListView
MessageWidget
```

Views shall only:

* Display data.
* Collect user input.
* Emit user interface events.
* Bind to ViewModel state.

Views shall not contain networking, database or cryptographic logic.

---

## ViewModel

ViewModels connect Views to application services.

Examples:

```text
LoginViewModel
MainViewModel
ChatViewModel
ConversationListViewModel
ContactListViewModel
SettingsViewModel
ProfileViewModel
AdminViewModel
```

ViewModels shall:

* Expose display-ready data.
* Validate user input.
* Call client services.
* Update loading states.
* Handle recoverable errors.
* Emit signals when state changes.

---

# Chapter 199 — Client Layer Structure

The client application shall use the following structure.

```text
client/
│
├── main.py
├── application.py
├── bootstrap.py
│
├── configuration/
│   ├── client_config.py
│   └── config_loader.py
│
├── models/
│   ├── user.py
│   ├── message.py
│   ├── conversation.py
│   ├── attachment.py
│   ├── contact.py
│   ├── notification.py
│   └── settings.py
│
├── views/
│   ├── windows/
│   ├── pages/
│   ├── dialogs/
│   └── widgets/
│
├── viewmodels/
│   ├── login_viewmodel.py
│   ├── main_viewmodel.py
│   ├── chat_viewmodel.py
│   ├── contact_viewmodel.py
│   ├── settings_viewmodel.py
│   └── admin_viewmodel.py
│
├── services/
│   ├── authentication_service.py
│   ├── messaging_service.py
│   ├── conversation_service.py
│   ├── contact_service.py
│   ├── file_transfer_service.py
│   ├── notification_service.py
│   ├── settings_service.py
│   └── presence_service.py
│
├── networking/
│   ├── api_client.py
│   ├── websocket_client.py
│   ├── connection_manager.py
│   ├── retry_manager.py
│   └── request_queue.py
│
├── security/
│   ├── encryption_manager.py
│   ├── key_manager.py
│   ├── signature_manager.py
│   └── secure_store.py
│
├── storage/
│   ├── cache_database.py
│   ├── conversation_cache.py
│   ├── attachment_cache.py
│   ├── settings_store.py
│   └── session_store.py
│
├── workers/
│   ├── upload_worker.py
│   ├── download_worker.py
│   ├── encryption_worker.py
│   ├── decryption_worker.py
│   └── thumbnail_worker.py
│
└── resources/
    ├── icons/
    ├── fonts/
    ├── styles/
    └── images/
```

---

# Chapter 200 — Application Startup

The client startup sequence shall be controlled by `ClientApplication`.

```python
class ClientApplication:
    """Controls the lifecycle of the BlueBubbles desktop client."""
```

Startup sequence:

```text
Create QApplication

↓

Load configuration

↓

Initialise structured logging

↓

Create dependency container

↓

Load user settings

↓

Load selected theme

↓

Initialise secure local storage

↓

Initialise networking services

↓

Display login window

↓

Authenticate user

↓

Load main window

↓

Connect WebSocket

↓

Synchronise conversations

↓

Begin presence heartbeat
```

If startup fails, a clear error dialog shall be displayed.

The client shall never silently terminate.

---

# Chapter 201 — Dependency Container

The application shall use a dependency container to construct and share services.

```python
class ClientContainer:
    """Creates and provides client-side application dependencies."""
```

The container shall provide:

```text
ApiClient
WebSocketClient
ConnectionManager
AuthenticationService
MessagingService
ConversationService
FileTransferService
NotificationService
EncryptionManager
KeyManager
CacheDatabase
SettingsService
```

Dependencies shall be passed through constructors.

Views and ViewModels shall not create services directly.

---

# Chapter 202 — Main Window Layout

The main window shall use a three-column layout.

```text
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Title Bar                                                         │
├──────────────┬──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┤
│ Navigation   │ Conversation List    │ Active Conversation         │
│              │                      │                             │
│ Home         │ Search               │ Conversation Header         │
│ Messages     │                      │                             │
│ Contacts     │ Conversation 1       │ Message History             │
│ Groups       │ Conversation 2       │                             │
│ Files        │ Conversation 3       │                             │
│ Settings     │                      │ Attachment Preview          │
│              │                      │                             │
│ User Profile │                      │ Message Composer            │
└──────────────┴──────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘
```

The layout shall be resizable.

Minimum supported window size:

```text
1024 × 700
```

Recommended size:

```text
1280 × 800 or greater
```

---

# Chapter 203 — MainWindow

```python
class MainWindow(QMainWindow):
    """Primary application window displayed after authentication."""
```

Responsibilities:

* Display the main application layout.
* Switch between application pages.
* Display connection status.
* Handle window state.
* Show application-level notifications.
* Minimise to the system tray.
* Restore the last window dimensions.

The MainWindow shall not load messages directly.

It shall bind to `MainViewModel`.

---

# Chapter 204 — MainViewModel

```python
class MainViewModel(QObject):
    """Provides application-wide state to the main window."""
```

Properties:

```text
current_user
current_page
connection_state
unread_message_count
active_conversation
server_health
```

Signals:

```text
current_page_changed
connection_state_changed
unread_count_changed
active_conversation_changed
error_occurred
```

Methods:

```text
navigate_to()
select_conversation()
refresh_data()
logout()
handle_connection_change()
```

---

# Chapter 205 — Login Interface

The login window shall contain:

```text
BlueBubbles logo
Server address
Username field
Password field
Remember server checkbox
Login button
Connection test button
Status message
Privacy and monitoring notice
```

The password shall be hidden by default.

The user may temporarily reveal it using a visibility button.

---

# Chapter 206 — LoginViewModel

```python
class LoginViewModel(QObject):
    """Handles login form state and authentication requests."""
```

Properties:

```text
server_address
username
password
is_loading
error_message
```

Methods:

```text
validate_input()
test_server_connection()
login()
clear_password()
```

Login operations shall execute outside the GUI thread.

The login button shall be disabled while authentication is in progress.

---

# Chapter 207 — Login Validation

The client shall validate:

* Server address is present.
* Username is present.
* Password is present.
* Server address uses an allowed LAN address or configured hostname.
* Input lengths are within configured limits.

The client-side validation improves usability.

The server must still repeat all security validation.

---

# Chapter 208 — Navigation Sidebar

The navigation sidebar shall contain:

```text
Messages
Contacts
Groups
Files
Announcements
Settings
Administration
```

The Administration button shall only appear for authorised users.

The sidebar shall also display:

* Current user avatar.
* Display name.
* Presence indicator.
* Connection status.
* Unread badge.

---

# Chapter 209 — Conversation List

The conversation list shall display:

```text
Avatar or group icon
Conversation title
Latest message preview
Latest message time
Unread count
Mute status
Pinned status
Delivery status
```

Conversations shall be sorted by most recent activity by default.

Supported sorting options:

```text
Most Recent
Alphabetical
Unread First
Pinned First
```

---

# Chapter 210 — ConversationListViewModel

```python
class ConversationListViewModel(QObject):
    """Maintains the list of conversations visible to the user."""
```

Responsibilities:

* Load conversations.
* Sort conversations.
* Filter conversations.
* Update message previews.
* Track unread counts.
* Process real-time conversation events.

Methods:

```text
load_conversations()
refresh_conversation()
sort_conversations()
filter_conversations()
pin_conversation()
mute_conversation()
archive_conversation()
```

---

# Chapter 211 — Chat Page

The chat page shall contain four sections.

```text
Conversation Header
Message History
Typing Indicator
Message Composer
```

The interface shall remain familiar to users of Teams, WhatsApp and Discord.

---

# Chapter 212 — Conversation Header

The header shall display:

```text
Conversation name
Conversation avatar
Participant count
Presence state
Search button
File button
Conversation information button
```

For direct conversations, it shall display the other user’s presence.

For groups, it shall display the number of members.

---

# Chapter 213 — Message History

Messages shall appear chronologically.

The newest messages shall appear at the bottom.

Older messages shall load when the user scrolls upward.

Only the visible area and a small buffer shall be rendered.

This prevents excessive memory usage in large conversations.

---

# Chapter 214 — MessageWidget

```python
class MessageWidget(QFrame):
    """Displays one message within a conversation."""
```

The widget shall display:

```text
Sender name
Sender avatar
Decrypted message content
Timestamp
Edited indicator
Delivery state
Attachments
Reply preview
```

Supported message styles:

```text
Outgoing
Incoming
System
Deleted
Failed
Pending
```

---

# Chapter 215 — Message Context Menu

Right-clicking a message shall show actions appropriate to the user’s permissions.

Possible actions:

```text
Reply
Copy Text
Save Attachment
Edit
Delete
View Information
Report
```

Edit and delete shall only be available when permitted.

---

# Chapter 216 — Message Composer

The message composer shall contain:

```text
Multiline text box
Attach file button
Emoji button
Send button
Upload progress area
```

Keyboard behaviour:

```text
Enter

Send message

Shift + Enter

Create a new line

Escape

Cancel reply or edit

Ctrl + V

Paste text or supported image

Ctrl + Enter

Optional alternative send shortcut
```

---

# Chapter 217 — Message Composition Rules

The composer shall:

* Prevent empty messages.
* Trim unnecessary trailing whitespace.
* Display the message length.
* Disable sending while encryption is incomplete.
* Preserve unsent drafts.
* Warn the user if an attachment is still processing.
* support drag-and-drop file attachments.

The maximum text length shall be configurable.

Recommended Version 1.0 limit:

```text
8,000 Unicode characters
```

---

# Chapter 218 — ChatViewModel

```python
class ChatViewModel(QObject):
    """Controls the currently active conversation."""
```

Properties:

```text
conversation
messages
draft_text
reply_target
editing_message
typing_users
is_loading
has_more_history
```

Methods:

```text
load_conversation()
load_older_messages()
send_message()
edit_message()
delete_message()
reply_to_message()
attach_file()
cancel_reply()
send_typing_event()
mark_messages_read()
```

The ViewModel shall call `MessagingService`.

It shall not perform encryption directly.

---

# Chapter 219 — Incoming Message Process

```text
WebSocket event received

↓

Validate event DTO

↓

Store encrypted payload in cache

↓

Queue decryption worker

↓

Verify sender signature

↓

Decrypt message

↓

Create MessageModel

↓

Update ChatViewModel

↓

Display notification if required

↓

Update unread count
```

Invalid or unverifiable messages shall not be rendered as ordinary messages.

---

# Chapter 220 — Message Loading

The client shall use paginated loading.

Initial load:

```text
50 newest messages
```

Additional load:

```text
50 older messages per request
```

The user shall not need to press a button.

Older messages shall load automatically while scrolling.

---

# Chapter 221 — Local Conversation Cache

The local cache shall store:

* Recently accessed encrypted messages.
* Decrypted display data where secure storage allows it.
* Conversation summaries.
* Unread counts.
* Draft messages.
* Cached public keys.
* Attachment metadata.

Sensitive cache contents shall be encrypted at rest.

The server remains the source of truth.

---

# Chapter 222 — Contacts Page

The contacts page shall display:

```text
Search bar
Department filter
Presence filter
Favourite contacts
All contacts
Suggested contacts
```

Each contact entry shall show:

```text
Avatar
Display name
Department
Job title
Presence
Status message
Favourite marker
```

---

# Chapter 223 — Contact Sorting

Contacts may be sorted by:

```text
Most Frequently Contacted
Recently Contacted
Alphabetical
Department
Online First
Favourites First
```

The weighted contact score shall be calculated using:

```text
Message frequency
Recent interactions
Shared group participation
Favourite status
```

The exact formula shall remain simple and documented.

---

# Chapter 224 — Groups Page

The groups page shall allow users to:

* View groups.
* Search groups.
* Create groups.
* Open a group conversation.
* View members.
* Leave groups.
* Manage groups where authorised.

Group owners and moderators may:

* Rename the group.
* Change the group icon.
* Add members.
* Remove members.
* Promote moderators.
* Demote moderators.
* Generate invite codes.

---

# Chapter 225 — Group Creation Dialog

The group creation dialog shall contain:

```text
Group name
Description
Group icon
Searchable member list
Selected member list
Create button
Cancel button
```

Validation:

* Name cannot be empty.
* Name length must not exceed the configured limit.
* At least one other member must be selected.
* Duplicate groups are allowed unless a business rule forbids them.

---

# Chapter 226 — File Transfer Interface

The file transfer page shall show:

```text
Active uploads
Active downloads
Completed transfers
Failed transfers
Cancelled transfers
```

Each transfer shall display:

```text
Filename
Progress
Current speed
Estimated time remaining
Transfer status
Pause button
Resume button
Cancel button
Open file button
Open folder button
```

---

# Chapter 227 — Drag-and-Drop Uploads

Users shall be able to drag files into:

* The message composer.
* The active conversation.
* The upload panel.

Before upload, the client shall:

```text
Validate file access

↓

Read metadata

↓

Calculate checksum

↓

Generate encryption key

↓

Create encrypted chunks

↓

Display upload confirmation
```

Dragging a directory shall be rejected in Version 1.0 unless directory compression is explicitly implemented.

---

# Chapter 228 — Attachment Preview

The client shall preview safe supported file types.

Supported Version 1.0 previews:

```text
PNG
JPEG
GIF
BMP
WebP
Plain text
PDF metadata
```

Office documents shall display an icon and metadata rather than being executed or rendered inside the application.

The client shall never automatically execute downloaded files.

---

# Chapter 229 — Image Viewer

Images shall open in an internal viewer.

The viewer shall support:

```text
Zoom
Pan
Rotate
Save As
Copy Image
Open Externally
```

Image decoding shall occur in a worker thread.

---

# Chapter 230 — Notifications

Notifications shall be delivered through:

* In-application banners.
* Windows desktop notifications.
* Taskbar badges.
* System tray indicators.
* Optional notification sounds.

Notifications shall respect user settings and Do Not Disturb status.

---

# Chapter 231 — Notification Rules

A notification should normally appear when:

* A direct message is received.
* The user is mentioned.
* A file transfer completes.
* A group invitation is received.
* An administrator sends an announcement.
* A connection failure requires attention.

A notification should not normally appear when:

* The active conversation is already visible.
* The conversation is muted.
* Do Not Disturb is enabled.
* The event was generated by the current user.

---

# Chapter 232 — System Tray

BlueBubbles shall support minimising to the Windows system tray.

The tray menu shall contain:

```text
Open BlueBubbles
Set Presence
Pause Notifications
Settings
Log Out
Exit
```

Closing the main window may minimise the application rather than terminate it.

This behaviour shall be configurable.

---

# Chapter 233 — Settings Window

The settings interface shall contain the following pages.

```text
Account
Appearance
Notifications
Messages
Files
Storage
Network
Security
Accessibility
About
```

Administrative settings shall be presented separately.

---

# Chapter 234 — Account Settings

Users may change:

```text
Display name
Avatar
Status message
Presence
Local device name
```

Username, department and role may be read-only when controlled by Active Directory.

---

# Chapter 235 — Appearance Settings

Supported options:

```text
Light theme
Dark theme
Follow system theme
Font size
Interface scale
Compact message mode
Show avatars
Reduce animations
```

Changes shall preview immediately.

---

# Chapter 236 — Theme Engine

```python
class ThemeManager(QObject):
    """Loads and applies application themes."""
```

Themes shall be defined using Qt Style Sheets.

Theme files:

```text
light.qss
dark.qss
high_contrast.qss
```

The application shall not hard-code colours inside individual widgets.

---

# Chapter 237 — Notification Settings

Users may configure:

```text
Desktop notifications
Notification sounds
Message previews
Direct message notifications
Group notifications
Mention notifications
File transfer notifications
Quiet hours
```

Notification preview text may be disabled for privacy.

---

# Chapter 238 — File Settings

Users may configure:

```text
Download directory
Ask before downloading
Automatically download images
Maximum automatic download size
Bandwidth limit
Maximum concurrent transfers
Open download folder after completion
```

---

# Chapter 239 — Storage Settings

The client shall show:

```text
Cache size
Cached message count
Cached attachment count
Storage location
Clear cache button
```

Clearing the cache shall not delete server-side data.

The user shall be warned before removing locally downloaded files.

---

# Chapter 240 — Network Settings

Network settings shall include:

```text
Server hostname
Server port
Connection timeout
Proxy disabled
Certificate information
Connection test
Reconnect automatically
```

Internet proxy support is not required because the application is LAN-only.

---

# Chapter 241 — Security Settings

Security settings shall include:

```text
Key information
Key creation date
Key rotation
Trusted devices
Session list
Log out other sessions
Clear local session
Certificate fingerprint
```

Private keys shall never be displayed.

---

# Chapter 242 — Accessibility

The interface shall support:

* Keyboard-only navigation.
* Visible focus indicators.
* Screen reader labels.
* Adjustable font size.
* High-contrast theme.
* Reduced motion.
* Sufficient colour contrast.
* Descriptive error messages.

Colour alone shall never communicate critical information.

---

# Chapter 243 — Search Interface

The global search field shall search:

```text
Users
Contacts
Conversations
Groups
Decrypted locally available messages
Attachment names
```

Because the server stores message content in encrypted form, server-side plaintext message search is not available.

Message search shall operate on content the authorised client has decrypted and indexed locally.

---

# Chapter 244 — Local Search Index

The client may maintain an encrypted local search index.

The index shall store:

```text
Normalised message words
Message identifier
Conversation identifier
Timestamp
Sender identifier
```

The index shall be encrypted at rest.

Users shall be able to clear and rebuild it.

---

# Chapter 245 — Administration Interface

The administration interface shall only appear when the authenticated user has the required permission.

Pages may include:

```text
Overview
Users
Groups
Announcements
Connections
Audit Logs
Server Health
Storage
Configuration
```

The client shall not assume that interface visibility provides security.

Every administrative operation must still be authorised by the server.

---

# Chapter 246 — Administrative Overview

The overview shall display:

```text
Connected users
Total registered users
Messages sent today
Files transferred today
Server CPU usage
Server memory usage
Database status
Redis status
Storage usage
Recent warnings
```

Data shall refresh at a reasonable interval.

Recommended interval:

```text
5 seconds for live health data
30 seconds for aggregate statistics
```

---

# Chapter 247 — Error Display

Errors shall be presented in plain language.

Example:

```text
Unable to connect to the BlueBubbles server.

Check that you are connected to the organisation’s network and that the server address is correct.

Error code: NETWORK-001
```

Users shall not see:

* Python tracebacks.
* SQL errors.
* Raw HTTP responses.
* Cryptographic key material.
* Internal server paths.

Technical details may be written to secure logs.

---

# Chapter 248 — Loading States

Every operation that may take noticeable time shall provide feedback.

Examples:

```text
Loading spinner
Progress bar
Skeleton placeholder
Disabled button
Status message
Transfer progress
```

The interface shall never appear frozen while work is taking place.

---

# Chapter 249 — Background Workers

Expensive operations shall not run on the GUI thread.

Worker tasks include:

```text
Message encryption
Message decryption
File hashing
File encryption
File decryption
Thumbnail generation
File upload
File download
Search indexing
Cache maintenance
```

Workers shall communicate with ViewModels through Qt signals.

---

# Chapter 250 — Worker Base Class

```python
class Worker(QObject):
    """Base class for asynchronous client-side tasks."""
```

Signals:

```text
started
progress_changed
completed
failed
cancelled
```

Methods:

```text
run()
cancel()
is_cancelled()
```

Workers shall report exceptions safely.

---

# Chapter 251 — Connection Status

The user shall always be able to determine the current connection state.

Supported states:

```text
Connected
Connecting
Reconnecting
Offline
Authentication Required
Server Unavailable
```

The connection indicator shall appear in the main window.

---

# Chapter 252 — Offline Behaviour

When the server is unavailable, the client shall allow users to:

* View locally cached conversations.
* View previously downloaded files.
* Prepare draft messages.
* Queue outgoing messages.
* View connection diagnostics.

Queued actions shall be clearly marked as pending.

Actions requiring live server validation shall be disabled.

---

# Chapter 253 — Draft Messages

Draft text shall be saved locally per conversation.

Drafts shall:

* Survive page changes.
* Survive temporary disconnections.
* Survive normal application closure.
* Be removed after successful sending.
* Be encrypted in local storage.

---

# Chapter 254 — Keyboard Shortcuts

Recommended shortcuts:

| Shortcut         | Action                        |
| ---------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Ctrl + K         | Global search                 |
| Ctrl + N         | New conversation              |
| Ctrl + G         | Create group                  |
| Ctrl + F         | Search active conversation    |
| Ctrl + ,         | Open settings                 |
| Ctrl + Shift + A | Attach file                   |
| Ctrl + L         | Focus conversation list       |
| Ctrl + Q         | Exit application              |
| Escape           | Close dialog or cancel action |

Shortcuts shall be documented in the application.

---

# Chapter 255 — Window State Persistence

The client shall remember:

```text
Window position
Window size
Maximised state
Sidebar width
Conversation list width
Last open page
Last selected conversation
```

Sensitive data shall not be written into ordinary plaintext settings files.

---

# Chapter 256 — Client Logging

The client shall log:

```text
Startup
Shutdown
Connection changes
Authentication results
API failures
WebSocket reconnects
Transfer failures
Cache failures
Cryptographic verification failures
Unhandled exceptions
```

The client shall never log:

```text
Passwords
Private keys
Plaintext message contents
Plaintext file contents
Raw access tokens
Raw refresh tokens
```

---

# Chapter 257 — Graceful Shutdown

Shutdown sequence:

```text
Prevent new operations

↓

Save drafts

↓

Save settings

↓

Finish or pause file transfers

↓

Close WebSocket

↓

Invalidate presence heartbeat

↓

Close local database

↓

Clear sensitive memory where practical

↓

Close application
```

The application shall avoid corrupting local cache data during shutdown.

---

# Chapter 258 — Client Testing Requirements

The desktop client shall include tests for:

* ViewModel validation.
* Navigation state.
* Message rendering.
* Conversation sorting.
* Contact sorting.
* Draft persistence.
* Settings loading.
* Theme switching.
* Notification rules.
* Offline queue behaviour.
* File transfer progress.
* Connection state handling.
* Error message generation.

Critical services shall be testable without starting the full GUI.

---

# Chapter 259 — Initial Client Scope

Version 1.0 must include:

```text
Login
Logout
Direct messages
Group conversations
Contacts
Encrypted file transfer
Image preview
Conversation history
Presence
Typing indicators
Notifications
Settings
Local cache
Automatic reconnection
Basic administration interface
```

The following features are outside the initial scope:

```text
Voice calling
Video calling
Screen sharing
Remote desktop
Plugins
Bots
Mobile clients
Animated stickers
Advanced message formatting
```

These features shall not delay completion of the essential messaging system.

---

# Chapter 260 — Client Design Summary

The BlueBubbles client shall remain:

* Familiar.
* Responsive.
* Modular.
* Secure.
* Accessible.
* Easy to test.
* Easy to extend.

PySide6 Views shall display information.

ViewModels shall manage interface state.

Services shall coordinate application operations.

Networking classes shall communicate with the server.

Security classes shall protect data.

Storage classes shall manage encrypted local persistence.

No layer shall bypass another layer.

---

# End of Part 9

Part 10 will define the complete server-side architecture, including:

* FastAPI application startup.
* Dependency injection.
* Service and repository layers.
* PostgreSQL and Redis connections.
* WebSocket connection management.
* Message routing.
* Authentication flow.
* Permission enforcement.
* File storage.
* Audit logging.
* Background jobs.
* Health monitoring.
* Graceful shutdown.

# Part 10 — Server-Side Architecture and Application Services

---

# Chapter 261 — Server Purpose

The BlueBubbles server is the central coordination point for the entire messaging system.

It is responsible for:

* Authenticating users.
* Validating sessions.
* Enforcing permissions.
* Routing encrypted messages.
* Storing encrypted messages.
* Managing conversations and groups.
* Storing encrypted attachments.
* Maintaining user presence.
* Delivering real-time events.
* Recording audit events.
* Providing administrative information.
* Coordinating PostgreSQL and Redis.
* Recovering safely after restarts.

The server shall not decrypt user messages or attachments.

The server shall never store plaintext message content.

---

# Chapter 262 — Server Technology Stack

The server shall use:

```text
Python 3.13+
FastAPI
Uvicorn
SQLAlchemy 2.x
PostgreSQL
Redis
Pydantic 2.x
Alembic
httpx
websockets
python-ldap or ldap3
PyJWT or python-jose
argon2-cffi
cryptography
structlog
psutil
pytest
pytest-asyncio
```

The server operating system shall be Debian 13 or a later compatible Debian release.

---

# Chapter 263 — Server Architecture

The server shall follow a layered architecture.

```text
FastAPI Application

↓

Routers

↓

Middleware

↓

Application Services

↓

Repositories

↓

PostgreSQL / Redis / File Storage
```

Each layer shall have one clear responsibility.

---

# Chapter 264 — Server Directory Structure

The server package shall use the following structure.

```text
server/
│
├── main.py
├── application.py
├── bootstrap.py
├── dependencies.py
│
├── api/
│   ├── router.py

│
├── services/
│   ├── authentication_service.py

│   ├── conversation_service.py

│   ├── group_service.py
│   ├── attachment_service.py
│   ├── presence_service.py

│
├── database/

│
├── redis/

├── authentication/

│   ├── session_manager.py

│
├── websocket/
│   ├── connection.py
│   ├── connection_manager.py
│   ├── event_dispatcher.py
│   ├── event_handlers.py
│   └── heartbeat_manager.py
│
├── middleware/
│   ├── authentication.py
│   ├── correlation_id.py
│   ├── request_logging.py
│   ├── rate_limiting.py
│   ├── security_headers.py
│   └── exception_handling.py
│
├── schemas/
│   ├── auth.py
│   ├── users.py
│   ├── conversations.py
│   ├── messages.py

│
```

---

# Chapter 265 — FastAPI Application Object

The main FastAPI application shall be created by an application factory.

```python
def create_application() -> FastAPI:
    """Create and configure the BlueBubbles FastAPI application."""
```

The application factory shall:

* Load configuration.
* Configure logging.
* Register middleware.
* Register routers.
* Configure exception handlers.
* Configure startup events.
* Configure shutdown events.
* Configure OpenAPI metadata.
* Add security headers.

The FastAPI application shall not be constructed directly inside route modules.

---

# Chapter 266 — Application Lifecycle

Server startup shall follow this sequence.

```text
Load environment configuration

↓

Validate required settings

↓

Configure structured logging

↓

Create PostgreSQL engine

↓

Test database connection

↓

Run or verify migrations

↓

Create Redis connection

↓

Test Redis connection

↓

Initialise repositories

↓

Initialise services

↓

Initialise WebSocket manager

↓

Initialise background workers

↓

Register API routes

↓

Begin accepting requests
```

The server shall stop startup if PostgreSQL is unavailable.

Redis failure may place the server into degraded mode where practical.

---

# Chapter 267 — ServerApplication Class

```python
class ServerApplication:
    """Coordinates startup, operation and shutdown of the server."""
```

Properties:

```text
settings
database_manager
redis_manager
service_container
websocket_manager
worker_manager
health_monitor
```

Methods:

```text
start()
stop()
validate_dependencies()
start_workers()
stop_workers()
health_check()
```

The class shall coordinate infrastructure but shall not contain business logic.

---

# Chapter 268 — Dependency Injection Container

The server shall use constructor-based dependency injection.

```python
class ServerContainer:
    """Creates and supplies server-side dependencies."""
```

The container shall create:

```text
Database sessions
Redis client
Repositories
Authentication providers
Token manager
Application services
WebSocket manager
File storage service
Health monitoring services
```

Example:

```python
message_repository = MessageRepository(database_session_factory)

audit_service = AuditService(audit_repository)

messaging_service = MessagingService(
    message_repository=message_repository,
    conversation_repository=conversation_repository,
    permission_service=permission_service,
    audit_service=audit_service,
    event_publisher=event_publisher,
)
```

Services shall not construct repositories internally.

---

# Chapter 269 — Router Responsibilities

API routers shall remain thin.

A route handler may:

* Accept a validated DTO.
* Obtain the authenticated user.
* Call one application service.
* Convert the result into a response DTO.
* Return the correct HTTP status.

A route handler shall not:

* Execute SQL.
* Implement permission rules.
* Manage Redis directly.
* Manipulate files directly.
* Contain cryptographic logic.
* Construct audit entries manually.

---

# Chapter 270 — Example Route Flow

```python
@router.post("/messages", response_model=SendMessageResponse)
async def send_message(
    request: SendMessageRequest,
    current_user: AuthenticatedUser = Depends(get_current_user),
    service: MessagingService = Depends(get_messaging_service),
) -> SendMessageResponse:
    result = await service.send_message(
        sender_id=current_user.id,
        request=request,
    )

    return SendMessageResponse.from_result(result)
```

The route delegates all rules to `MessagingService`.

---

# Chapter 271 — Service Layer Responsibilities

Application services implement business use cases.

Examples:

```text
Authenticate a user
Send a message
Create a group
Remove a group member
Upload an attachment
Disable an account
Publish an announcement
Retrieve server statistics
```

Services may coordinate:

* Multiple repositories.
* Redis.
* File storage.
* Audit logging.
* WebSocket events.
* Permission checks.

Services shall expose task-focused methods rather than general-purpose database access.

---

# Chapter 272 — AuthenticationService

```python
class AuthenticationService:
    """Authenticates users and creates valid application sessions."""
```

Dependencies:

```text
AuthenticationProvider
UserRepository
SessionRepository
TokenManager
AuditService
```

Methods:

```text
authenticate()
refresh_session()
logout()
validate_session()
get_current_user()
```

Authentication process:

```text
Receive username and password

↓

Validate input

↓

Authenticate against LDAP or local provider

↓

Find or synchronise user record

↓

Verify account is enabled

↓

Create server-side session

↓

Generate access token

↓

Generate refresh token

↓

Store token hashes

↓

Create audit event

↓

Return session information
```

---

# Chapter 273 — Authentication Provider Interface

```python
class AuthenticationProvider(ABC):
    """Defines the interface for user authentication providers."""
```

Abstract methods:

```text
authenticate()
get_user_profile()
is_account_enabled()
get_group_memberships()
```

Implementations:

```text
LDAPAuthenticationProvider
LocalAuthenticationProvider
```

Local authentication shall exist only for development or deployments without Active Directory.

---

# Chapter 274 — LDAP Authentication

LDAP integration shall support:

* Active Directory bind authentication.
* User profile lookup.
* Enabled or disabled account checks.
* Group membership retrieval.
* Department and job title synchronisation.
* Stable Active Directory identifiers.

LDAP credentials used by the server shall come from environment variables or a protected secret file.

LDAP passwords shall never appear in source control.

---

# Chapter 275 — SessionService

```python
class SessionService:
    """Creates, validates, refreshes and invalidates user sessions."""
```

Methods:

```text
create_session()
validate_access_token()
refresh_access_token()
invalidate_session()
invalidate_all_user_sessions()
remove_expired_sessions()
```

Sessions shall be stored server-side.

Logging out shall immediately invalidate the server-side session.

A valid JWT associated with an invalidated session shall be rejected.

---

# Chapter 276 — TokenManager

```python
class TokenManager:
    """Creates and validates signed access and refresh tokens."""
```

Responsibilities:

* Generate access tokens.
* Generate refresh tokens.
* Decode tokens.
* Verify signatures.
* Verify expiration.
* Extract session identifiers.
* Reject malformed tokens.

Recommended lifetimes:

```text
Access token: 15 minutes
Refresh token: 7 days
```

These values shall be configurable.

---

# Chapter 277 — UserService

```python
class UserService:
    """Provides user profile and account management operations."""
```

Methods:

```text
get_user()
search_users()
update_profile()
synchronise_from_directory()
enable_user()
disable_user()
export_user_data()
request_user_deletion()
```

Only permitted roles may enable or disable accounts.

Disabling an account shall invalidate all active sessions.

---

# Chapter 278 — ContactService

```python
class ContactService:
    """Manages relationships and contact ordering between users."""
```

Methods:

```text
list_contacts()
add_contact()
remove_contact()
favourite_contact()
unfavourite_contact()
block_contact()
unblock_contact()
calculate_weight()
```

A blocked user shall not be able to begin a new direct conversation unless organisational policy overrides this behaviour.

---

# Chapter 279 — ConversationService

```python
class ConversationService:
    """Creates and manages direct and group conversations."""
```

Methods:

```text
list_conversations()
get_conversation()
create_direct_conversation()
create_group_conversation()
archive_conversation()
rename_conversation()
validate_membership()
```

Direct conversations shall be unique for each pair of users unless the design explicitly supports multiple direct threads.

---

# Chapter 280 — MessagingService

```python
class MessagingService:
    """Validates, stores and routes encrypted messages."""
```

Dependencies:

```text
MessageRepository
ConversationRepository
PermissionService
AuditService
EventPublisher
```

Methods:

```text
send_message()
get_messages()
edit_message()
delete_message()
mark_delivered()
mark_read()
add_reaction()
remove_reaction()
```

The service shall never decrypt the message payload.

---

# Chapter 281 — Message Send Workflow

```text
Receive encrypted message request

↓

Validate sender session

↓

Validate conversation exists

↓

Validate sender membership

↓

Validate request structure

↓

Validate recipient key entries

↓

Validate message size

↓

Validate client signature fields exist

↓

Create database transaction

↓

Insert encrypted message

↓

Insert recipient message keys

↓

Insert audit event

↓

Commit transaction

↓

Publish WebSocket event

↓

Return acknowledgement
```

If database insertion fails, no success event shall be published.

---

# Chapter 282 — Message Validation

The server shall validate:

* Conversation identifier.
* Sender membership.
* Ciphertext is present.
* Nonce has the expected encoded length.
* Authentication tag is present where required.
* Signature is present.
* Recipient key entries match valid recipients.
* Payload size is within limits.
* Reply target belongs to the same conversation.
* Protocol version is supported.

The server does not need to verify that ciphertext decrypts successfully.

---

# Chapter 283 — Message Editing

Editing shall create a new encrypted payload.

The existing row may be updated with:

```text
New ciphertext
New nonce
New authentication tag
New signature
Edited timestamp
Increased version
```

An edit history may be stored as separate encrypted versions if required.

Only the original sender may edit a message.

The edit time limit shall be configurable.

---

# Chapter 284 — Message Deletion

Message deletion shall use soft deletion.

The server shall preserve:

```text
Message identifier
Sender identifier
Conversation identifier
Original timestamp
Deletion timestamp
Encrypted content
Audit record
```

The normal client response shall indicate that the message was deleted.

Whether the encrypted content is eventually permanently removed shall be controlled by data retention policy.

---

# Chapter 285 — GroupService

```python
class GroupService:
    """Manages group conversations and membership permissions."""
```

Methods:

```text
create_group()
rename_group()
delete_group()
add_member()
remove_member()
promote_member()
demote_member()
leave_group()
transfer_ownership()
list_members()
```

Every membership change shall create an audit event.

---

# Chapter 286 — Group Permission Rules

Group roles:

```text
Owner
Moderator
Member
```

Owner permissions:

* Rename group.
* Delete group.
* Add members.
* Remove members.
* Promote moderators.
* Demote moderators.
* Transfer ownership.

Moderator permissions:

* Add members where policy allows.
* Remove ordinary members.
* Manage selected group settings.

Member permissions:

* Send messages.
* Receive messages.
* Leave the group.
* View members.

A user removed from a group shall immediately lose access to future messages and group events.

---

# Chapter 287 — PermissionService

```python
class PermissionService:
    """Determines whether an authenticated user may perform an action."""
```

Methods:

```text
has_permission()
require_permission()
can_access_conversation()
can_manage_group()
can_manage_user()
can_view_audit_event()
```

Permission checks shall be performed server-side for every protected action.

GUI visibility shall never be treated as permission enforcement.

---

# Chapter 288 — AttachmentService

```python
class AttachmentService:
    """Coordinates encrypted attachment upload, storage and download."""
```

Dependencies:

```text
AttachmentRepository
FileStorage
ConversationRepository
PermissionService
ChecksumService
AuditService
```

Methods:

```text
begin_upload()
upload_chunk()
complete_upload()
cancel_upload()
download_chunk()
delete_attachment()
get_attachment_metadata()
```

The server shall store only encrypted attachment bytes.

---

# Chapter 289 — Upload Session

An upload shall begin by creating an upload session.

Upload session properties:

```text
upload_id
user_id
conversation_id
filename
declared_size
encrypted_size
chunk_size
chunk_count
checksum
created_at
expires_at
received_chunks
```

Temporary upload state may be stored in Redis.

Permanent attachment metadata shall only be created after successful finalisation.

---

# Chapter 290 — Chunk Upload Workflow

```text
Client requests upload session

↓

Server validates permission

↓

Server creates temporary upload directory

↓

Client uploads encrypted chunks

↓

Server verifies chunk number and size

↓

Server records received chunk

↓

Client submits completion request

↓

Server verifies all chunks exist

↓

Server calculates encrypted file checksum

↓

Server compares expected checksum

↓

Server moves file into permanent storage

↓

Server creates attachment row

↓

Server writes audit event

↓

Server notifies recipients
```

Incomplete uploads shall expire and be removed automatically.

---

# Chapter 291 — FileStorage Interface

```python
class FileStorage(ABC):
    """Defines storage operations for encrypted attachment data."""
```

Methods:

```text
create_temporary_upload()
write_chunk()
read_chunk()
assemble_chunks()
move_to_permanent_storage()
delete_file()
file_exists()
get_file_size()
```

The initial implementation shall use local filesystem storage.

The interface shall permit future network-attached storage without changing business services.

---

# Chapter 292 — Attachment Paths

Clients shall never provide final filesystem paths.

The server shall generate all paths using UUID values.

Example:

```text
/data/bluebubbles/attachments/
2026/
07/
conversation_uuid/
message_uuid/
attachment_uuid.bin
```

Filenames supplied by users shall be stored as metadata only.

User filenames shall never be used directly as storage paths.

---

# Chapter 293 — Path Traversal Protection

The server shall reject:

* Absolute paths.
* Parent-directory references.
* Null bytes.
* Invalid Unicode path sequences.
* Unexpected separators.
* Filenames exceeding configured limits.

Storage identifiers shall be generated by the server.

---

# Chapter 294 — PresenceService

```python
class PresenceService:
    """Tracks live user availability using Redis."""
```

Methods:

```text
set_presence()
get_presence()
heartbeat()
mark_offline()
list_online_users()
```

Presence records shall expire automatically using Redis TTL values.

PostgreSQL may store only long-term information such as `last_seen`.

---

# Chapter 295 — Typing Events

Typing events shall:

* Be stored temporarily in Redis.
* Expire after approximately five seconds.
* Be visible only to conversation members.
* Never be written to PostgreSQL.
* Never create audit records unless organisational policy explicitly requires it.

Typing event flow:

```text
Client sends typing event

↓

Server verifies membership

↓

Redis stores short-lived state

↓

WebSocket event sent to other members
```

---

# Chapter 296 — NotificationService

```python
class NotificationService:
    """Creates and routes non-message user notifications."""
```

Notification examples:

```text
Group invitation
Account warning
File transfer completion
Announcement
Administrative notice
Server maintenance
```

Notification contents shall not reveal plaintext message data unless the recipient client generates the preview locally.

---

# Chapter 297 — AnnouncementService

```python
class AnnouncementService:
    """Creates and distributes organisation-wide announcements."""
```

Methods:

```text
create_announcement()
publish_announcement()
withdraw_announcement()
list_announcements()
acknowledge_announcement()
```

Announcements may target:

```text
All users
Specific departments
Specific roles
Specific groups
Selected users
```

Only authorised roles may create announcements.

---

# Chapter 298 — AuditService

```python
class AuditService:
    """Creates append-only records of security and business events."""
```

Methods:

```text
record_event()
record_authentication_event()
record_message_event()
record_group_event()
record_file_event()
record_admin_event()
verify_chain()
export_events()
```

Audit entries shall be created through this service only.

Application code shall not insert audit rows manually.

---

# Chapter 299 — Audit Event Structure

Each audit event shall contain:

```text
id
event_type
actor_user_id
target_type
target_id
session_id
source_ip
timestamp
severity
result
details
previous_hash
entry_hash
```

Audit details shall contain metadata only.

Plaintext message content shall never be included.

---

# Chapter 300 — Tamper-Evident Audit Chain

Each audit entry shall include the hash of the previous entry.

```text
Previous entry hash

+

Current entry canonical data

↓

SHA-256

↓

Current entry hash
```

Changing or removing an earlier entry shall break the chain.

This design makes the audit log tamper-evident.

Database administrator access may still technically alter data, so the system shall not inaccurately claim that alteration is impossible.

---

# Chapter 301 — Audit Immutability Controls

The database role used by the application shall receive:

```text
INSERT permission
SELECT permission
```

It shall not receive:

```text
UPDATE permission
DELETE permission
```

for the audit table.

Database triggers shall reject ordinary update and delete attempts.

Audit exports may also be signed or copied to protected backup storage.

---

# Chapter 302 — Audit Privacy

Helpdesk access shall normally expose only operational metadata.

Example:

```text
Message identifier
Sender
Recipient or conversation
Timestamp
Delivery result
Attachment status
```

Helpdesk users shall not receive message content.

HR or administrators shall only receive data permitted by organisational policy and law.

---

# Chapter 303 — Repository Pattern

Every persistent entity shall have a repository.

Repositories shall:

* Open database sessions.
* Perform queries.
* Add or update models.
* Handle pagination.
* Convert database rows into domain objects where required.
* Raise repository-specific exceptions.

Repositories shall not:

* Enforce business permissions.
* Publish WebSocket events.
* Read application configuration directly.
* Generate user-facing messages.

---

# Chapter 304 — BaseRepository

```python
class BaseRepository(Generic[ModelType]):
    """Provides common persistence operations."""
```

Possible methods:

```text
get_by_id()
list()
add()
update()
soft_delete()
exists()
```

Entity-specific repositories shall add focused query methods.

---

# Chapter 305 — UserRepository

Methods:

```text
get_by_id()
get_by_username()
get_by_email()
get_by_directory_guid()
search()
create()
update_directory_fields()
set_enabled()
```

Search queries shall be indexed and paginated.

---

# Chapter 306 — MessageRepository

Methods:

```text
create_message()
create_message_keys()
get_message()
list_conversation_messages()
update_encrypted_payload()
soft_delete_message()
mark_delivered()
mark_read()
```
