```text
High-priority banner or modal where policy requires
```

Priority shall always include visible text or accessible description.

---

# Chapter 2153 — Announcement Acknowledgement

Required acknowledgement control:

```text
I acknowledge this announcement
```

The user shall deliberately activate the control.

Opening the announcement alone shall not automatically count as acknowledgement.

After success:

```text
Acknowledged on 16 July 2026 at 17:42
```

---

# Chapter 2154 — Settings Page Structure

Recommended categories:

```text
Account
Appearance
Notifications
Messages
Files and transfers
Storage
Privacy and security
Sessions
Diagnostics
About
```

The category list may appear in the second column.

Settings shall use clear labels and descriptions.

---

# Chapter 2155 — Account Settings

Show:

```text
Display name
Username
Email where permitted
Department
Job title
Profile image
Status message
Authentication source
```

Directory-managed fields shall display:

```text
Managed by your organisation
```

and shall not be editable.

---

# Chapter 2156 — Appearance Settings

Options:

```text
Theme
Font size
Compact or comfortable message spacing
Reduced motion
High contrast
Show avatars
```

Themes:

```text
System
Light
Dark
High contrast
```

Changes should preview immediately where safe.

---

# Chapter 2157 — Font Scale

Suggested range:

```text
80% to 160%
```

Default:

```text
100%
```

The application shall test:

```text
100%
125%
150%
```

Controls and message bubbles shall grow without clipping important content.

---

# Chapter 2158 — Notification Settings

Options:

```text
Enable desktop notifications
Show sender name
Show message preview
Play sound
Notify for direct messages
Notify for group messages
Notify only when not focused
Urgent announcements
```

Conversation-specific mute state shall override general message notifications.

Server-mandated urgent announcements may override some preferences.

---

# Chapter 2159 — Privacy Settings

Possible options:

```text
Show notification message previews
Cache decrypted messages
Store search index
Clear clipboard after copying sensitive data where implemented
Lock after inactivity where implemented
```

The interface shall explain security and usability consequences.

---

# Chapter 2160 — Storage Settings

Show:

```text
Current cache use
Configured cache maximum
Attachment cache use
Thumbnail cache use
Search index size
Local database size
Download folder
```

Actions:

```text
Clear selected cache
Clear all local data
Open cache folder only where appropriate
Rebuild local database cache
```

---

# Chapter 2161 — Clear Cache Dialog

Selectable categories:

```text
Message display cache
Attachment cache
Thumbnail cache
Search index
Transfer history
Drafts
Offline messages
```

Dangerous categories such as drafts and offline messages shall show strong warnings.

The default selection shall exclude unsent work.

---

# Chapter 2162 — Clear All Local Data Dialog

The dialog shall explain:

```text
This will remove local messages, drafts, transfer state, cached files, session tokens and local encryption keys from this device.

Your server account and server-stored encrypted records will not be deleted.
```

Confirmation may require typing:

```text
CLEAR
```

or a second confirmation step.

---

# Chapter 2163 — Session Management Page

Each session row:

```text
Device name
Client version
Source IP where permitted
Created time
Last active
Current device marker
Status
```

Actions:

```text
Revoke session
Revoke all other sessions
```

The current session may be revoked through logout.

---

# Chapter 2164 — Revoke Session Confirmation

Example:

```text
Sign out Dell XPS 15?

BlueBubbles on that device will lose access and must sign in again.
```

For all sessions:

```text
Sign out all other devices?
```

The current device remains active unless explicitly included.

---

# Chapter 2165 — Diagnostics Page

Sections:

```text
Connection
Authentication
Local storage
Downloads
Encryption-key availability
Application version
Server version
Protocol version
```

Each check shall show:

```text
Pass
Warning
Fail
Unknown
```

with plain-language guidance.

---

# Chapter 2166 — Diagnostic Result Presentation

Example:

```text
Server connection

Passed

This device can reach the BlueBubbles server.
```

Example:

```text
Download folder

Failed

BlueBubbles cannot write to the selected folder.

Choose another folder in Files and transfers.
```

---

# Chapter 2167 — Diagnostic Package Control

Button:

```text
Create diagnostic package
```

Before creation, show included and excluded data.

After creation:

```text
Diagnostic package saved successfully.
```

Actions:

```text
Open folder
Copy path
Close
```

The package shall not upload automatically.

---

# Chapter 2168 — About Page

Show:

```text
BlueBubbles
Application version
Protocol version
Build identifier
Licence
Third-party notices
Copyright
Support information
```

Optional actions:

```text
Copy version information
Open licence
Check server compatibility
```

---

# Chapter 2169 — Administration Navigation

Administration shall appear only when the capability response indicates authorised features.

Possible pages:

```text
Dashboard
Users
Sessions
Connections
Audit
Security alerts
Announcements
System health
Workers
Configuration
Exports
```

Server permission enforcement remains mandatory.

---

# Chapter 2170 — Administration Layout

Administrative pages may use:

```text
Secondary navigation list
Page toolbar
Filter controls
Data table
Detail panel
Action buttons
```

Dense tables are acceptable but shall remain readable.

Administrative views shall support:

* Sorting.
* Filtering.
* Pagination.
* Keyboard navigation.
* Clear confirmation dialogs.

---

# Chapter 2171 — Administration Dashboard

Cards:

```text
Server status
Connected users
Active sessions
Messages today
Attachments today
Storage use
Database status
Redis status
Open alerts
Failed logins
```

Charts are optional.

Every chart shall have a textual or tabular alternative.

---

# Chapter 2172 — Dashboard Health Card

A health card shall show:

```text
Component
State
Short explanation
Last checked
Response time where useful
```

Example:

```text
Redis

Degraded

Presence and typing indicators may be unavailable.
```

---

# Chapter 2173 — User Administration Page

Main table columns:

```text
Display name
Username
Department
Role
Enabled state
Online state
Active sessions
Last login
```

Filters:

```text
Role
Department
Enabled
Online
Authentication source
```

Actions:

```text
View
Enable
Disable
Change role
Revoke sessions
View audit history
```

---

# Chapter 2174 — User Administration Detail Panel

Show:

```text
Profile fields
Account state
Directory state
Authentication source
Role
Last directory sync
Last login
Active sessions
Recent relevant audit events
```

Do not show:

* Password hash.
* Tokens.
* Private keys.
* Message content.

---

# Chapter 2175 — Disable User Dialog

Required fields:

```text
Reason
Confirmation
```

Explanation:

```text
The user will be prevented from signing in.

Active sessions will be revoked and connected devices will be disconnected.

Existing messages and files will remain according to retention policy.
```

The confirm button shall use a destructive style.

---

# Chapter 2176 — Role Change Dialog

Show:

```text
Current role
New role
Permission summary
Reason
```

Warnings:

* Privilege increase.
* Privilege decrease.
* Session invalidation where applicable.
* Last SuperAdministrator protection.

The server shall reject forbidden changes even if the interface offers them incorrectly.

---

# Chapter 2177 — Active Sessions Page

Columns:

```text
User
Device
Client version
Source IP
Created
Last active
Expiry
State
```

Actions:

```text
Revoke
View user
View related audit events
```

Raw token values shall never appear.

---

# Chapter 2178 — Active Connections Page

Columns:

```text
User
Device
Connection time
Last heartbeat
Client version
Source IP
Connection state
```

Actions:

```text
Disconnect socket
Revoke session
View user
```

Disconnecting the socket shall be distinguished from invalidating the session.

---

# Chapter 2179 — Audit Page

Components:

```text
Filter toolbar
Audit table
Details panel
Export action
Integrity status
```

Filters:

```text
Date range
Category
Action
Severity
Result
Actor
Target
Correlation ID
```

---

# Chapter 2180 — Audit Table Columns

Suggested columns:

```text
Sequence
Timestamp
Severity
Category
Action
Actor
Target
Result
Correlation reference
```

The details panel may show role-filtered structured metadata.

Long values shall be copyable.

---

# Chapter 2181 — Audit Integrity Status

Display:

```text
Recent audit verification:

Valid

Last checked:

16 July 2026, 17:30
```

Failure:

```text
Audit integrity check failed.

Administrative investigation is required.
```

This shall use a critical banner and shall not be dismissible without acknowledgement.

---

# Chapter 2182 — Audit Export Dialog

Options:

```text
Applied filters
Date range
Format
Included visibility level
```

Formats:

```text
CSV
JSON
```

The dialog shall explain that the export may contain sensitive administrative metadata.

---

# Chapter 2183 — Security Alerts Page

Alert list shall group by:

```text
Open
Acknowledged
Resolved
```

Each alert shows:

```text
Severity
Title
Summary
Source component
First occurrence
Latest occurrence
Occurrence count
Status
```

---

# Chapter 2184 — Security Alert Actions

Actions:

```text
Acknowledge
Resolve
Dismiss where permitted
View related audit event
View related user or session
```

Resolution shall require notes for high or critical alerts.

---

# Chapter 2185 — Worker Administration Page

Columns:

```text
Worker
State
Last run
Last success
Next run
Failure count
Last error
```

Actions:

```text
Run now
Pause where permitted
Resume
View execution history
```

Critical workers shall display stronger warnings before pausing.

---

# Chapter 2186 — Configuration Administration Page

Settings shall be grouped by category.

Each editable setting shall show:

```text
Name
Description
Current value
Allowed range
Restart requirement
Security implications
```

Secrets shall not be displayed or edited here unless a separately designed secure workflow exists.

---

# Chapter 2187 — Configuration Save Review

Before applying changes, show a summary:

```text
Message limit:

8000 → 10000

Maximum attachment size:

2 GiB → 1 GiB

Restart required:

No
```

Require:

```text
Change reason
```

The save operation shall be atomic.

---

# Chapter 2188 — Announcement Administration Page

Fields:

```text
Title
Body
Priority
Target
Acknowledgement required
Publish time where implemented
Expiry
```

Actions:

```text
Preview
Save draft
Publish
Withdraw
```

The preview shall use the same safe renderer as users receive.

---

# Chapter 2189 — Administrative Data Tables

Tables shall support:

* Keyboard row navigation.
* Sort indicators.
* Column resizing.
* Copy selected cells.
* Pagination.
* Accessible column names.
* Empty states.
* Loading states.
* Error states.

Tables shall not freeze while loading large result sets.

---

# Chapter 2190 — Loading Indicators

Use different loading indicators based on scope.

```text
Button spinner:

One action

Inline skeleton or spinner:

One panel

Page spinner:

Whole page initial load

Progress bar:

Measured long operation
```

The application shall not display a modal spinner for ordinary background refreshes.

---

# Chapter 2191 — Empty States

Every list or table shall define an empty state.

Examples:

```text
No messages yet
No contacts match your search
No active transfers
No open security alerts
No audit events match these filters
```

Where appropriate, include one useful action.

---

# Chapter 2192 — Error States

Page-level error state shall show:

```text
What failed
Whether cached data is available
Suggested action
Error reference
Retry action
Diagnostics action where relevant
```

Example:

```text
Conversations could not be refreshed.

Your cached conversation list is still available.
```

---

# Chapter 2193 — Confirmation Dialog Rules

Confirmation dialogs are required for:

```text
Delete message
Remove group member
Leave group
Transfer ownership
Disable user
Change privileged role
Revoke all sessions
Clear drafts
Clear offline queue
Clear all local data
Cancel permanent transfer
Delete group
Withdraw announcement
```

The primary action text shall describe the action.

Avoid generic:

```text
OK
```

for destructive confirmation.

---

# Chapter 2194 — Destructive Button Styling

Destructive actions shall use:

* Warning or destructive colour.
* Explicit verb.
* Accessible destructive description.
* Separation from safe actions.

Examples:

```text
Delete message
Disable user
Clear all local data
```

A red colour alone shall not be the only indication.

---

# Chapter 2195 — Toast Notifications

Toasts may be used for:

```text
Settings saved
Message copied
Download completed
Connection restored
Draft recovered
```

They shall not be used as the only presentation for:

* Permanent message failure.
* Session expiry.
* Destructive-action failure.
* Security warning.
* Data-loss risk.

Toasts shall be keyboard and screen-reader accessible where practical.

---

# Chapter 2196 — Toast Duration

Suggested durations:

```text
Success:

3 to 5 seconds

Informational:

5 seconds

Warning:

8 seconds or until dismissed

Critical:

Use banner or dialog instead
```

The user shall be able to dismiss non-critical toasts.

---

# Chapter 2197 — Dialog Keyboard Behaviour

Standard behaviour:

```text
Escape:

Cancel or close when safe

Enter:

Activate default non-destructive action

Tab:

Move focus

Shift+Tab:

Move focus backward
```

A destructive action shall not automatically become the default Enter action unless explicitly justified.

---

# Chapter 2198 — Focus Management

After navigation:

* Move focus to the page heading or first meaningful control where appropriate.
* Preserve focus during non-destructive refresh.
* Restore focus after closing a dialog.
* Move focus to the first validation error after failed submission.
* Avoid trapping focus outside modal dialogs.

---

# Chapter 2199 — Keyboard-Only Messaging

A keyboard-only user shall be able to:

```text
Open chats
Select conversation
Read message list
Move through messages
Focus composer
Write message
Insert line break
Send
Open context menu
Reply
Copy
Attach a file
Cancel action
```

Mouse use shall not be required for core workflows.

---

# Chapter 2200 — Global Keyboard Shortcuts

Recommended:

```text
Ctrl+N:

New conversation

Ctrl+K:

Focus conversation search

Ctrl+Shift+F:

Open message search

Ctrl+Comma:

Open settings

Ctrl+L:

Focus conversation list

Ctrl+M:

Focus message composer

Escape:

Cancel current reply, edit or dialog where safe
```

Shortcut conflicts with standard text-editing behaviour shall be avoided.

---

# Chapter 2201 — Message Navigation Shortcuts

Optional accessible shortcuts:

```text
Alt+Up:

Previous message

Alt+Down:

Next message

Alt+R:

Reply to focused message

Ctrl+C:

Copy focused message when no text selection exists
```

These shall not interfere with screen-reader navigation.

---

# Chapter 2202 — Accessibility Names

Every interactive control shall have an accessible name.

Examples:

```text
Attach file
Send message
Open conversation information
Mute conversation
Retry failed transfer
Remove selected attachment
```

Icon-only controls shall always provide:

* Accessible name.
* Tooltip.
* Focus state.

---

# Chapter 2203 — Screen-Reader Message Labels

A message accessible description should include:

```text
Sender
Timestamp
Message text or content type
Edited state
Delivery state where relevant
```

Example:

```text
Alex, 17:42. Project files have been uploaded. Edited.
```

For the current user:

```text
You, 17:43. Thanks. Delivered.
```

---

# Chapter 2204 — Colour Contrast

Text and controls shall meet reasonable accessibility contrast targets.

Test at minimum:

```text
Light theme
Dark theme
High-contrast theme
Disabled control state
Selected row state
Error state
Warning state
Success state
```

Important text shall not use faint low-contrast colours.

---

# Chapter 2205 — Colour Independence

The following states shall use text or icons in addition to colour:

```text
Online
Offline
Unread
Selected
Warning
Error
Success
Transfer state
Audit severity
Health state
```

Example:

```text
Red circle alone:

Not sufficient

Red warning icon plus “Failed”:

Acceptable
```

---

# Chapter 2206 — High-Contrast Theme

The high-contrast theme shall:

* Use strong foreground and background separation.
* Use visible borders.
* Avoid transparency-dependent controls.
* Use underlines or shapes where colour differences are insufficient.
* Preserve visible focus rings.
* Avoid low-contrast disabled states.

---

# Chapter 2207 — Reduced Motion

When reduced motion is enabled:

* Disable decorative transitions.
* Avoid animated scrolling where unnecessary.
* Replace pulsing indicators with static progress indicators.
* Keep essential progress animation minimal.
* Avoid rapid flashing.

No interface element shall flash at unsafe rates.

---

# Chapter 2208 — Theme Architecture

Themes shall be defined through:

```text
QSS files
Theme tokens
Icon variants
Shared spacing constants
```

Avoid large duplicated inline styles in views.

Suggested tokens:

```text
background_primary
background_secondary
surface
text_primary
text_secondary
accent
border
success
warning
error
focus
```

---

# Chapter 2209 — Light Theme

Suggested characteristics:

```text
Light neutral background
White or slightly tinted surfaces
Dark primary text
Muted secondary text
Blue accent
Clear borders
```

Message bubbles shall remain distinct from the page background.

---

# Chapter 2210 — Dark Theme

Suggested characteristics:

```text
Dark neutral background
Slightly lighter surfaces
Near-white primary text
Muted grey secondary text
Blue or cyan accent
Visible borders
```

Pure black backgrounds may be avoided unless high contrast requires them.

---

# Chapter 2211 — Theme Switching

Theme changes shall:

* Apply without application restart where practical.
* Update all open dialogs.
* Update icons.
* Preserve focus.
* Avoid losing drafts.
* Save user preference.

Custom widgets shall respond to theme changes.

---

# Chapter 2212 — Spacing System

Use a consistent spacing scale.

Example:

```text
4 pixels
8 pixels
12 pixels
16 pixels
24 pixels
32 pixels
```

Layouts shall use the scale rather than arbitrary unrelated margins.

---

# Chapter 2213 — Corner Radius

Suggested use:

```text
Small controls:

4 to 6 pixels

Cards and message bubbles:

8 to 12 pixels

Large panels:

Minimal or no excessive rounding
```

The application shall avoid overly decorative rounded containers that reduce usable space.

---

# Chapter 2214 — Icon System

Icons shall be:

* Consistent in style.
* Vector-based where practical.
* Available in required theme variants.
* Legible at standard scaling.
* Accompanied by accessible names.
* Licensed appropriately.

The project shall not mix many unrelated icon families.

---

# Chapter 2215 — Avatar Behaviour

Avatars may show:

```text
User image
Initials fallback
Group icon
System icon
```

Fallback initials shall use the display name.

Avatar images shall be:

* Cropped safely.
* Loaded asynchronously.
* Cached.
* Given accessible labels.
* Replaced by fallback on failure.

---

# Chapter 2216 — Presence Display

Presence states:

```text
Online
Away
Do not disturb
Offline
Unknown
```

Display with:

```text
Text
Tooltip
Small indicator icon
```

Stale presence shall show:

```text
Last seen at…
```

or:

```text
Unknown
```

rather than falsely showing online.

---

# Chapter 2217 — Presence Privacy

The server or organisation policy may restrict last-seen information.

The client shall display only fields included in the authorised profile response.

It shall not calculate or infer hidden presence history from unrelated metadata.

---

# Chapter 2218 — Responsive Layout Behaviour

At reduced width:

```text
Navigation sidebar remains visible.

Conversation panel may narrow.

Content panel retains minimum readable width.
```

At the minimum supported width, the application may collapse the conversation panel after selecting a chat.

A back action shall return to the list.

---

# Chapter 2219 — Collapsed Conversation Layout

Optional compact behaviour:

```text
Chats list view

↓

Select conversation

↓

Chat occupies second and third columns

↓

Back button returns to list
```

This is useful below approximately:

```text
1050 pixels
```

The coding AI may implement a simpler fixed three-column layout first if minimum-size usability remains acceptable.

---

# Chapter 2220 — DPI Scaling

The interface shall support Windows scaling levels such as:

```text
100%
125%
150%
175%
200%
```

Requirements:

* Use layouts rather than fixed absolute positioning.
* Use device-independent Qt sizing.
* Use scalable icons.
* Avoid fixed text heights.
* Test multi-monitor scaling changes where possible.

---

# Chapter 2221 — Multi-Monitor Behaviour

The window shall:

* Reopen on a valid display.
* Avoid restoring beyond visible work areas.
* Handle different display scaling.
* Keep dialogs associated with the main window.
* Avoid opening confirmation dialogs on a disconnected monitor.

---

# Chapter 2222 — System Tray Behaviour

When enabled:

```text
Closing main window:

Hide to tray

Exit command:

Fully shut down
```

Tray menu:

```text
Open BlueBubbles
Connection state
Unread count
Pause notifications
Settings
Log out
Exit
```

The close-to-tray behaviour shall be configurable.

---

# Chapter 2223 — Desktop Notifications

Notification content may include:

```text
Application name
Sender
Conversation
Message preview where permitted
Attachment summary
```

Example with preview:

```text
Alex — Project Team

The updated document is ready.
```

Privacy mode:

```text
BlueBubbles

New message received.
```

---

# Chapter 2224 — Notification Activation

Selecting a notification shall:

```text
Open or focus BlueBubbles

↓

Navigate to conversation

↓

Load relevant message

↓

Mark read only according to normal read policy
```

The application shall handle the notification being selected after a client restart where possible.

---

# Chapter 2225 — Notification Suppression

Do not show a desktop message notification when:

* The active conversation is visible and focused, if configured.
* The conversation is muted.
* Notifications are paused.
* The application is logging out.
* The message was sent by the current user.
* The message is a duplicate event.
* The user lacks authorised decrypted content.

Urgent announcements may use separate policy.

---

# Chapter 2226 — Clipboard Behaviour

Copying message text shall place plaintext on the operating-system clipboard.

The user shall be informed through documentation that other local applications may access clipboard content.

Optional future behaviour:

```text
Clear copied message after a configured delay
```

Version 1.0 may omit automatic clipboard clearing.

---

# Chapter 2227 — Drag and Drop

Supported drag-and-drop target:

```text
Chat composer attachment area
```

The client shall validate all dropped paths.

Directories shall be:

* Rejected in Version 1.0.
* Or expanded only if a future explicit multi-file design is implemented.

Dropping a file shall not send it automatically.

---

# Chapter 2228 — File Picker Filters

The file picker may display:

```text
All allowed files
Images
Documents
```

Blocked extensions shall be excluded where practical but still validated after selection.

The client shall not rely solely on file-picker filters.

---

# Chapter 2229 — Contextual Tooltips

Tooltips shall explain unfamiliar icons.

Examples:

```text
Mute conversation
Pin conversation
Retry transfer
Message could not be verified
Stored on server
```

Tooltips shall be concise and shall not contain secrets.

---

# Chapter 2230 — Help and Guidance

Version 1.0 shall include a basic help section or contextual guidance for:

```text
Sending messages
Creating groups
Sending files
Offline behaviour
Search limitations
Session management
Diagnostics
Privacy and encryption
```

Help content shall use plain language.

---

# Chapter 2231 — Encryption Information Dialog

A user-facing explanation may state:

```text
Messages and files are encrypted on your device before being sent.

The BlueBubbles server stores encrypted content and delivery metadata.

Administrators do not automatically receive your private decryption keys.
```

The interface shall avoid unsupported claims such as absolute security guarantees.

---

# Chapter 2232 — Key Fingerprint Dialog

The dialog may show:

```text
User
Key type
Key version
Fingerprint
Creation date
Revocation state
```

Actions:

```text
Copy fingerprint
Close
```

Version 1.0 does not require a complex manual trust-verification ceremony unless implemented fully.

---

# Chapter 2233 — Session Expiry Flow

When the session expires and cannot refresh:

```text
Display blocking session-expired banner or dialog

↓

Save drafts

↓

Pause outgoing actions

↓

Close WebSocket

↓

Return to login
```

Message:

```text
Your session has expired.

Sign in again to continue. Your saved drafts have been preserved.
```

---

# Chapter 2234 — Account Disabled Flow

When an account-disabled event is received:

```text
Stop protected operations

↓

Save local recoverable state

↓

Clear session tokens

↓

Lock private-key store

↓

Return to login
```

Message:

```text
This account has been disabled.

Contact the helpdesk if you believe this is a mistake.
```

---

# Chapter 2235 — User Removed from Group Flow

If the active user is removed:

* Stop allowing new messages.
* Update group membership.
* Show a system notice.
* Remove future-message access.
* Preserve previously authorised local content according to policy.
* Disable composer.

Example:

```text
You are no longer a member of this group.
```

---

# Chapter 2236 — Conversation Deleted Flow

The page shall show:

```text
This conversation is no longer available.
```

The composer shall be disabled.

The client shall remove it from the active conversation list after synchronisation, unless archived history remains permitted.

---

# Chapter 2237 — Server Policy Update Flow

When policy changes:

```text
Recalculate effective settings

↓

Update controls and limits

↓

Show notice when user behaviour changes
```

Example:

```text
File uploads are now limited to 500 MiB by your organisation.
```

Existing valid transfers shall follow server policy.

---

# Chapter 2238 — Application Update Required

Blocking page or dialog:

```text
BlueBubbles must be updated before it can connect to this server.

Installed version:

1.0.0

Required version:

1.2.0 or later
```

Actions:

```text
Open approved update location
Copy version information
Exit
```

The client shall not continue with an unsupported protocol.

---

# Chapter 2239 — Application Update Available

Non-blocking notice:

```text
A newer BlueBubbles version is available.
```

Actions:

```text
View update information
Dismiss
```

The user may continue if the installed version remains supported.

---

# Chapter 2240 — Progress and Cancellation Accessibility

Progress indicators shall expose:

```text
Operation name
Current percentage where known
Current state
Cancel availability
```

Screen readers shall receive meaningful updates without being flooded for every percentage point.

Suggested announcement intervals:

```text
Start
25%
50%
75%
Complete
Failure
```

---

# Chapter 2241 — Table Pagination Controls

Administrative pagination controls:

```text
Previous
Next
Page size
Current range
Total where known
```

Cursor-based APIs may display:

```text
1–50
```

without claiming an exact total.

Buttons shall disable correctly at boundaries.

---

# Chapter 2242 — Sorting Indicators

Sortable table headers shall show:

```text
Ascending
Descending
Not sorted
```

Accessible header text shall announce sort state.

Sort changes shall preserve filters.

---

# Chapter 2243 — Filter Chips

Applied filters may appear as removable chips.

Example:

```text
Severity: High
Result: Failure
Last 7 days
```

Action:

```text
Clear all filters
```

This helps users understand why results are limited.

---

# Chapter 2244 — Inline Validation Styling

Invalid fields shall show:

* Visible border or icon.
* Error text.
* Accessible invalid state.
* Focus on first error after submission.

Validation shall not clear valid fields.

---

# Chapter 2245 — Form Submission Safety

While submitting:

* Disable duplicate submit.
* Preserve values.
* Display progress.
* Permit safe cancellation where applicable.
* Restore controls after failure.
* Show whether the operation completed.

A timeout shall not automatically imply failure if idempotency status is uncertain.

---

# Chapter 2246 — Optimistic UI Rules

Optimistic updates may be used for:

```text
Pinning
Muting
Local preferences
Draft state
```

Use caution for:

```text
Message stored state
Role changes
User disabling
Group removal
Attachment completion
```

Critical or permanent states shall wait for server acknowledgement.

---

# Chapter 2247 — Message Send Optimism

The client may insert the outgoing message immediately as:

```text
Pending
```

It shall not show:

```text
Stored
Delivered
Read
```

until corresponding server acknowledgements exist.

---

# Chapter 2248 — Administrative Action Completion

After a successful administrative action:

```text
Update the row
Show success notice
Record returned audit reference where useful
Refresh affected details
```

After failure:

```text
Leave authoritative state unchanged
Show reason
Allow retry where safe
```

---

# Chapter 2249 — Error Reference Copying

Error dialogs may include:

```text
Error reference:
a73d4d13-e978-4bd6-b443-aee18c26d171
```

Action:

```text
Copy reference
```

The reference shall be selectable without exposing sensitive technical context.

---

# Chapter 2250 — Interface Logging Restrictions

Views and widgets shall not log:

```text
Message text
Draft text
Search terms
Password input
Attachment plaintext
Clipboard content
Private-key fingerprints unnecessarily
```

Views may log:

```text
Page opened
Dialog action selected
ViewModel error code
Widget construction failure
```

where useful and non-sensitive.

---

# Chapter 2251 — UI Performance Requirements

The interface shall remain responsive during:

* Synchronisation.
* Encryption.
* File transfer.
* Search.
* Cache cleanup.
* Administrative refresh.
* Message pagination.

Blocking operations shall not run on the GUI thread.

---

# Chapter 2252 — Conversation Rendering Performance

The message view shall:

* Render bounded pages.
* Avoid creating widgets for tens of thousands of messages simultaneously.
* Remove or virtualise distant items where practical.
* Load thumbnails lazily.
* Avoid recalculating complete history on every event.

Version 1.0 may use paged widget creation with careful limits if full virtualisation is too complex.

---

# Chapter 2253 — Resize Performance

Window resizing shall not trigger:

* Database queries.
* Network requests.
* Message decryption.
* Search re-indexing.
* Full thumbnail regeneration.

Layout operations shall remain presentation-only.

---

# Chapter 2254 — UI Error Recovery

If one page fails to load:

* Keep the application open.
* Show page-level error.
* Preserve other sections.
* Allow retry.
* Avoid clearing valid cached data.

A failure in the administration dashboard shall not stop ordinary messaging.

---

# Chapter 2255 — UI State Restoration

After temporary navigation away:

```text
Scroll position
Draft
Selected message where useful
Search query
Applied filters
Transfer selection
```

may be restored.

Sensitive pages shall not remain visible after logout.

---

# Chapter 2256 — Logout Interface Cleanup

On logout:

* Close user-specific dialogs.
* Clear message widgets.
* Dispose ViewModels.
* Clear decrypted previews.
* Clear search results.
* Hide administrative pages.
* Return to login.
* Clear notification content.
* Preserve only policy-approved encrypted local state.

---

# Chapter 2257 — Screenshot Privacy Mode

Optional development or demonstration feature:

```text
Privacy mode
```

It may replace message text and names with placeholders for safe screenshots.

If implemented, it shall clearly show that displayed data is obscured.

Version 1.0 may omit this feature.

---

# Chapter 2258 — Demonstration Mode Interface

Demonstration mode shall display a visible label:

```text
Demonstration environment
```

It shall not be easily confused with production.

Sample data may be reset.

Demonstration credentials shall not be shown in production builds.

---

# Chapter 2259 — User Interface Test Identifiers

Important widgets may receive stable object names.

Examples:

```text
login_username_input
login_password_input
login_submit_button
conversation_list
message_composer_input
message_send_button
transfer_progress_bar
admin_user_table
```

These support automated Qt tests.

Object names shall not encode sensitive data.

---

# Chapter 2260 — Required GUI Unit Tests

Tests shall cover:

```text
Login field validation
Login busy state
Navigation selection
Conversation filtering
Message pending state
Message failure state
Draft restoration
Reply state
Edit state
Attachment selection
Transfer progress
Search result navigation
Settings save
Cache confirmation
Session revocation confirmation
Administrative permission visibility
```

---

# Chapter 2261 — Required Widget Tests

Tests shall verify:

```text
Buttons enable and disable correctly
Keyboard shortcuts work
Focus order is valid
Dialogs return expected actions
Message text wraps
Long usernames elide
Large font scaling does not clip controls
Theme switching updates widgets
Error banners display
Empty states display
```

---

# Chapter 2262 — Accessibility Tests

Manual and automated checks:

```text
Keyboard-only login
Keyboard-only message send
Screen-reader control names
Visible focus indicator
Error focus movement
High-contrast theme
150% font scale
Colour-independent states
Dialog focus trapping
Notification accessible text
```

---

# Chapter 2263 — Visual Regression Candidates

Suitable snapshot candidates:

```text
Login window
Empty chat page
Direct conversation
Group conversation
Failed message
Attachment card
Settings page
Administration dashboard
Audit table
Security alert dialog
Dark theme
High-contrast theme
```

Snapshots shall be reviewed rather than accepted automatically.

---

# Chapter 2264 — Manual Usability Tests

Users shall be asked to complete:

```text
Log in
Start a direct conversation
Create a group
Send a message
Reply to a message
Send a file
Find an old cached message
Mute a conversation
Revoke another session
Run diagnostics
```

Record:

```text
Completion success
Time
Errors
Confusion
User comments
```

---

# Chapter 2265 — Interface Acceptance Criteria

The UI shall be accepted when:

* Core workflows can be completed without assistance.
* All required controls have labels.
* Keyboard navigation works.
* Text remains readable at larger scale.
* Background work does not freeze the window.
* Errors explain what happened.
* Destructive actions require confirmation.
* Connection state is always understandable.
* Administration does not reveal plaintext.
* No required feature remains represented by a non-functional button.

---

# Chapter 2266 — Simplified Version 1.0 Interface Scope

Version 1.0 shall implement:

```text
Login window
Three-column main window
Navigation sidebar
Conversation list
Direct chat page
Group chat page
Message bubbles
Reply
Edit
Delete
Drafts
Attachment cards
Transfer page
Contacts page
Groups page
Search page
Announcements page
Settings
Session management
Diagnostics
Administration dashboard
User administration
Audit page
Security alerts
Light theme
Dark theme
High-contrast theme
Keyboard navigation
Accessible labels
Desktop notifications
System tray
```

The following may be deferred:

```text
Animated transitions
Customisable colour themes
Sticker picker
GIF search
Voice messages
Voice calling
Video calling
Message reactions if not already implemented
Complex markdown rendering
Multiple detachable chat windows
Full message-list virtualisation
Touch-optimised tablet layout
```

---

# Chapter 2267 — Interface Implementation Requirements

The coding AI shall:

* Use Qt layouts rather than fixed positioning.
* Keep views separate from ViewModels.
* Avoid blocking the GUI thread.
* Provide loading, empty and error states for each major page.
* Implement functional keyboard shortcuts.
* Include accessible names for icon-only controls.
* Support theme switching.
* Preserve drafts.
* Show pending and failed message states.
* Show accurate transfer progress.
* Confirm destructive actions.
* Remove inaccessible administrative controls.
* Still rely on server-side permission enforcement.
* Avoid showing buttons for deferred non-functional features.

---

# Chapter 2268 — User Interface Summary

BlueBubbles shall provide a consistent PySide6 desktop interface centred around a three-column messaging layout.

The navigation sidebar shall provide access to chats, contacts, groups, transfers, search, announcements, settings and authorised administration.

The conversation list shall show current activity, unread state and message previews.

The chat page shall support:

* Paginated message history.
* Pending and failed states.
* Replies.
* Editing.
* Deletion.
* Drafts.
* Attachments.
* Delivery and read states.
* Typing indicators.
* Verification warnings.

File operations shall expose preparation, upload, download, verification and recovery states.

Settings shall separate:

* User preferences.
* Server-enforced policy.
* Storage controls.
* Privacy controls.
* Session management.
* Diagnostics.

Administrative pages shall provide health, users, sessions, connections, audit events, alerts, workers, configuration and announcements without exposing user private keys or plaintext message content.

The interface shall support keyboard navigation, accessible labels, high contrast, text scaling and reduced motion.

The application shall remain responsive while performing encryption, transfer, search, synchronisation and administration operations.

No destructive action shall occur without clear confirmation.

No required feature shall be represented by a button that does nothing.

---

# End of Part 23

Part 24 will define the complete cryptographic implementation contract and key lifecycle, including:

* Exact algorithm selections.
* Identity-key generation.
* Signing keys.
* Encryption keys.
* Content keys.
* Nonce requirements.
* Message authenticated data.
* Recipient key envelopes.
* Attachment chunk encryption.
* Local storage encryption.
* Key rotation.
* Key revocation.
* Key backup limitations.
* Multi-device limitations.
* Cryptographic test vectors.


# Part 24 — Cryptographic Implementation Contract and Key Lifecycle

---

# Chapter 2269 — Cryptographic Contract Purpose

This section defines the exact cryptographic responsibilities of the BlueBubbles client and server.

It shall ensure that:

* Message plaintext is encrypted before leaving the client.
* Attachment plaintext is encrypted before upload.
* The server stores ciphertext rather than plaintext content.
* Recipients receive only the encrypted keys intended for them.
* Messages are authenticated and signed.
* Attachment chunks are independently authenticated.
* Nonces are generated safely.
* Key versions are tracked.
* Revoked keys are not used for new encryption.
* Local cached plaintext is encrypted at rest.
* Cryptographic failures stop content display.
* The application does not invent custom cryptographic algorithms.

The coding AI shall use established, maintained cryptographic libraries and shall not implement primitive algorithms manually.

---

# Chapter 2270 — Security Boundary

The intended security boundary is:

```text
Sender client
    Encrypts and signs plaintext
            │
            ▼
BlueBubbles server
    Stores and forwards ciphertext
            │
            ▼
Recipient client
    Verifies and decrypts ciphertext
```

The server may process:

* User identifiers.
* Conversation membership.
* Key versions.
* Message identifiers.
* Timestamps.
* Ciphertext sizes.
* Delivery metadata.
* Attachment transfer state.

The server shall not require access to:

* Message plaintext.
* Attachment plaintext.
* Message content keys.
* Attachment file keys.
* User private identity keys.
* Local cache encryption keys.

---

# Chapter 2271 — Threat Model Scope

The cryptographic design shall protect content against:

* Passive network observers.
* Accidental plaintext exposure in the server database.
* Server filesystem readers without client keys.
* Database backup readers without client keys.
* Modification of encrypted message payloads.
* Modification of attachment chunks.
* Substitution of signed message metadata.
* Use of the wrong recipient key envelope.
* Accidental cross-user local cache access.

The Version 1.0 design does not fully protect against:

* A compromised sender device before encryption.
* A compromised recipient device after decryption.
* Malware reading plaintext from client memory.
* A malicious authorised recipient copying content.
* A malicious server withholding messages.
* A malicious server altering membership information before clients obtain it.
* Traffic analysis based on message timing and size.
* Complete cryptographic forward secrecy.
* Full multi-device key synchronisation.
* Recovery after permanent loss of all private keys.

These limitations shall be documented honestly.

---

# Chapter 2272 — Approved Cryptographic Library

Preferred Python library:

```text
cryptography
```

The implementation shall use high-level APIs from the library wherever possible.

Alternative libraries may be used only when:

* They are actively maintained.
* They support Python 3.13.
* Their APIs are well documented.
* They provide the required algorithms safely.
* The change is documented and tested.

The application shall not copy primitive implementations from blogs or informal examples.

---

# Chapter 2273 — Mandatory Algorithm Set

Version 1.0 shall use:

```text
Symmetric authenticated encryption:

AES-256-GCM

Public-key encryption or key agreement:

X25519 with an authenticated key-wrapping construction

Digital signatures:

Ed25519

Hashing:

SHA-256

Key derivation:

HKDF-SHA-256

Password hashing:

Argon2id

Search token generation:

HMAC-SHA-256

Secure randomness:

Operating-system cryptographic random source
```

Algorithm identifiers shall be stored in envelopes and validated explicitly.

---

# Chapter 2274 — Algorithm Separation

Separate keys shall be used for separate purposes.

A key shall not be reused as both:

* Encryption key and signing key.
* Message content key and local cache key.
* Attachment key and message key.
* Search-index key and database key.
* Token-signing secret and encryption key.
* Password-derived key and public identity key.

Key-purpose separation reduces the effect of accidental misuse.

---

# Chapter 2275 — Identity Key Types

Each cryptographic user profile shall have at least two identity key pairs:

```text
X25519 encryption key pair

Ed25519 signing key pair
```

Encryption key pair purpose:

```text
Protect message and attachment content keys for the user
```

Signing key pair purpose:

```text
Sign outgoing encrypted message envelopes and selected attachment metadata
```

The public keys may be uploaded to the server.

The private keys shall remain encrypted on the client device.

---

# Chapter 2276 — Identity Key Versioning

Each identity key shall have:

```text
User ID
Key type
Key version
Algorithm
Public key
Fingerprint
Created timestamp
Expiry timestamp where used
Revocation timestamp
Primary state
```

Version numbering shall begin at:

```text
1
```

and increase monotonically for each user and key type.

Encryption and signing key versions may rotate independently.

---

# Chapter 2277 — Public Key Encoding

Public keys shall use a stable binary encoding provided by the cryptographic library.

Recommended:

```text
Raw X25519 public-key bytes
Raw Ed25519 public-key bytes
```

At JSON protocol boundaries, binary values shall use:

```text
Base64
```

The protocol model shall validate decoded key lengths.

PEM may be used for administrative export or development inspection, but raw key encoding is preferred for message envelopes.

---

# Chapter 2278 — Public Key Fingerprints

Fingerprint calculation:

```text
SHA-256(
    algorithm_identifier
    ||
    key_type_identifier
    ||
    key_version
    ||
    raw_public_key
)
```

The exact canonical field encoding shall be fixed and tested.

Display format may group hexadecimal characters:

```text
A1B2 C3D4 E5F6 0718
92A3 B4C5 D6E7 F809
```

The fingerprint is an identifier, not proof that the key belongs to the claimed user unless independently verified.

---

# Chapter 2279 — Identity Key Generation

Identity keys shall be generated on the client using the cryptographic library.

Conceptual interface:

```python
class IdentityKeyGenerator:
    """Generates client identity encryption and signing keys."""

    def generate_encryption_key_pair(
        self,
        version: int,
    ) -> EncryptionKeyPair:
        ...

    def generate_signing_key_pair(
        self,
        version: int,
    ) -> SigningKeyPair:
        ...
```

The implementation shall use the library’s X25519 and Ed25519 key-generation methods.

---

# Chapter 2280 — Secure Randomness

Random cryptographic values shall come from:

```text
Operating-system cryptographic random source
```

Suitable Python source:

```python
secrets.token_bytes()
```

or library-managed key and nonce generation.

Do not use:

```python
random.random()
random.randint()
uuid-based byte truncation
timestamps
user IDs
message IDs alone
```

for secret keys or nonces.

---

# Chapter 2281 — Private Key Storage

Private identity keys shall be stored:

```text
Serialised

↓

Encrypted using a dedicated local key-encryption key

↓

Written to the user-specific private-key store
```

The private-key store shall contain:

```text
Key type
Version
Algorithm
Encrypted private-key bytes
Nonce
Authentication tag
Creation timestamp
Public-key fingerprint
```

The plaintext private key shall never be written to disk.

---

# Chapter 2282 — Private Key Encryption

Private-key records shall use:

```text
AES-256-GCM
```

with:

* A dedicated local key-encryption key.
* A fresh random nonce for every encrypted key record.
* Authenticated metadata as AAD.
* No nonce reuse under the same key.

Authenticated metadata may include:

```text
User ID
Device ID
Key type
Key version
Algorithm
Public-key fingerprint
```

---

# Chapter 2283 — Private Key Handle

Private-key consumers should receive a restricted handle rather than casually sharing raw private-key bytes.

Conceptual model:

```python
class PrivateKeyHandle:
    """Provides controlled access to one in-memory private key."""

    key_type: PrivateKeyType
    version: int
    algorithm: str
    fingerprint: str

    def use_key(self) -> ContextManager[PrivateKeyObject]:
        ...
```

The implementation shall minimise private-key lifetime in temporary variables.

Python cannot guarantee perfect memory erasure, so documentation shall not claim it does.

---

# Chapter 2284 — Local Key-Encryption Key

The key used to encrypt private identity keys shall:

* Be 256 random bits.
* Be unique per client user profile.
* Be protected by the operating-system secure store.
* Never be uploaded.
* Never be derived only from username or device name.
* Be destroyed when all local cryptographic data is intentionally cleared.

Where possible, use a separate key from the local message-cache key.

---

# Chapter 2285 — Key Registration

After generating new identity keys, the client shall upload only:

```text
Public encryption key
Public signing key
Key versions
Algorithms
Fingerprints
Creation metadata
```

The registration request shall be authenticated.

The server shall reject:

* Duplicate conflicting key versions.
* Unsupported algorithms.
* Invalid key lengths.
* Private-key fields.
* Fingerprints that do not match the submitted public keys.

---

# Chapter 2286 — Public Key Retrieval

Before encrypting content for a recipient, the client shall retrieve or validate:

```text
Recipient user ID
Active public encryption key
Key version
Algorithm
Fingerprint
Revocation state
```

Before verifying a message, the client shall retrieve:

```text
Sender public signing key
Signing key version
Algorithm
Fingerprint
Revocation metadata
```

Public-key results may be cached locally with expiry and invalidation.

---

# Chapter 2287 — Public Key Trust Limitation

In Version 1.0, the server distributes user public keys.

Therefore, a fully malicious server could potentially substitute public keys before encryption unless users independently compare fingerprints.

The design shall provide:

* Stable fingerprints.
* Key-change events.
* Key-version history.
* Visible key information.
* Warnings for unexpected replacement.

A future version may add stronger independent key verification or transparency mechanisms.

---

# Chapter 2288 — Key Change Warning

When a known user’s primary public key changes unexpectedly, the client may display:

```text
Alex’s encryption key has changed.

This may happen after a device reset or key rotation.
```

The client shall:

* Refresh key metadata.
* Stop using the old key for new content if revoked.
* Preserve old key versions for historical verification.
* Avoid silently replacing a pinned fingerprint where manual verification was enabled.

---

# Chapter 2289 — Message Content Key

Each message shall use a fresh random content key.

Requirements:

```text
Algorithm:

AES-256-GCM

Key length:

32 bytes

Key scope:

One message version only
```

A content key shall never be reused for:

* Another message.
* An edited version of the same message.
* An attachment.
* Local caching.
* Multiple protocol purposes.

---

# Chapter 2290 — Message Nonce

Each message AES-GCM encryption shall use:

```text
96-bit nonce
```

Requirements:

* Generated randomly.
* Fresh for every message content key.
* Stored with the ciphertext.
* Included in signed metadata.
* Never reused with the same content key.

Because each message uses a fresh random key, accidental nonce collision under one key is already strongly limited, but unique nonce generation remains mandatory.

---

# Chapter 2291 — AES-GCM Tag Representation

The implementation may use either:

```text
Ciphertext with appended authentication tag
```

or:

```text
Ciphertext and tag stored separately
```

One representation shall be selected consistently.

The current database design permits:

```text
ciphertext
authentication_tag
```

If the library returns combined output, the adapter shall split and reconstruct it safely.

Expected GCM tag size:

```text
128 bits
```

Tag truncation shall not be used.

---

# Chapter 2292 — Message Plaintext Structure

Before encryption, the message content shall be serialised into a validated structure.

Example:

```json
{
    "format_version": 1,
    "text": "Example message",
    "mentions": [],
    "client_metadata": {}
}
```

The plaintext structure may contain:

* Message text.
* Mention references.
* Limited display metadata.

It shall not contain:

* Access tokens.
* Private keys.
* Recipient private information unrelated to the message.
* Unbounded arbitrary executable content.

---

# Chapter 2293 — Message Authenticated Data

AES-GCM additional authenticated data shall bind the ciphertext to critical unencrypted metadata.

Recommended AAD fields:

```text
Protocol version
Message ID
Conversation ID
Sender user ID
Message type
Reply target ID or null marker
Client-created timestamp
Message version
Ordered attachment ID list
Content-encryption algorithm
```

The exact canonical encoding shall be fixed.

If any authenticated field changes, decryption shall fail.

---

# Chapter 2294 — AAD Exclusions

AAD shall not include mutable server-generated fields that may legitimately change after encryption, such as:

```text
Delivery state
Read timestamp
Server processing duration
WebSocket event ID
Unread count
```

Including mutable fields would make normal server updates invalidate ciphertext.

---

# Chapter 2295 — Canonical Message AAD

Conceptual model:

```python
class MessageAuthenticatedData(BaseModel):
    protocol_version: int
    message_id: UUID
    conversation_id: UUID
    sender_id: UUID
    message_type: MessageType
    reply_to_message_id: UUID | None
    client_created_at: datetime
    message_version: int
    attachment_ids: tuple[UUID, ...]
    content_algorithm: str
```

Canonical serialisation shall produce identical bytes on all supported clients.

---

# Chapter 2296 — Message Signature Scope

The sender shall sign the encrypted message envelope.

Signed fields shall include:

```text
Message AAD
Ciphertext
Nonce
Authentication tag
Recipient envelope descriptors
Signing key version
Signature algorithm
```

Recipient envelope descriptors shall include at least:

```text
Recipient user ID
Recipient encryption-key version
Wrapped content-key bytes
Ephemeral public key
Key-envelope algorithm
```

This prevents the server from replacing or reassigning recipient key envelopes without invalidating the signature.

---

# Chapter 2297 — Recipient Envelope Ordering

Recipient key envelopes shall be sorted canonically before signing.

Recommended ordering:

```text
recipient_user_id ascending by UUID bytes
```

The canonical serialiser shall reject duplicate recipients.

The same logical recipient set shall always produce the same ordering for signature verification.

---

# Chapter 2298 — Message Signature Algorithm

Use:

```text
Ed25519
```

The sender signs canonical bytes derived from the encrypted envelope.

The signature shall be stored with:

```text
Signing key version
Signature algorithm identifier
```

The server may validate structural presence but does not need the private key.

Recipients shall verify the signature before attempting to display plaintext.

---

# Chapter 2299 — Signature Verification Order

Recommended recipient processing order:

```text
Validate envelope structure

↓

Retrieve sender signing public key

↓

Reconstruct canonical signed bytes

↓

Verify Ed25519 signature

↓

Locate recipient key envelope

↓

Decrypt content key

↓

Reconstruct AES-GCM AAD

↓

Decrypt and authenticate ciphertext

↓

Parse plaintext
```

The client shall not display content before signature and GCM authentication succeed.

---

# Chapter 2300 — Message Key Envelope Construction

Each message content key shall be separately wrapped for every active recipient, including the sender.

Required inputs:

```text
Message content key
Recipient X25519 public key
Recipient key version
Message ID
Recipient user ID
Protocol version
```

The design shall use an ephemeral sender-side X25519 key for each recipient envelope or one carefully specified ephemeral key per message.

For Version 1.0, the clearest design is:

```text
One fresh ephemeral X25519 key pair per recipient envelope
```

This increases envelope size but reduces cross-recipient coupling and simplifies reasoning.

---

# Chapter 2301 — X25519 Shared Secret

For one recipient:

```text
Ephemeral private key
    +
Recipient public X25519 key
    ↓
X25519 shared secret
```

The raw shared secret shall not be used directly as an AES key.

It shall be passed through:

```text
HKDF-SHA-256
```

to derive a wrapping key.

---

# Chapter 2302 — Key-Wrapping Key Derivation

Recommended HKDF inputs:

```text
Input key material:

X25519 shared secret

Salt:

Protocol-defined value or message-specific random salt

Info:

Protocol identifier
Message ID
Recipient user ID
Recipient key version
Envelope algorithm identifier
```

Derived output:

```text
32-byte AES wrapping key
```

If a random salt is used, it shall be stored in the recipient envelope.

The exact derivation format shall be fixed and tested.

---

# Chapter 2303 — Message Content-Key Wrapping

The message content key shall be encrypted using:

```text
AES-256-GCM
```

with the derived wrapping key.

The key-envelope record shall contain:

```text
Recipient user ID
Recipient key version
Ephemeral public key
HKDF salt where used
Wrapping nonce
Wrapped content key
Wrapping authentication tag
Envelope algorithm
```

The wrapping nonce shall be unique for that derived wrapping key.

A fresh derived key and fresh nonce shall be used for every envelope.

---

# Chapter 2304 — Key-Envelope Authenticated Data

Wrapping AAD shall include:

```text
Protocol version
Message ID
Conversation ID
Recipient user ID
Recipient key version
Ephemeral public key
Envelope algorithm
Content-encryption algorithm
```

This prevents the wrapped key from being moved to another message or recipient without authentication failure.

---

# Chapter 2305 — Recipient Envelope Model

Conceptual model:

```python
class RecipientKeyEnvelope(BaseModel):
    recipient_user_id: UUID
    recipient_key_version: int
    envelope_algorithm: str
    ephemeral_public_key: bytes
    hkdf_salt: bytes
    wrapping_nonce: bytes
    wrapped_content_key: bytes
    wrapping_authentication_tag: bytes
```

All binary fields shall be Base64 encoded only at JSON boundaries.

---

# Chapter 2306 — Sender Envelope Requirement

The sender shall receive their own recipient envelope.

Reasons:

* The sender can decrypt sent messages after restart.
* Sent-message cache can be rebuilt.
* The server does not need a special plaintext key copy.
* Message retrieval uses one consistent recipient mechanism.

The sender’s envelope shall use the sender’s active public encryption key like any other recipient.

---

# Chapter 2307 — Recipient Coverage

For a normal conversation message, the request shall include envelopes for:

```text
Every active conversation member authorised for the message

including

The sender
```

The server shall compare the recipient set against active membership.

The server shall reject:

* Missing recipient envelope.
* Extra unrelated recipient envelope.
* Duplicate recipient.
* Revoked key version where known.
* Envelope for a removed member.
* Envelope using unsupported key algorithm.

---

# Chapter 2308 — Group History Behaviour

When a user joins a group:

* They receive key envelopes only for new messages sent after joining.
* Historical message keys are not automatically rewrapped.
* The server does not decrypt or re-encrypt historical messages.
* Existing members are not required to rewrite all prior messages.

This matches the default Version 1.0 group history policy.

---

# Chapter 2309 — Member Removal Behaviour

When a user is removed:

* They receive no future recipient envelopes.
* Their previously stored envelopes remain part of historical message records.
* The system cannot revoke plaintext already decrypted or copied.
* The client shall refresh membership before processing its offline send queue.

Removal protects future content, not information already received.

---

# Chapter 2310 — Edited Message Cryptography

An edited message shall use:

```text
New random content key
New message nonce
New recipient key envelopes
New signature
Incremented message version
```

The previous content key and nonce shall not be reused.

The message ID remains the same.

The new version number shall be included in AAD and signature input.

---

# Chapter 2311 — Reply Cryptographic Binding

The reply target ID shall be included in message AAD and signed fields.

The plaintext may contain a display preview, but the authoritative reply relationship is:

```text
reply_to_message_id
```

If the reply target is modified, signature or GCM verification shall fail.

---

# Chapter 2312 — Attachment Cryptographic Overview

Each attachment shall use:

```text
One random 256-bit file key

Fresh authenticated encryption per chunk

Recipient-specific file-key envelopes

Encrypted file metadata

Optional encrypted thumbnail
```

The server shall never require the plaintext file key.

---

# Chapter 2313 — Attachment File Key

Requirements:

```text
Length:

32 bytes

Algorithm:

AES-256-GCM

Scope:

One attachment only
```

The file key may be used to derive separate subkeys for:

* Chunk encryption.
* Metadata encryption.
* Thumbnail encryption.

Direct reuse of one key with overlapping nonce namespaces should be avoided.

---

# Chapter 2314 — Attachment Subkey Derivation

Preferred design:

```text
Random attachment master file key

↓

HKDF-SHA-256

├── Chunk encryption key
├── Metadata encryption key
└── Thumbnail encryption key
```

HKDF `info` values shall be distinct:

```text
bluebubbles-attachment-chunks-v1
bluebubbles-attachment-metadata-v1
bluebubbles-attachment-thumbnail-v1
```

This provides purpose separation.

The recipient envelope wraps the attachment master file key.

---

# Chapter 2315 — Attachment Chunk Encryption

Version 1.0 shall encrypt each chunk independently using:

```text
AES-256-GCM
```

Each chunk shall have:

```text
Chunk index
Fresh random 96-bit nonce
Ciphertext
128-bit authentication tag
Encrypted checksum
Plaintext length metadata where required
```

Independent chunk encryption supports:

* Resume.
* Retry.
* Out-of-order upload.
* Bounded memory use.
* Per-chunk corruption detection.

---

# Chapter 2316 — Attachment Chunk Nonce Rule

Every chunk encrypted under the same chunk-encryption key shall use a unique nonce.

The implementation shall generate a fresh random 96-bit nonce per chunk.

The client shall never derive the nonce only from:

```text
Chunk index
Filename
Timestamp
```

unless a formally specified deterministic nonce construction guarantees uniqueness.

Random nonces are preferred for Version 1.0.

---

# Chapter 2317 — Chunk Authenticated Data

Chunk AAD shall include:

```text
Protocol version
Attachment ID
Conversation ID
Uploader user ID
Chunk index
Total chunk count
Expected plaintext chunk length
Content algorithm
Attachment manifest version
```

This prevents:

* Reordering without detection.
* Substitution between attachments.
* Reuse under another conversation.
* Incorrect chunk index interpretation.

---

# Chapter 2318 — Chunk Ciphertext Hash

The client shall calculate:

```text
SHA-256 of the complete encrypted chunk representation
```

The representation shall include, consistently:

```text
Nonce
Ciphertext
Authentication tag
```

The server may use this hash to:

* Detect transfer corruption.
* Validate repeated identical chunk uploads.
* Confirm stored bytes match submitted metadata.

This hash is not a replacement for AES-GCM authentication.

Only the client can validate plaintext authenticity through the GCM tag.

---

# Chapter 2319 — Attachment Plaintext Hash

The client should calculate:

```text
SHA-256 of the complete plaintext file
```

Purpose:

* Verify final decrypted output.
* Detect local source changes.
* Confirm recipient reassembly.

The plaintext hash shall be stored only inside encrypted attachment metadata.

It shall not be exposed as server-readable metadata unless a specific privacy decision is made.

---

# Chapter 2320 — Attachment Ciphertext Hash

The client shall calculate an overall encrypted-file hash.

Recommended input:

```text
Canonical ordered sequence of:
    chunk index
    nonce
    ciphertext
    authentication tag
```

The server may validate that final stored encrypted chunks match the declared encrypted manifest.

The overall encrypted hash does not authenticate plaintext by itself.

---

# Chapter 2321 — Attachment Metadata

Plaintext attachment metadata may include:

```json
{
    "format_version": 1,
    "original_filename": "report.pdf",
    "mime_type": "application/pdf",
    "plaintext_size": 123456,
    "plaintext_sha256": "hex-value",
    "chunk_count": 12,
    "chunk_size": 1048576
}
```

This metadata shall be encrypted using the attachment metadata subkey.

The server may separately store limited user-supplied display metadata according to the selected database model, but it must not be treated as trustworthy plaintext inspection.

---

# Chapter 2322 — Attachment Metadata AAD

Metadata encryption AAD shall include:

```text
Protocol version
Attachment ID
Conversation ID
Uploader user ID
Manifest version
Metadata algorithm
```

The encrypted metadata record shall contain:

```text
Nonce
Ciphertext
Authentication tag
```

---

# Chapter 2323 — Attachment Manifest

The encrypted or signed attachment manifest shall bind:

```text
Attachment ID
Conversation ID
Uploader ID
Chunk count
Chunk ordering
Encrypted chunk hashes
Encrypted total size
Recipient key-envelope descriptors
Encrypted metadata
Protocol version
```

The sender should sign the manifest using Ed25519.

Recipients shall verify the manifest signature before trusting chunk arrangement or encrypted metadata.

---

# Chapter 2324 — Attachment Signature Scope

Signed attachment fields shall include:

```text
Manifest metadata
Encrypted metadata fields
Every chunk index and encrypted hash
Recipient file-key envelope descriptors
Uploader signing-key version
Attachment algorithm identifiers
```

This prevents the server from:

* Reordering chunks.
* Replacing chunk hashes.
* Reassigning recipient envelopes.
* Changing manifest metadata.

---

# Chapter 2325 — Attachment File-Key Envelopes

The attachment master file key shall be wrapped separately for every active conversation member, including the uploader.

The wrapping process shall use the same general X25519, HKDF-SHA-256 and AES-256-GCM design as message content-key envelopes.

Envelope AAD shall bind:

```text
Attachment ID
Conversation ID
Recipient user ID
Recipient key version
Uploader user ID
Protocol version
```

---

# Chapter 2326 — Attachment Upload Sequence

Cryptographic preparation:

```text
Select file

↓

Validate client-side limits

↓

Generate attachment ID

↓

Generate random attachment master file key

↓

Derive chunk, metadata and thumbnail subkeys

↓

Read file in chunks

↓

Encrypt every chunk with unique nonce and AAD

↓

Calculate encrypted hashes

↓

Encrypt metadata

↓

Create recipient file-key envelopes

↓

Build and sign manifest

↓

Start server upload
```

The server receives only encrypted chunk bytes and encrypted metadata.

---

# Chapter 2327 — Attachment Download Sequence

Recipient flow:

```text
Fetch authorised attachment metadata

↓

Fetch recipient file-key envelope

↓

Retrieve uploader signing key

↓

Verify manifest signature

↓

Unwrap attachment master file key

↓

Derive subkeys

↓

Download encrypted chunks

↓

Verify encrypted chunk hashes

↓

Decrypt and authenticate each chunk

↓

Write plaintext to partial destination

↓

Verify final plaintext SHA-256

↓

Atomically rename completed file
```

Failure at any verification stage shall stop completion.

---

# Chapter 2328 — Partial Plaintext Download Safety

During decryption, plaintext shall be written to:

```text
A controlled partial file
```

Example suffix:

```text
.bluebubbles-partial
```

The partial file shall:

* Use restrictive local permissions.
* Not be treated as complete.
* Be deleted after permanent verification failure.
* Be renamed only after complete hash verification.
* Not overwrite an existing destination before confirmation.

---

# Chapter 2329 — Thumbnail Cryptography

Optional thumbnails shall be:

* Generated on the sender client.
* Encrypted with the thumbnail subkey.
* Given a fresh nonce.
* Bound to the attachment ID using AAD.
* Included in the signed manifest.

Recipients may decrypt thumbnails after obtaining the attachment file key.

The server shall not create plaintext thumbnails from encrypted files.

---

# Chapter 2330 — Local Cache Encryption

Sensitive local values shall use:

```text
AES-256-GCM
```

Examples:

* Decrypted message cache.
* Drafts.
* Offline action payloads.
* Search previews.
* Transfer manifests.
* Local private-key records.

Each record shall use:

* Appropriate purpose-specific key.
* Fresh random nonce.
* Context-specific AAD.
* Authentication-tag verification before deserialisation.

---

# Chapter 2331 — Local Encryption Purposes

Recommended purposes:

```text
PRIVATE_KEY_STORAGE
MESSAGE_DISPLAY_CACHE
DRAFT_STORAGE
OFFLINE_QUEUE
SEARCH_PREVIEW
TRANSFER_STATE
THUMBNAIL_CACHE
```

Each purpose shall have:

* A separate key.
* Or a derived subkey using HKDF with a unique `info` value.

Using one unrestricted key for every local purpose shall be avoided.

---

# Chapter 2332 — Local Master Key

A client profile may have one random local master key protected by the operating-system secure store.

Purpose-specific keys may then be derived:

```text
Local master key

↓

HKDF-SHA-256

├── Private-key storage key
├── Display-cache key
├── Draft key
├── Offline-queue key
├── Search-preview key
└── Transfer-state key
```

The HKDF salt and derivation version shall be stored safely.

The master key shall never leave the device.

---

# Chapter 2333 — Local Record AAD

Local cache AAD shall bind encrypted values to their intended record.

Examples:

Message cache:

```text
User ID
Message ID
Conversation ID
Cache format version
```

Draft:

```text
User ID
Conversation ID
Draft version
```

Offline action:

```text
User ID
Action ID
Action type
```

Moving ciphertext between records shall cause authentication failure.

---

# Chapter 2334 — Local Database Encryption Fallback

If SQLCipher is unavailable, application-level encryption shall protect sensitive columns.

Unencrypted local database fields may still reveal metadata such as:

* Conversation identifiers.
* Cache timestamps.
* Record sizes.
* Transfer state categories.

The limitation shall be documented.

The application shall not claim the entire SQLite file is encrypted unless SQLCipher or equivalent full-database encryption is actually active.

---

# Chapter 2335 — Search Index Cryptography

Search tokens shall use:

```text
HMAC-SHA-256
```

Input:

```text
Normalised token
```

Key:

```text
User-specific local search key
```

Stored value:

```text
HMAC digest
```

This prevents ordinary plaintext search words from appearing directly in the index.

It does not prevent frequency analysis by someone with access to a large index.

---

# Chapter 2336 — Password Hashing

Local account passwords shall use:

```text
Argon2id
```

The password-hashing library shall store the complete encoded hash containing:

* Algorithm.
* Version.
* Memory cost.
* Time cost.
* Parallelism.
* Salt.
* Hash output.

Plaintext passwords shall never be stored.

The application shall support rehashing after parameter upgrades.

---

# Chapter 2337 — Password Hashing Parameters

Parameters shall be selected using the library’s secure recommendations and tested on deployment hardware.

The target should make one verification expensive enough to resist bulk guessing while remaining acceptable for login.

The final parameters shall be documented.

They shall not be copied blindly without performance measurement.

---

# Chapter 2338 — Token Hashing

Refresh tokens shall be:

* Random high-entropy values.
* Stored on the client in the secure store.
* Stored on the server only as hashes.

Recommended server storage:

```text
SHA-256 of the random refresh token
```

A password hash is unnecessary for a sufficiently random token because offline dictionary guessing is not the threat.

Comparison shall use constant-time comparison where practical.

---

# Chapter 2339 — Access Token Signing

Access tokens shall use a supported signing configuration.

For a simple Version 1.0 deployment, an HMAC-signed token may use:

```text
HS256
```

with a strong random server secret.

A stronger asymmetric deployment may use:

```text
EdDSA or another supported server-signing algorithm
```

One algorithm shall be configured explicitly.

The token decoder shall reject algorithm substitution.

---

# Chapter 2340 — Transport Security

All client-server communication in production shall use:

```text
TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3
```

TLS remains required even though message contents are end-to-end encrypted.

TLS protects:

* Credentials.
* Tokens.
* Public-key distribution.
* Message metadata.
* Conversation membership.
* Delivery information.
* Administrative endpoints.
* WebSocket events.
* Attachment transfer metadata.

---

# Chapter 2341 — Nonce Generation Interface

```python
class NonceGenerator:
    """Generates nonces of the required size using secure randomness."""

    def generate_gcm_nonce(self) -> bytes:
        nonce = secrets.token_bytes(12)
        if len(nonce) != 12:
            raise CryptographicConfigurationError()
        return nonce
```

The service shall not keep a predictable global counter unless the complete key and counter lifecycle is formally specified.

---

# Chapter 2342 — Nonce-Reuse Testing

Tests shall verify that a large sample of generated nonces contains no duplicates.

This test does not prove future uniqueness, but it can detect:

* Constant nonce defects.
* Broken mocks.
* Incorrect truncation.
* Accidental deterministic reuse.

The cryptographic design shall rely primarily on correct generation and key scoping, not on runtime databases of every nonce ever used.

---

# Chapter 2343 — Canonical Serialisation Rules

Canonical serialisation shall define:

```text
UTF-8 encoding
Stable key ordering
No insignificant whitespace
Explicit null representation
Canonical UUID representation
Canonical UTC timestamp representation
Binary values represented consistently
Integer formatting
Array ordering rules
```

The same canonical fields shall produce identical bytes on all supported systems.

---

# Chapter 2344 — Timestamp Canonicalisation

Timestamps included in signed or authenticated data shall be:

```text
UTC
RFC 3339-compatible
Fixed precision
Explicit Z suffix
```

Example:

```text
2026-07-16T17:42:13.123456Z
```

The chosen precision shall be fixed.

Equivalent timestamps with different textual offsets shall be normalised before serialisation.

---

# Chapter 2345 — UUID Canonicalisation

UUIDs shall be serialised using:

```text
Lowercase standard hyphenated representation
```

Example:

```text
a73d4d13-e978-4bd6-b443-aee18c26d171
```

or raw 16-byte form.

One representation shall be selected for each canonical serialiser and tested.

---

# Chapter 2346 — Binary Canonicalisation

Within canonical signed structures, binary values may be represented as:

```text
Raw length-prefixed bytes
```

or:

```text
Base64 strings using one fixed alphabet and padding rule
```

Raw length-prefixed binary is preferable for internal canonical builders.

JSON Base64 may be used where all clients share the exact implementation.

---

# Chapter 2347 — Domain Separation

Cryptographic hashes, HKDF values and signatures shall include context identifiers.

Examples:

```text
bluebubbles-message-aad-v1
bluebubbles-message-signature-v1
bluebubbles-message-key-envelope-v1
bluebubbles-attachment-manifest-v1
bluebubbles-local-cache-v1
```

This prevents the same bytes from being interpreted across unrelated cryptographic purposes.

---

# Chapter 2348 — Protocol Version Binding

Every encrypted or signed structure shall include:

```text
Protocol version
Format version
Algorithm identifiers
```

Recipients shall reject unsupported versions.

They shall not guess how to interpret an unknown cryptographic format.

---

# Chapter 2349 — Algorithm Registry

Conceptual class:

```python
class CryptographicAlgorithmRegistry:
    """Maps protocol algorithm identifiers to approved implementations."""

    def get_content_cipher(
        self,
        identifier: str,
    ) -> ContentCipher:
        ...

    def get_signature_service(
        self,
        identifier: str,
    ) -> SignatureService:
        ...

    def require_supported(
        self,
        identifier: str,
    ) -> None:
        ...
```

The registry shall contain an allowlist.

It shall not load arbitrary algorithms by class name from network input.

---

# Chapter 2350 — Cryptographic Agility

The protocol shall record algorithm identifiers so later versions can migrate.

However, Version 1.0 shall implement only the approved algorithm suite.

Cryptographic agility shall not result in:

* Accepting weak fallback algorithms.
* Negotiating to an insecure client choice.
* Supporting unused legacy algorithms.
* Complex dynamic plugin loading.

---

# Chapter 2351 — Deprecated Algorithm Handling

If an algorithm becomes deprecated:

```text
Stop using it for new encryption.

Continue reading historical content where policy permits.

Warn administrators.

Require client updates if necessary.

Rotate identity keys where required.

Record migration status.
```

Historical content shall not be silently rendered unreadable without a migration or retention decision.

---

# Chapter 2352 — Key Rotation Purpose

Key rotation may be required after:

* Suspected private-key compromise.
* Device reset.
* Planned security policy.
* Algorithm migration.
* Key-store corruption.
* User-requested identity reset.
* Expiry policy.

Rotation affects future encryption and signature verification.

---

# Chapter 2353 — Encryption Key Rotation

Process:

```text
Generate new X25519 key pair

↓

Assign next encryption-key version

↓

Encrypt private key locally

↓

Register new public key

↓

Mark new key primary

↓

Publish key-change event

↓

Stop using old key for new envelopes

↓

Retain old private key for historical messages
```

The old key may be revoked for new use while remaining locally available for decryption of old content.

---

# Chapter 2354 — Signing Key Rotation

Process:

```text
Generate new Ed25519 key pair

↓

Assign next signing-key version

↓

Store private key locally

↓

Register public key

↓

Mark new key primary

↓

Sign new messages with new key

↓

Retain old public key on server
```

Recipients use the signing key version stored in each message to verify historical signatures.

---

# Chapter 2355 — Key Revocation

Revocation shall store:

```text
Key type
Key version
Revoked timestamp
Reason
Replacement key version where applicable
```

Revoked keys shall not be selected for new encryption or signing.

Historical verification may continue using a revoked signing public key because revocation does not mean every historical signature was invalid.

The UI shall distinguish:

```text
Revoked for future use
```

from:

```text
Cryptographically invalid
```

---

# Chapter 2356 — Compromised Encryption Key

If a private encryption key is believed compromised:

* Revoke it for future envelopes.
* Generate a replacement.
* Invalidate relevant device session where appropriate.
* Create audit and security alerts.
* Warn that previously encrypted content for that key may be exposed.
* Do not claim old ciphertext becomes safe after rotation.

Forward secrecy is not provided for historical envelopes.

---

# Chapter 2357 — Compromised Signing Key

If a signing private key is believed compromised:

* Revoke it for new signatures.
* Generate a replacement.
* Record compromise timestamp.
* Warn that signatures made with the key may require contextual review.
* Preserve the public key for verification and investigation.
* Do not silently relabel old signatures as definitely malicious.

---

# Chapter 2358 — Key Expiry

Version 1.0 may support optional key expiry metadata.
