```text
Open dashboard
Identify a degraded component
Search for a user
Disable the user
Confirm sessions were revoked
Re-enable the user
Change an allowed role
View the audit records
Acknowledge an alert
Run an allowed worker
Publish an announcement
Create an audit export
Enter and exit maintenance mode in test environment
```

Every completed action shall produce expected audit evidence.

---

# Chapter 2562 — Helpdesk Acceptance Tests

Helpdesk tester shall:

```text
Search for ordinary user
View account state
View active sessions
Revoke one session
Run permitted diagnostic
View limited audit result
Confirm private message content is unavailable
Attempt forbidden role change and receive denial
```

---

# Chapter 2563 — SuperAdministrator Acceptance Tests

SuperAdministrator tester shall:

```text
Review security configuration
Change a safe setting
Review configuration history
Run full audit verification
Resolve a critical test alert
Attempt to disable final SuperAdministrator and receive protection
Review maintenance controls
```

All actions shall be performed in a test environment.

---

# Chapter 2564 — Operational Runbook Requirements

The administration documentation shall include runbooks for:

```text
User cannot log in
User lost device
User role incorrect
Redis unavailable
Active Directory unavailable
Attachment storage full
Database unavailable
Outbox backlog
Audit verification failed
Certificate nearing expiry
Worker repeatedly failing
Backup failed
Configuration rollback
Emergency administrator recovery
```

---

# Chapter 2565 — Runbook Structure

Each runbook shall contain:

```text
Symptoms
Likely causes
Impact
Checks
Safe recovery steps
When to escalate
Evidence to preserve
Relevant error codes
Relevant logs
Relevant dashboard pages
```

It shall not instruct administrators to disable security controls casually.

---

# Chapter 2566 — Simplified Version 1.0 Administration Scope

Version 1.0 shall implement:

```text
Role and permission enforcement
Administrative capability response
Dashboard
User search and detail
Enable and disable user
Role changes
Session listing and revocation
Active connection listing and disconnect
Tamper-evident audit log
Audit query and filtering
Recent and full audit verification
Security alerts
Alert acknowledgement and resolution
Worker status and manual execution
Configuration history and approved updates
Announcement administration
Audit exports
Detailed health checks
Backup-status reporting
Maintenance mode
Administrative diagnostics
Last SuperAdministrator protection
Bootstrap administration
```

The following may be deferred:

```text
External SIEM integration
External email or SMS alerts
Advanced behavioural analytics
Distributed tracing platform
Multi-person approval workflows
Hardware security module administration
Automatic cloud backup management
Remote shell access
Database query console
Plaintext content moderation
```

---

# Chapter 2567 — Administration Design Summary

BlueBubbles administration shall provide operational control without breaking end-to-end content privacy.

Every administrative action shall require:

* An active authenticated session.
* A named permission.
* Resource-level policy validation.
* A reason where the action is high impact.
* A durable audit event.

The dashboard shall show aggregated operational state rather than user content.

User administration shall support enabling, disabling and role changes while protecting the final SuperAdministrator.

Session revocation shall commit before active WebSockets are disconnected.

Connection disconnection shall remain distinct from session revocation.

Audit events shall be append-only, sequence-ordered and hash-linked.

Audit verification shall detect modification, deletion, reordering and chain-state mismatch.

Security alerts shall support deduplication, acknowledgement, resolution and automatic recovery where suitable.

Health monitoring shall distinguish liveness, readiness and degraded capabilities.

Configuration changes shall be validated, versioned and auditable.

Long-running exports and maintenance tasks shall use background jobs.

Administrative tools shall never expose passwords, tokens, private keys, message plaintext or attachment plaintext.

---

# End of Part 25

Part 26 will define the complete offline operation, synchronisation and conflict-resolution specification, including:

* Offline login limitations.
* Cached conversation access.
* Draft persistence.
* Offline message queues.
* Idempotent replay.
* Reconnection synchronisation.
* Event-gap recovery.
* Conversation-version conflicts.
* Message-edit conflicts.
* Membership changes while offline.
* Attachment-transfer recovery.
* Local cache reconciliation.
* Queue failure handling.
* Offline and recovery testing.


# Part 26 — Offline Operation, Synchronisation and Conflict Resolution

---

# Chapter 2568 — Offline Subsystem Purpose

The offline subsystem shall allow the BlueBubbles client to remain useful during temporary loss of server connectivity.

It shall support:

* Access to authorised cached conversations.
* Access to authorised cached messages.
* Draft creation and editing.
* Offline message preparation.
* Queued message submission.
* Queued conversation preference changes.
* Resumable attachment transfers.
* Reconnection detection.
* Missed-event recovery.
* Server-state reconciliation.
* Conflict detection.
* Safe replay.
* User-visible failure recovery.

Offline operation shall not bypass authentication, permissions, encryption or server authority.

---

# Chapter 2569 — Offline Design Principles

The offline subsystem shall follow these principles:

```text
The server remains authoritative.

Local pending state is clearly labelled.

Every replayable write is idempotent.

Permissions are refreshed before queued writes are replayed.

Membership changes take priority over stale local assumptions.

Drafts are never silently discarded.

Permanent failures require user attention.

Retryable failures remain recoverable.

Offline data is encrypted locally.

Queued actions are ordered where causality matters.

The client never fabricates server acknowledgements.
```

---

# Chapter 2570 — Offline Capability Categories

Client functionality shall be divided into:

```text
Available offline

Available with limitations

Unavailable offline
```

Available offline:

```text
Open authenticated local profile
View cached conversations
View cached messages
Write drafts
Search locally indexed messages
Review completed transfer history
Change purely local preferences
```

Available with limitations:

```text
Compose outgoing messages
Prepare attachments
Queue selected actions
Open previously downloaded files
```

Unavailable offline:

```text
New authentication against server
User directory search beyond cache
Server-side conversation creation
Role changes
Group membership changes
Session administration
Audit queries
Server health
Announcement acknowledgement submission
```

---

# Chapter 2571 — Offline Authentication Limitation

Version 1.0 shall not provide full server-independent login.

A user may access encrypted local data only when:

* A local authenticated profile already exists.
* The client possesses the required local storage key.
* The profile has not been locally cleared.
* Offline access policy permits it.
* The local profile is not marked revoked or disabled from the last known server state.

The client shall not validate an Active Directory password offline as though the server had authenticated it.

---

# Chapter 2572 — Offline Profile Unlock

Where offline profile unlocking is implemented, it shall be separate from server authentication.

Possible process:

```text
Open existing local profile

↓

Use operating-system secure store or local unlock mechanism

↓

Unlock local encrypted database

↓

Display cached data as offline

↓

Require server authentication before protected writes are accepted remotely
```

The interface shall label this clearly as offline access rather than successful server login.

---

# Chapter 2573 — Offline Access Policy

Server-controlled policy may define:

```text
Whether offline cached access is permitted
Maximum offline access duration
Whether drafts may be created offline
Whether messages may be queued offline
Whether attachments may be prepared offline
Whether local search is permitted
Whether sensitive previews are hidden offline
```

The client shall cache the last valid signed or authenticated policy response.

---

# Chapter 2574 — Maximum Offline Duration

A configurable policy may restrict offline access after:

```text
Last successful server authentication

or

Last successful policy refresh
```

Example:

```text
Maximum offline period:

7 days
```

After expiry:

```text
Local content remains encrypted.

The client requires reconnection and authentication before displaying it.
```

The timer shall use UTC and shall be resistant to obvious local clock rollback where practical.

---

# Chapter 2575 — Local Clock Limitations

The client cannot fully trust a user-controlled system clock.

Version 1.0 may store:

```text
Last trusted server timestamp
Local monotonic elapsed time where available
Last successful connection time
```

The client shall detect major backwards clock changes and may require reconnection.

It shall not claim strong tamper-proof offline expiry enforcement without trusted hardware.

---

# Chapter 2576 — Offline State Model

Client connectivity state shall include:

```text
STARTING
CONNECTING
CONNECTED
SYNCHRONISING
OFFLINE
DEGRADED
REAUTHENTICATION_REQUIRED
SHUTTING_DOWN
```

State transitions shall be explicit and tested.

---

# Chapter 2577 — Connectivity State Transitions

Typical transitions:

```text
STARTING → CONNECTING

CONNECTING → CONNECTED

CONNECTING → OFFLINE

CONNECTED → SYNCHRONISING

SYNCHRONISING → CONNECTED

CONNECTED → OFFLINE

OFFLINE → CONNECTING

OFFLINE → REAUTHENTICATION_REQUIRED

CONNECTED → DEGRADED

DEGRADED → CONNECTED
```

Invalid transitions shall be rejected or normalised by one connectivity controller.

---

# Chapter 2578 — ConnectivityMonitor Responsibilities

The `ConnectivityMonitor` shall:

* Track current connection state.
* Observe REST and WebSocket failures.
* Distinguish temporary loss from authentication failure.
* Avoid excessive probing.
* Trigger reconnect attempts.
* Notify synchronisation services.
* Expose state to ViewModels.
* Stop reconnecting after explicit logout.

It shall not process offline actions directly.

---

# Chapter 2579 — Connectivity Evidence

The client may consider:

```text
WebSocket state
Recent successful API request
Dedicated health request
DNS resolution result
TLS connection result
Authentication result
```

A failed public Internet check shall not determine BlueBubbles LAN availability.

Only the configured server matters.

---

# Chapter 2580 — Connectivity Failure Classification

Possible categories:

```text
DNS_FAILURE
TLS_FAILURE
CONNECTION_REFUSED
CONNECTION_TIMEOUT
SERVER_MAINTENANCE
SERVER_UNAVAILABLE
AUTHENTICATION_EXPIRED
PROTOCOL_UNSUPPORTED
LOCAL_NETWORK_UNAVAILABLE
```

Classification determines whether to:

* Retry.
* Require user action.
* Stop reconnecting.
* Open diagnostics.
* Return to login.

---

# Chapter 2581 — Cached Conversation Access

While offline, the client may display locally cached:

```text
Conversation summaries
Participants
Membership role
Unread state
Cached message history
Drafts
Downloaded attachment metadata
Local transfer state
```

Every offline page shall indicate that data may be stale.

---

# Chapter 2582 — Stale Data Presentation

The client shall avoid presenting cached state as current.

Examples:

```text
Last synchronised 18 minutes ago
```

```text
Offline — membership changes may not be shown
```

Presence and typing indicators shall be hidden or marked unavailable offline.

---

# Chapter 2583 — Cached Conversation Restrictions

While offline, the client shall not assume that:

* The user remains a group member.
* Another user remains enabled.
* Public keys remain active.
* The conversation still exists.
* Attachment access remains valid.
* Message editing remains within the permitted window.
* The user’s role remains unchanged.

Queued actions shall therefore be revalidated after reconnection.

---

# Chapter 2584 — Offline Draft Persistence

Drafts shall be saved locally and encrypted.

Draft fields:

```text
Draft ID
User ID
Conversation ID
Text
Reply target
Edit target where applicable
Prepared attachment references
Created timestamp
Updated timestamp
Draft format version
```

Draft saving shall occur after a short debounce and during graceful shutdown.

---

# Chapter 2585 — Draft Save Frequency

Recommended debounce:

```text
300 to 750 milliseconds
```

Immediate save shall also occur when:

* Changing conversation.
* Closing the chat page.
* Minimising or closing the application.
* Beginning logout.
* Entering offline mode.
* Adding or removing a prepared attachment.
* Changing reply or edit target.

---

# Chapter 2586 — Draft Encryption

Draft plaintext shall be encrypted using:

```text
Local DRAFT_STORAGE key
AES-256-GCM
Fresh nonce
Conversation-specific AAD
```

AAD shall include:

```text
User ID
Conversation ID
Draft ID
Draft version
```

Draft plaintext shall not appear in ordinary SQLite columns.

---

# Chapter 2587 — Draft Conflict Model

Drafts are local-authoritative until a successful send or explicit deletion.

A remote server does not modify the local draft directly.

Possible conflict:

```text
Same local profile opened in two client processes
```

Version 1.0 shall prevent this using a single-instance profile lock.

---

# Chapter 2588 — Single-Instance Profile Lock

The client shall prevent two processes from writing the same local profile concurrently.

The lock shall:

* Be profile-specific.
* Be acquired before opening the writable local database.
* Be released during shutdown.
* Detect stale locks where possible.
* Refuse unsafe concurrent opening.

The second process may show:

```text
BlueBubbles is already open for this profile.
```

---

# Chapter 2589 — Offline Action Queue Purpose

The offline action queue stores writes that cannot yet be submitted.

Suitable queued actions:

```text
Send encrypted message
Edit a locally pending message
Cancel a locally pending message
Mark conversation read
Update mute preference
Update pin preference
Acknowledge announcement
Resume attachment upload
```

Actions with high conflict or administrative risk should not be queued by default.

---

# Chapter 2590 — Non-Queueable Actions

Version 1.0 shall not queue:

```text
Create group
Add or remove group member
Transfer ownership
Change role
Disable user
Revoke another session
Publish announcement
Modify server configuration
Delete server-confirmed message administratively
```

These actions require current server state and immediate authoritative validation.

---

# Chapter 2591 — OfflineAction Model

```python
class OfflineAction:
    id: UUID
    user_id: UUID
    action_type: OfflineActionType
    scope_type: str
    scope_id: UUID | None
    sequence_number: int
    idempotency_key: str
    encrypted_payload: bytes
    payload_nonce: bytes
    payload_authentication_tag: bytes
    created_at: datetime
    updated_at: datetime
    next_attempt_at: datetime | None
    attempt_count: int
    state: OfflineActionState
    failure_code: str | None
    dependency_action_id: UUID | None
```

---

# Chapter 2592 — Offline Action States

```text
PENDING
READY
PROCESSING
RETRY_WAIT
BLOCKED
COMPLETED
FAILED_PERMANENT
CANCELLED
```

Meaning:

```text
PENDING

Created but not yet ready.

READY

Eligible for processing.

PROCESSING

Currently being submitted.

RETRY_WAIT

Waiting for retry delay.

BLOCKED

Waiting on another action or user decision.

COMPLETED

Server accepted or equivalent state confirmed.

FAILED_PERMANENT

Cannot succeed without changed input or permissions.

CANCELLED

Removed by user before completion.
```

---

# Chapter 2593 — Offline Action State Transitions

Allowed transitions:

```text
PENDING → READY
PENDING → CANCELLED

READY → PROCESSING
READY → CANCELLED

PROCESSING → COMPLETED
PROCESSING → RETRY_WAIT
PROCESSING → BLOCKED
PROCESSING → FAILED_PERMANENT

RETRY_WAIT → READY
RETRY_WAIT → CANCELLED

BLOCKED → READY
BLOCKED → FAILED_PERMANENT
BLOCKED → CANCELLED
```

Terminal states shall not return to processing automatically.

---

# Chapter 2594 — Queue Sequence Numbers

Each queued action shall receive a local monotonic sequence number.

Purpose:

* Preserve creation order.
* Support deterministic replay.
* Support diagnostics.
* Avoid relying only on timestamps.

Sequence values need only be unique within the local user profile.

---

# Chapter 2595 — Idempotency Key

Every replayable write shall include a stable idempotency key.

Examples:

```text
Message send:

Message UUID

Read update:

User ID + conversation ID + target message ID

Preference update:

Action UUID

Announcement acknowledgement:

Announcement ID + user ID
```

The key shall remain unchanged across retries.

---

# Chapter 2596 — Idempotent Message Submission

A queued outgoing message shall use a client-generated message UUID.

On retry, the same:

```text
Message UUID
Ciphertext
Recipient envelopes
Signature
Attachment IDs
```

shall be submitted unless the action is deliberately rebuilt after a membership or key conflict.

The server shall compare duplicate submissions safely.

---

# Chapter 2597 — Duplicate Message Handling

If the server already contains the message ID for the same sender:

```text
If encrypted payload and metadata match:

Return existing acknowledgement.

If they differ:

Return conflict.
```

The client shall never treat a conflicting duplicate as success.

---

# Chapter 2598 — Rebuilding a Queued Message

A message may require rebuilding when:

* Conversation membership changed.
* Recipient public keys changed.
* A recipient key was revoked.
* The original attachment preparation expired.
* Protocol requirements changed.

The rebuilt message shall normally use:

```text
A new message UUID
A new content key
A new nonce
New recipient envelopes
A new signature
```

The original queued action shall be marked superseded or cancelled.

---

# Chapter 2599 — Queue Payload Encryption

Offline action payloads shall be encrypted with the local `OFFLINE_QUEUE` key.

AAD shall include:

```text
User ID
Action ID
Action type
Sequence number
Payload format version
```

The queue table may store non-sensitive scheduling metadata in plaintext.

---

# Chapter 2600 — Queue Payload Integrity Failure

If local queue decryption fails:

* Do not submit the action.
* Mark it blocked or permanently failed.
* Preserve safe metadata.
* Notify the user.
* Offer deletion.
* Log a local integrity error.
* Avoid displaying partially decoded content.

For a message action, the original draft may still be recoverable separately.

---

# Chapter 2601 — Queue Ordering Scope

Ordering shall be preserved within causally related scopes.

Examples:

```text
Messages in one conversation
Preference changes for one conversation
Attachment completion before message send
Edit after original message send
```

Actions in unrelated conversations may later be processed concurrently.

Version 1.0 may process the entire queue serially for simpler correctness.

---

# Chapter 2602 — Action Dependencies

A queued action may depend on another action.

Examples:

```text
Send message depends on attachment upload completion.

Edit pending message depends on original message send.

Announcement acknowledgement depends on refreshed announcement state.
```

The dependency shall be represented explicitly rather than inferred only from ordering.

---

# Chapter 2603 — Dependency Failure

If a dependency permanently fails:

```text
Dependent action → BLOCKED
```

The client shall offer actions such as:

```text
Remove failed attachment and send text only
Retry attachment
Edit message
Cancel message
```

The dependent action shall not be submitted with missing required data.

---

# Chapter 2604 — Offline Message Creation

While offline:

```text
User writes message

↓

Client validates local format

↓

Client loads cached conversation state

↓

Client creates pending message UUID

↓

Client preserves plaintext draft securely

↓

Client may encrypt immediately using cached keys

↓

Client stores pending message and queue action

↓

UI displays Waiting for connection
```

Immediate encryption is allowed only when cached membership and keys are available.

---

# Chapter 2605 — Deferred Encryption

If current recipient keys are unavailable, the client may store:

```text
Encrypted local plaintext draft
Pending unencrypted-for-transport action
```

After reconnection:

```text
Refresh membership and keys

↓

Encrypt message

↓

Submit
```

The queue shall clearly distinguish locally encrypted draft content from completed transport ciphertext.

---

# Chapter 2606 — Pre-Encrypted Offline Message Risk

A message encrypted offline using cached membership may become invalid if membership changes before reconnection.

Therefore, before submission, the client shall:

* Refresh conversation membership.
* Refresh active recipient public keys.
* Compare recipient set.
* Compare required key versions.
* Rebuild the encrypted envelope if necessary.

The server shall still perform the final check.

---

# Chapter 2607 — Offline Message User Experience

Pending message label:

```text
Waiting for connection
```

Possible actions:

```text
Edit
Cancel
Copy
View queue status
```

The user shall not see a delivered or stored indicator.

---

# Chapter 2608 — Editing an Offline Pending Message

If a message has not been acknowledged by the server:

* The user may edit its local plaintext source.
* The existing transport envelope shall be discarded.
* A new content key and nonce shall be generated.
* The same message UUID may be retained if no submission was accepted.
* Queue payload and local pending display shall be updated atomically.

---

# Chapter 2609 — Cancelling an Offline Pending Message

Cancellation shall:

* Mark the queue action cancelled.
* Remove the pending chat item or show a local cancelled placeholder.
* Remove associated transport ciphertext.
* Preserve the draft only if the user chooses.
* Cancel dependent attachment uploads where selected.
* Avoid sending the action after reconnect.

---

# Chapter 2610 — Server-Acknowledged Message Boundary

After the server returns a valid stored acknowledgement:

* The message is no longer purely local.
* Local cancellation cannot erase the server record.
* Further changes use edit or delete APIs.
* The queue action becomes completed.
* The draft is removed.
* The pending UI state becomes stored.

This transition shall be committed locally atomically.

---

# Chapter 2611 — Read-State Queueing

A read update may be queued offline.

The action shall contain:

```text
Conversation ID
Highest locally read message ID
Server ordering metadata
Local timestamp
```

On replay, the server shall advance the read position only if the submitted message is valid for the user.

---

# Chapter 2612 — Monotonic Read Position

Read positions shall never move backwards unintentionally.

If multiple queued read actions exist for one conversation:

```text
Keep only the furthest valid read position
```

Older queued read updates may be coalesced.

---

# Chapter 2613 — Preference Queueing

Conversation preferences suitable for queueing:

```text
Mute
Unmute
Pin
Unpin
Archive
Unarchive
Notification level
```

The latest local value may supersede earlier pending updates for the same field.

---

# Chapter 2614 — Preference Coalescing

Example queue:

```text
Mute conversation
Unmute conversation
Mute conversation
```

Before submission, this may become:

```text
Set muted = true
```

Coalescing shall preserve the final user intent and reduce unnecessary requests.

---

# Chapter 2615 — Announcement Acknowledgement Queueing

An acknowledgement may be queued when offline.

Before replay, verify:

* Announcement still exists.
* Acknowledgement is still required.
* User remains a target.
* Announcement has not been withdrawn.
* Server has not already recorded acknowledgement.

The action shall be idempotent.

---

# Chapter 2616 — Attachment Preparation Offline

The client may prepare an attachment offline by:

* Reading the source file.
* Calculating plaintext hash.
* Encrypting chunks.
* Creating encrypted metadata.
* Persisting a local transfer manifest.

Recipient file-key envelopes should be finalised only after current membership and public keys are refreshed.

---

# Chapter 2617 — Offline Prepared Attachment Storage

Offline encrypted chunks shall be stored in a managed temporary directory.

Metadata shall include:

```text
Transfer ID
Attachment ID
Source-file identity
Encrypted chunk paths
Chunk hashes
File size
Preparation timestamp
Conversation ID
Master-key protection reference
```

The attachment master key shall remain protected locally.

---

# Chapter 2618 — Source File Change Detection

Before uploading a prepared attachment, the client shall determine whether the original source file changed after preparation.

Possible checks:

```text
File size
Modification timestamp
Stored plaintext hash where recomputation is acceptable
File identity metadata
```

If changed:

```text
Require re-preparation.
```

The client shall not upload ciphertext that no longer matches the selected source intent without informing the user.

---

# Chapter 2619 — Prepared Attachment Expiry

Prepared offline attachments may expire locally after a configurable period.

Example:

```text
7 days
```

Before cleanup:

* Ensure no active draft or queue action references the attachment.
* Warn the user where unsent work would be affected.
* Preserve encrypted draft text.
* Never silently remove an attachment from an outgoing message.

---

# Chapter 2620 — Reconnection Process Overview

On connectivity restoration:

```text
Establish TLS connection

↓

Validate server and protocol

↓

Refresh access token if needed

↓

Open WebSocket

↓

Refresh capabilities and policy

↓

Refresh current user and permissions

↓

Synchronise conversation and membership state

↓

Synchronise keys

↓

Recover missed messages and events

↓

Reconcile local cache

↓

Process offline queue

↓

Resume attachment transfers

↓

Mark connected
```

Queue processing shall not begin before authoritative security state is refreshed.

---

# Chapter 2621 — Reauthentication During Reconnection

If token refresh fails because the session expired or was revoked:

```text
Stop queue processing.

Preserve encrypted queued actions.

Preserve drafts.

Return to login.

Require successful authentication.
```

After the same user signs in again, eligible queued work may resume.

If a different user signs in, the previous user’s queue shall remain isolated.

---

# Chapter 2622 — User Identity Mismatch

If the authenticated server user does not match the local profile owning the queue:

* Do not open that queue.
* Do not decrypt its drafts.
* Do not submit its actions.
* Offer switching back to the correct profile.
* Keep data encrypted and isolated.

---

# Chapter 2623 — SynchronisationService Purpose

The `SynchronisationService` shall reconcile:

```text
Server authoritative state

with

Local cached and pending state
```

It shall coordinate:

* Scope refresh.
* Event recovery.
* Message retrieval.
* Membership changes.
* Key changes.
* Policy changes.
* Queue readiness.
* Local deletion and tombstones.

---

# Chapter 2624 — Synchronisation Scope Types

Suggested scopes:

```text
CURRENT_USER
POLICIES
CONVERSATIONS
CONVERSATION_MEMBERSHIP
MESSAGES
PUBLIC_KEYS
ANNOUNCEMENTS
SESSIONS
ADMIN_CAPABILITIES
TRANSFERS
```

A full sync may combine all scopes.

A targeted sync may refresh only one conversation.

---

# Chapter 2625 — Synchronisation State Record

```python
class SynchronisationState:
    scope: str
    scope_id: UUID | None
    last_successful_sync_at: datetime | None
    server_cursor: str | None
    last_event_id: UUID | None
    last_server_version: int | None
    failure_count: int
    state: str
```

This record shall not contain plaintext message content.

---

# Chapter 2626 — Initial Synchronisation

After first successful login on a device:

```text
Fetch policy and capabilities

↓

Create or unlock local profile

↓

Fetch current user

↓

Fetch conversation summaries

↓

Fetch active memberships

↓

Fetch required public keys

↓

Fetch latest authorised message pages

↓

Fetch announcements

↓

Build local search index where allowed

↓

Open main interface
```

The client may show the interface after essential state loads while lower-priority sync continues.

---

# Chapter 2627 — Reconnection Synchronisation

A reconnection sync shall prefer incremental recovery.

Inputs:

```text
Last processed event ID
Last successful sync timestamp
Per-scope cursors
Local conversation versions
```

If incremental recovery is unavailable or invalid:

```text
Perform targeted or full resynchronisation.
```

---

# Chapter 2628 — WebSocket Event Identifier

Every durable WebSocket event should include:

```text
event_id
created_at
event_type
aggregate_id
aggregate_version where relevant
```

The client stores the last processed durable event ID.

Transient events such as typing may not require durable replay.

---

# Chapter 2629 — Event Replay API

Suggested endpoint:

```text
GET /api/v1/events
```

Query:

```text
after_event_id
limit
```

The server may return durable events retained for a bounded period.

If the requested event is too old:

```text
EVENT_CURSOR_EXPIRED
```

The client shall perform broader resynchronisation.

---

# Chapter 2630 — Durable Versus Transient Events

Durable events:

```text
Message stored
Message edited
Message deleted
Group membership changed
Conversation changed
Session revoked
Policy changed
Announcement published
Key changed
```

Transient events:

```text
Typing started
Typing stopped
Short-lived presence heartbeat
Temporary connection count
```

Only durable events require gap recovery.

---

# Chapter 2631 — Event Gap Detection

A gap may be detected when:

* Event cursor is rejected.
* Event sequence is non-contiguous where sequencing exists.
* Local aggregate version jumps unexpectedly.
* WebSocket reconnect reports missed events.
* A referenced message or conversation is absent.
* Local state checksum or count is inconsistent.

The client shall not ignore detected gaps silently.

---

# Chapter 2632 — Event Gap Recovery

```text
Pause normal event application for affected scope

↓

Mark scope stale

↓

Fetch authoritative snapshot or delta

↓

Apply transactionally

↓

Update cursor and versions

↓

Resume event handling

↓

Process buffered later events
```

Buffered events shall be bounded to avoid unbounded memory growth.

---

# Chapter 2633 — Event Buffer

During targeted resynchronisation, incoming durable events for the affected scope may be buffered.

Buffer rules:

* Maximum item count.
* Maximum age.
* Deduplication by event ID.
* Ordered application.
* Full resync if bounds exceeded.

Transient events may be discarded.

---

# Chapter 2634 — Event Deduplication

The client shall maintain recently processed event IDs.

On duplicate:

```text
Do not apply the business change twice.
```

Idempotent event handlers shall also check aggregate versions or record existence.

---

# Chapter 2635 — Aggregate Versioning

Mutable server records should include versions.

Examples:

```text
Conversation version
Membership version
Message version
User version
Configuration version
Announcement version
```

The client stores the latest applied version.

---

# Chapter 2636 — Version Application Rules

When receiving versioned state:

```text
Incoming version < local version:

Ignore as stale.

Incoming version = local version:

Treat as duplicate or verify equivalence.

Incoming version = local version + 1:

Apply normally.

Incoming version > local version + 1:

Detected gap; resynchronise scope.
```

Not every append-only record requires aggregate versioning.

---

# Chapter 2637 — Conversation Summary Reconciliation

For each server conversation summary:

```text
Insert if absent.

Update server-controlled fields.

Preserve local-only preference state only where not server-synchronised.

Merge unread and last-activity state.

Apply deleted or archived status.

Update participant cache.
```

Local pending messages shall not be removed merely because they are absent from the server snapshot.

---

# Chapter 2638 — Missing Conversation Reconciliation

If a locally cached conversation is absent from an authoritative server list:

Possible causes:

* User removed.
* Conversation deleted.
* Pagination incomplete.
* Server filtering.
* Sync defect.

The client shall confirm through targeted lookup before deleting local state.

---

# Chapter 2639 — Conversation Tombstone

When server state confirms the conversation is unavailable:

```text
Create local tombstone
Disable composer
Preserve permitted historical cache according to policy
Stop queued writes
Mark related actions blocked or failed
Hide from active list
```

A tombstone prevents stale events from recreating deleted state incorrectly.

---

# Chapter 2640 — Membership Reconciliation

Membership state shall be refreshed before:

* Replaying messages.
* Replaying attachments.
* Editing messages.
* Loading new group history.
* Generating recipient envelopes.

The client shall record:

```text
Joined at
Removed at
Role
Membership version
```

---

# Chapter 2641 — User Removed While Offline

If the user was removed from a group while offline:

```text
Disable composer.

Stop pending group sends.

Stop attachment uploads.

Mark actions BLOCKED.

Display removal notice.

Refresh visible history according to policy.
```

The client shall not submit messages using stale membership.

---

# Chapter 2642 — Pending Message After Removal

The user shall be offered:

```text
Copy message text
Save as local draft
Delete pending message
```

The message shall not be sent to a different conversation automatically.

Any prepared attachments remain local until removed or reused deliberately.

---

# Chapter 2643 — User Added While Offline

If the user was added while disconnected:

* Add conversation after synchronisation.
* Retrieve only authorised history according to membership policy.
* Retrieve current public keys.
* Display membership event.
* Do not fabricate access to earlier encrypted messages.

---

# Chapter 2644 — Role Changed While Offline

If the user’s group role changed:

* Refresh allowed controls.
* Revalidate queued group actions.
* Remove moderation controls after demotion.
* Expose new controls after promotion.
* Require server validation for every action regardless of UI state.

---

# Chapter 2645 — Public Key Reconciliation

The client shall refresh public keys before queue replay.

For each cached key:

```text
Check primary state
Check version
Check revocation
Check replacement
Check fingerprint
```

Unexpected key changes shall trigger the configured warning process.

---

# Chapter 2646 — Key Cache Invalidation

Key cache shall be invalidated on:

* Key-change event.
* Key-revocation event.
* Protocol change.
* Signature verification failure.
* Recipient-envelope conflict.
* Cache expiry.
* Manual key refresh.

A failed verification shall not automatically accept newly returned key material without change validation.

---

# Chapter 2647 — Message Synchronisation

Message synchronisation shall:

* Retrieve authorised encrypted messages.
* Retrieve only the current user’s key envelope.
* Deduplicate by message ID.
* Apply version updates.
* Apply deletion state.
* Preserve local pending items.
* Decrypt and cache where permitted.
* Update local search index.
* Update conversation summary.

---

# Chapter 2648 — Pending and Server Message Merge

When the server returns a message with the same client-generated ID as a local pending message:

```text
Verify sender matches.

Verify encrypted payload or accepted acknowledgement matches.

Replace pending state with server state.

Preserve local display plaintext cache.

Complete queue action.

Remove draft.
```

A mismatch shall become a conflict.

---

# Chapter 2649 — Message Version Conflict

An edit may fail because:

```text
Expected version differs from current server version.
```

The server returns:

```text
Current version
Current encrypted message
Conflict code
```

The client shall not overwrite automatically.

---

# Chapter 2650 — Editing Conflict User Experience

The client shall display:

```text
This message changed before your edit was saved.
```

Options:

```text
View current message
Copy your edited text
Replace your draft with current version
Retry edit after review
Cancel
```

The client shall preserve the user’s attempted edit securely.

---

# Chapter 2651 — Pending Edit While Offline

A server-confirmed message may be edited locally while offline only if:

* Policy allows offline edit queueing.
* The client has the current version.
* The edit window appears locally valid.

Before replay:

* Refresh message state.
* Refresh server time relationship.
* Confirm edit permission.
* Confirm expected version.
* Re-encrypt using current recipients.

---

# Chapter 2652 — Edit Window Expiry

If the edit window expired while offline:

```text
Mark action FAILED_PERMANENT.
```

Preserve attempted text as a local draft.

Display:

```text
This message can no longer be edited.

Your revised text has been saved as a draft.
```

---

# Chapter 2653 — Delete Conflict

A queued delete may encounter:

```text
Message already deleted
```

This may be treated as idempotent success if the authoritative deleted state matches the intended outcome.

If the message was replaced or requester lost permission:

* Return conflict or permission failure.
* Refresh state.
* Do not claim the local request performed the deletion.

---

# Chapter 2654 — Server Deletion While Offline

After reconnection, a deleted message shall:

* Stop displaying cached plaintext as active content.
* Show the appropriate deletion placeholder.
* Remove it from search index.
* Remove or invalidate related local preview.
* Preserve encrypted historical data only if policy requires it.

---

# Chapter 2655 — Local Cache Reconciliation Transaction

For one synchronisation page:

```text
Begin local transaction

↓

Insert or update conversations

↓

Insert or update encrypted messages

↓

Update membership

↓

Update tombstones

↓

Update sync cursor

↓

Commit
```

User-interface events shall be emitted only after commit.

---

# Chapter 2656 — Partial Synchronisation Failure

If a page fails:

* Roll back that local transaction.
* Keep the previous sync cursor.
* Preserve already committed earlier pages.
* Retry from the last successful cursor.
* Avoid applying only half of one page.

---

# Chapter 2657 — Synchronisation Cursor Safety

The client shall advance a cursor only after:

```text
All records in the page were validated and committed locally.
```

Storing the cursor before committing records could permanently skip data after a crash.

---

# Chapter 2658 — Synchronisation Checkpoint

A checkpoint may include:

```text
Scope
Cursor
Last event ID
Aggregate version
Committed timestamp
Page checksum where useful
```

Checkpoints shall be updated atomically with the corresponding local data.

---

# Chapter 2659 — Full Resynchronisation

A full resync may be required when:

* Local database integrity check fails.
* Event cursor expired.
* Protocol changed significantly.
* Aggregate versions are inconsistent.
* Key cache is corrupted.
* User requests cache rebuild.
* Migration invalidated old read models.

---

# Chapter 2660 — Full Resynchronisation Safety

A full resync shall not immediately delete:

* Drafts.
* Pending messages.
* Offline queue.
* Prepared attachments.
* User preferences.

It shall rebuild server-derived cache while preserving local unsent work.

---

# Chapter 2661 — Shadow Cache Rebuild

Safer full resync process:

```text
Create temporary replacement cache tables or database

↓

Download and validate authoritative state

↓

Reconcile local pending records

↓

Validate replacement

↓

Atomically switch active cache

↓

Remove old cache after success
```

Version 1.0 may use table-level transactions if the dataset is manageable.

---

# Chapter 2662 — Cache Record Origins

Local records should identify origin:

```text
SERVER_CONFIRMED
LOCAL_PENDING
LOCAL_DRAFT
SERVER_TOMBSTONE
DERIVED_INDEX
```

This helps prevent full resync from removing unsent local state.

---

# Chapter 2663 — Conflict Categories

Conflict categories:

```text
VERSION_CONFLICT
MEMBERSHIP_CONFLICT
PERMISSION_CONFLICT
KEY_CONFLICT
DELETION_CONFLICT
ATTACHMENT_CONFLICT
PROTOCOL_CONFLICT
DUPLICATE_ID_CONFLICT
POLICY_CONFLICT
```

Each category shall have a defined recovery path.

---

# Chapter 2664 — Retryable Failures

Examples:

```text
Temporary network loss
Server 503
Maintenance mode
Redis-related transient failure
Request timeout with idempotency key
Temporary database contention
Rate limit with retry-after
```

The action shall enter:

```text
RETRY_WAIT
```

with bounded backoff.

---

# Chapter 2665 — Permanent Failures

Examples:

```text
User removed from conversation
Account disabled
Unsupported protocol
Message exceeds new policy limit
Edit window expired
Attachment extension blocked
Recipient key permanently unavailable
Conversation deleted
Invalid signature generated from corrupted local state
```

The action shall not retry automatically without user or application change.

---

# Chapter 2666 — Queue Failure Classification

The `OfflineActionExecutor` shall return:

```python
class OfflineActionExecutionResult:
    outcome: OfflineActionOutcome
    server_reference: UUID | None
    failure_code: str | None
    retry_after: datetime | None
    replacement_required: bool
    user_action_required: bool
```

The queue service shall not infer retryability only from text messages.

---

# Chapter 2667 — Retry Backoff

Suggested retry schedule:

```text
1 second
2 seconds
5 seconds
10 seconds
30 seconds
1 minute
5 minutes
```

Maximum delay shall be configurable.

Apply jitter to avoid many clients retrying simultaneously.

---

# Chapter 2668 — Retry Limits

A retryable action shall not necessarily become permanent merely because a fixed count was exceeded.

Instead:

* Slow the retry interval.
* Mark it as requiring attention.
* Allow manual retry.
* Continue after future reconnection.

Security-sensitive failures may stop immediately.

---

# Chapter 2669 — Queue Processing Trigger

Queue processing may begin after:

* Successful reconnection sync.
* Successful reauthentication.
* Manual `Retry now`.
* Policy refresh.
* Key refresh.
* Attachment dependency completion.

Only one processor shall run per user profile.

---

# Chapter 2670 — Queue Processing Order

Recommended order:

```text
1. Refresh policy and permissions

2. Refresh conversation membership

3. Refresh public keys

4. Resume required attachment uploads

5. Submit pending messages

6. Submit message edits or deletes

7. Submit read positions

8. Submit preference updates

9. Submit announcement acknowledgements
```

Dependencies may alter the exact order.

---

# Chapter 2671 — Queue Processing Loop

```text
Acquire processing lock

↓

Load next eligible action

↓

Decrypt and validate payload

↓

Refresh affected state if required

↓

Mark PROCESSING

↓

Execute with idempotency key

↓

Classify result

↓

Persist resulting state

↓

Update UI

↓

Continue
```

The action state shall not remain `PROCESSING` permanently after a crash.

---

# Chapter 2672 — Interrupted Processing Recovery

On client startup:

```text
Actions left PROCESSING are reset to READY or RETRY_WAIT
```

provided no local completion record exists.

The server’s idempotency handling protects against duplicate submission.

---

# Chapter 2673 — Queue Crash Consistency

For a successful server response, local completion shall be written atomically with:

* Server reference.
* Updated local message state.
* Queue completion state.
* Draft removal.
* Attachment link state.

If the client crashes before local commit, replay shall retrieve the existing server result idempotently.

---

# Chapter 2674 — Manual Queue Management

The user may view:

```text
Pending
Waiting for connection
Retrying
Blocked
Failed
Completed recently
```

Actions:

```text
Retry now
Edit
Cancel
Copy content
View error
Remove completed history
```

Sensitive queue payloads shall not be shown in diagnostic raw form.

---

# Chapter 2675 — Queue Error Presentation

Example:

```text
Message could not be sent.

You are no longer a member of this conversation.
```

Actions:

```text
Copy message
Save as draft
Delete
```

Example retryable:

```text
Message is waiting for the server to become available.
```

---

# Chapter 2676 — Attachment Upload Recovery

After reconnection:

```text
Query server upload session

↓

Compare local encrypted chunk manifest

↓

Identify missing chunks

↓

Verify existing server chunk hashes

↓

Upload missing chunks

↓

Complete upload

↓

Update dependent message action
```

The client shall not re-encrypt unchanged chunks unnecessarily.

---

# Chapter 2677 — Expired Upload Session

If the server upload session expired:

```text
Create new upload session

↓

Reuse local encrypted chunks only if protocol and recipient state remain valid

or

Rebuild attachment envelopes and manifest
```

If membership changed, recipient file-key envelopes shall be rebuilt before completion.

---

# Chapter 2678 — Attachment Membership Conflict

If a group member was added or removed after offline preparation:

* The attachment recipient envelope set is stale.
* The client shall refresh active members.
* It shall create a new signed manifest.
* It shall add or remove file-key envelopes.
* Existing encrypted chunks may remain valid because the file key and chunk ciphertext need not change.
* The server upload session may need restarting.

---

# Chapter 2679 — Attachment Source Missing

If the original file is missing but encrypted prepared chunks remain intact:

* The upload may continue from encrypted chunks.
* The client shall preserve the encrypted manifest and master-key access.
* The completed recipient can still decrypt the original prepared content.

If prepared chunks are incomplete, the action shall fail and require reselecting the source.

---

# Chapter 2680 — Download Recovery

For interrupted downloads:

* Preserve encrypted downloaded chunks.
* Preserve verified chunk hashes.
* Preserve partial plaintext only according to policy.
* Query authoritative attachment state.
* Resume missing encrypted chunks.
* Re-verify before final rename.

---

# Chapter 2681 — Partial Plaintext Recovery Policy

Preferred Version 1.0 behaviour:

```text
Preserve encrypted downloaded chunks.

Do not preserve long-lived partial plaintext after application shutdown.
```

On resume:

```text
Decrypt again from locally stored encrypted chunks.
```

This reduces plaintext-at-rest exposure.

---

# Chapter 2682 — Download Revocation During Offline Period

If attachment access is no longer authorised after reconnect:

* Stop download.
* Remove unverified partial plaintext.
* Mark transfer failed permanently.
* Retain encrypted cache only according to policy.
* Display an access-revoked message.

Previously completed plaintext files outside managed storage cannot be remotely revoked.

---

# Chapter 2683 — Announcement Reconciliation

On reconnect:

* Retrieve active announcements.
* Apply withdrawals.
* Retrieve acknowledgement state.
* Submit queued acknowledgements.
* Remove expired banners.
* Preserve required acknowledgement notices.

---

# Chapter 2684 — Policy Conflict

A queued action may violate a newer policy.

Examples:

```text
Message now exceeds maximum length.
File type is newly blocked.
Attachment maximum size was reduced.
Offline queueing was disabled.
Group size policy changed.
```

The client shall not ignore the new policy.

---

# Chapter 2685 — Policy Conflict Recovery

Possible actions:

```text
Edit message to fit limit
Remove blocked attachment
Save content as local draft
Cancel queued action
Contact administrator
```

The original user content shall remain encrypted locally until resolved or deleted.

---

# Chapter 2686 — Protocol Change During Offline Period

If the server no longer supports the queued action’s protocol:

```text
Attempt migration only through a defined converter.

Otherwise mark replacement required.
```

The client shall not submit an obsolete cryptographic envelope blindly.

---

# Chapter 2687 — Queued Message Protocol Migration

A safe queued-message migration requires:

* Decrypting the local source draft.
* Loading current recipients.
* Generating new content key.
* Re-encrypting under supported format.
* Re-signing.
* Replacing old queued transport data.
* Preserving user-visible text.

Ciphertext shall not be mechanically rewritten without decryption and re-encryption.

---

# Chapter 2688 — Server Time Reconciliation

The client shall obtain a trusted server timestamp during sync.

It may use this to:

* Re-evaluate edit windows.
* Re-evaluate announcement expiry.
* Re-evaluate session policy.
* Detect large client clock drift.
* Display accurate relative times.

Server time shall not be used to alter previously signed client timestamps.

---

# Chapter 2689 — Local Notification Reconciliation

After reconnect, the client shall avoid flooding the user with notifications for every old missed message.

Suggested policy:

```text
Notify summary:

12 new messages in 4 conversations
```

The client may notify individual recent high-priority events.

---

# Chapter 2690 — Missed Message Notification Summary

Summary may include:

```text
Total new messages
Number of conversations
Urgent announcement count
Failed queued action count
```

It shall respect privacy-preview settings.

---

# Chapter 2691 — Presence Reconciliation

Presence is transient.

After reconnect:

* Discard stale local presence values.
* Request or receive fresh presence summaries.
* Restart heartbeat.
* Do not replay old typing indicators.
* Mark unknown users offline or unknown until refreshed.

---

# Chapter 2692 — Session Revocation While Offline

If the session was revoked during disconnection:

* Token refresh or WebSocket authentication shall fail.
* The client shall stop queue replay.
* Preserve encrypted local data.
* Return to login.
* Display revocation message where safe.
* Require new authentication.

Queued actions may resume only after successful login and revalidation.

---

# Chapter 2693 — Account Disable While Offline

After detecting account disable:

* Clear active tokens.
* Lock private-key store.
* Close local authorised view.
* Preserve encrypted local profile.
* Do not process queue.
* Display helpdesk guidance.

Offline cached access may be revoked immediately once the client learns the state.

---

# Chapter 2694 — Role Change While Offline

After global role change:

* Refresh administrative capabilities.
* Dispose unauthorised admin pages.
* Clear cached administrative data.
* Revalidate ordinary actions.
* Possibly require reauthentication.
* Do not infer group roles from global application role.

---

# Chapter 2695 — Synchronisation Error Isolation

A failure in one scope shall not necessarily block all scopes.

Example:

```text
Announcement sync fails.

Messaging sync succeeds.
```

The client may enter a degraded state and retry the failed scope separately.

Critical failures such as user or membership refresh failure shall block queue processing.

---

# Chapter 2696 — Critical Synchronisation Scopes

Queue processing requires success for:

```text
CURRENT_USER
POLICIES
CONVERSATION_MEMBERSHIP for affected conversation
PUBLIC_KEYS for affected recipients
PROTOCOL compatibility
```

Non-critical scopes may retry later.

---

# Chapter 2697 — Synchronisation Progress

Initial or full sync progress may show:

```text
Preparing
Loading conversations
Loading messages
Updating encryption keys
Restoring offline work
Finishing
```

The interface shall not expose misleading exact percentages when totals are unknown.

---

# Chapter 2698 — Synchronisation Cancellation

User cancellation may be allowed during a long non-essential full cache rebuild.

Cancellation shall:

* Stop at a safe boundary.
* Preserve last committed checkpoint.
* Leave previous cache active.
* Not corrupt queue state.
* Allow later resume or restart.

Essential post-login security sync may not be cancellable.

---

# Chapter 2699 — Local Cache Eviction and Offline Access

Cache eviction shall not remove:

* Drafts.
* Pending messages.
* Queue payloads.
* Active transfer manifests.
* Required private keys.
* Current policy.
* Essential membership state for pending actions.

Ordinary historical message cache may be evicted by policy.

---

# Chapter 2700 — Evicted Message Retrieval

When online, an evicted message can be fetched again.

When offline:

```text
This message is not stored on this device.
```

The interface shall not show an empty bubble suggesting corruption.

---

# Chapter 2701 — Search Reconciliation

After message sync:

* Add new decrypted messages to the local index.
* Remove deleted messages.
* Replace edited-message tokens.
* Remove inaccessible conversation entries.
* Preserve index transaction integrity.

Search-index update failure shall not roll back the authoritative message cache.

---

# Chapter 2702 — Search Index Rebuild Trigger

Rebuild may be triggered by:

* Index-format upgrade.
* Local integrity failure.
* Key change.
* Cache rebuild.
* User request.
* Large detected inconsistency.

Rebuild shall use locally authorised decrypted cache only.

---

# Chapter 2703 — Local Database Integrity Failure

If integrity verification fails:

```text
Stop ordinary writes.

Preserve database file for diagnostics.

Attempt safe backup.

Offer cache rebuild.

Preserve secure-store keys.

Preserve separately stored prepared attachments.

Require reauthentication where necessary.
```

The client shall not continue using a corrupted database silently.

---

# Chapter 2704 — Corrupted Queue Record Isolation

One corrupted queue record shall not necessarily destroy the entire queue.

The client shall:

* Isolate the record.
* Mark it failed.
* Continue processing unrelated valid actions.
* Preserve diagnostic metadata.
* Notify the user.

If database-wide integrity is uncertain, stop broader processing.

---

# Chapter 2705 — Queue Storage Cleanup

Completed actions may be retained briefly.

Example:

```text
7 days
```

After retention:

* Remove encrypted payload.
* Preserve minimal safe diagnostic result where needed.
* Remove orphaned prepared attachments.
* Maintain failure history only according to policy.

Pending and blocked actions shall never be removed merely due to age without user notice.

---

# Chapter 2706 — Abandoned Queue Warning

If an action remains pending or blocked beyond a threshold:

```text
Your message has been waiting for 3 days.
```

Actions:

```text
Retry now
Edit
Copy
Delete
Run diagnostics
```

The warning shall not expose message content in notifications unless allowed.

---

# Chapter 2707 — OfflineQueueService Contract

Required methods:

```python
async def enqueue(
    self,
    action: PendingOfflineAction,
) -> OfflineAction:
    ...

async def process_pending(
    self,
) -> QueueProcessingResult:
    ...

async def unblock_after_sync(
    self,
    scope: SynchronisationScope,
) -> int:
    ...

async def retry_now(
    self,
    action_id: UUID,
) -> None:
    ...

async def cancel(
    self,
    action_id: UUID,
) -> None:
    ...

async def list_visible(
    self,
) -> list[OfflineActionSummary]:
    ...
```

---

# Chapter 2708 — OfflineActionExecutor Contract

```python
class OfflineActionExecutor:
    async def execute(
        self,
        action: OfflineAction,
        payload: OfflineActionPayload,
    ) -> OfflineActionExecutionResult:
        ...
```

It shall dispatch by validated action type.

It shall not dynamically import arbitrary classes from payload data.

---

# Chapter 2709 — SynchronisationService Contract

Required methods:

```python
async def initial_sync(
    self,
    cancellation_token: CancellationToken,
) -> SynchronisationResult:
    ...

async def reconnect_sync(
    self,
    last_event_id: UUID | None,
) -> SynchronisationResult:
    ...

async def synchronise_conversation(
    self,
    conversation_id: UUID,
) -> SynchronisationResult:
    ...

async def full_resync(
    self,
    cancellation_token: CancellationToken,
) -> SynchronisationResult:
    ...
```

---

# Chapter 2710 — SynchronisationResult

```python
class SynchronisationResult:
    started_at: datetime
    completed_at: datetime
    scopes_succeeded: tuple[str, ...]
    scopes_failed: tuple[str, ...]
    conversations_updated: int
    messages_added: int
    messages_updated: int
    tombstones_applied: int
    queue_actions_unblocked: int
    requires_reauthentication: bool
    degraded: bool
```

---

# Chapter 2711 — ConflictResolver Contract

```python
class ConflictResolver:
    async def resolve(
        self,
        conflict: SynchronisationConflict,
    ) -> ConflictResolution:
        ...
```

Automatic resolution shall be limited to safe deterministic cases.

User input shall be required when content or intent may be lost.

---

# Chapter 2712 — Automatically Resolvable Conflicts

Examples:

```text
Duplicate identical message submission
Read position already advanced further
Preference already equals requested value
Announcement already acknowledged
Message already deleted as requested
Repeated event ID
Older aggregate version received
```

These may be completed without user interruption.

---

# Chapter 2713 — User-Resolvable Conflicts

Examples:

```text
Edited text conflicts with newer server version
User removed from conversation
Message exceeds new limit
Attachment type newly blocked
Recipient key changed unexpectedly
Conversation deleted
Prepared source file changed
```

The client shall preserve local work and request a decision.

---

# Chapter 2714 — Conflict Record

```python
class SynchronisationConflict:
    id: UUID
    conflict_type: ConflictType
    scope_id: UUID | None
    local_reference: UUID | None
    server_reference: UUID | None
    created_at: datetime
    encrypted_local_context: bytes | None
    safe_summary: str
    resolution_state: str
```

Sensitive local context shall remain encrypted.

---

# Chapter 2715 — Conflict Persistence

Unresolved conflicts shall survive restart.

The user shall be able to return to them through:

```text
Pending actions
Failed messages
Transfer page
Conflict notification
```

Resolved conflict records may be retained briefly for diagnostics.

---

# Chapter 2716 — No Last-Write-Wins for Messages

BlueBubbles shall not silently use last-write-wins for encrypted message edits.

Reasons:

* It may discard user text.
* Timestamps may be unreliable.
* The server version is authoritative.
* Different ciphertexts cannot be meaningfully merged automatically.

Explicit version checks and user review are required.

---

# Chapter 2717 — Last-Write-Wins for Preferences

Last-write-wins may be acceptable for simple user preferences such as:

```text
Muted
Pinned
Archived
Notification level
```

The server timestamp or version shall define the authoritative result.

The client shall update to the returned server value.

---

# Chapter 2718 — Concurrent Message Sending

Multiple clients are not fully supported cryptographically in Version 1.0.

Within one client, concurrent sends to the same conversation shall preserve user order using:

```text
Local sequence
Client-created timestamp
Server-created timestamp
Message UUID
```

The server timestamp remains authoritative for global ordering.

---

# Chapter 2719 — Message Ordering Reconciliation

Local pending messages may appear before server timestamps exist.

After acknowledgement:

* Reorder according to server-created timestamp.
* Avoid disruptive visual movement where possible.
* Preserve a short local pending sequence indicator.
* Use message UUID as a stable tie-breaker.

---

# Chapter 2720 — Equal Server Timestamps

Ordering shall use:

```text
server_created_at
then
message UUID
```

This provides deterministic pagination and local display.

---

# Chapter 2721 — Conversation Activity Reconciliation

Conversation list ordering shall use authoritative:

```text
last_activity_at
```

Local pending messages may temporarily move a conversation upward.

If the message permanently fails, the conversation shall return to authoritative ordering while preserving the failed item inside the chat.

---

# Chapter 2722 — Unread Count Reconciliation

The server remains authoritative for unread state.

The client may optimistically mark a conversation read locally.

On sync:

* Apply server read position.
* Submit queued read update.
* Recalculate count.
* Avoid negative unread values.
* Handle messages arriving during the read update.

---

# Chapter 2723 — Read Update Race

If messages arrive after the queued target message:

* Only messages up to the submitted target become read.
* Later messages remain unread.
* The server shall not interpret the update as `mark everything current and future read`.

---

# Chapter 2724 — Cache-to-Server Comparison

The client may periodically compare:

```text
Conversation versions
Latest message IDs
Unread positions
Membership versions
Key versions
```

It shall not upload local plaintext or search-index data for comparison.

---

# Chapter 2725 — Reconciliation Diagnostics

Safe diagnostic information may include:

```text
Last successful sync
Failed scope
Event cursor age
Pending action count
Blocked action count
Last conflict code
Conversation version mismatch count
```

It shall not include draft or message plaintext.

---

# Chapter 2726 — Offline Privacy

While offline:

* Notification previews shall follow local privacy policy.
* Cached data remains encrypted at rest.
* The client shall lock after configured inactivity where implemented.
* Administrative cached data shall be minimal.
* Server revocation cannot take effect until the client reconnects.

This revocation delay shall be documented as an offline limitation.

---

# Chapter 2727 — Offline Revocation Limitation

A device that remains permanently offline may retain previously authorised cached plaintext access until:

* Offline access duration expires.
* Local application lock occurs.
* Local profile is cleared.
* Device storage is removed or protected externally.

The server cannot remotely erase an offline device.

The application shall not claim otherwise.

---

# Chapter 2728 — Offline Operation Logging

Safe logs may include:

```text
Entered offline state
Reconnect attempt
Sync scope started
Sync scope failed
Queue action type
Queue action state
Conflict category
Transfer resume result
```

Logs shall not include:

```text
Draft text
Message plaintext
Search query
Attachment plaintext
Queue decrypted payload
Private key
```

---

# Chapter 2729 — Offline Metrics

Possible metrics:

```text
Offline duration
Reconnect attempts
Sync duration
Events replayed
Full resync count
Queue actions completed
Queue actions failed
Conflict count by category
Upload chunks resumed
```

User identifiers shall not be used as metric labels.

---

# Chapter 2730 — Unit Tests for Offline Queue

Required tests:

```text
Action payload encrypts and decrypts
Wrong local key fails
Actions process in sequence
Processing lock prevents duplicate processor
Retryable failure enters retry wait
Permanent failure stops retry
Cancelled action never executes
Dependency blocks action
Dependency completion unblocks action
Interrupted processing recovers on startup
Completed action is not replayed unnecessarily
```

---

# Chapter 2731 — Idempotency Tests

Required tests:

```text
Repeated identical message submission returns same result
Conflicting duplicate message ID is rejected
Repeated acknowledgement remains successful
Repeated read update does not move state backwards
Repeated preference update yields final requested value
Client crash after server success does not create duplicate
```

---

# Chapter 2732 — Synchronisation Unit Tests

Required tests:

```text
Initial sync ordering
Reconnect incremental sync
Expired event cursor triggers full scope refresh
Duplicate event ignored
Version gap detected
Cursor advances only after commit
Failed page does not advance cursor
Tombstone applied
Pending local record preserved
Policy sync blocks invalid queued action
```

---

# Chapter 2733 — Conflict Resolution Tests

Required tests:

```text
Identical duplicate resolves automatically
Edit conflict preserves attempted text
Removed member blocks send
Changed key rebuilds envelope
Expired edit saves draft
Blocked attachment offers removal
Conversation deletion creates tombstone
Stale preference resolves to server value
```

---

# Chapter 2734 — Membership Change Tests

Required scenarios:

```text
User removed while message queued
User added while offline
Moderator demoted while offline
Owner transfer while offline
Member removed then re-added
Queued message uses only current active members
Removed user receives no new recipient envelope
```

---

# Chapter 2735 — Key Change Tests

Required tests:

```text
Cached active key remains valid
New primary key invalidates cache
Revoked key rejected
Unexpected fingerprint shows warning
Queued message rebuilt with new key
Historical message still uses old private key
Missing old private key reports correct state
```

---

# Chapter 2736 — Attachment Recovery Tests

Required tests:

```text
Upload resumes missing chunks
Server already has matching chunk
Server has conflicting chunk hash
Upload session expired
Membership changed before finalisation
Source file changed
Original source missing but prepared chunks complete
Download resumes encrypted chunks
Partial plaintext removed after failure
```

---

# Chapter 2737 — Reconnection Integration Test

```text
Authenticate two clients

↓

Disconnect Client A

↓

Create queued messages and draft

↓

Client B sends messages and changes group membership

↓

Reconnect Client A

↓

Refresh policy and membership

↓

Recover missed messages

↓

Block invalid queued action

↓

Submit valid action once

↓

Verify final state on both clients
```

---

# Chapter 2738 — Server Restart Recovery Test

```text
Client queues message during server outage

↓

Server restarts

↓

WebSocket reconnects

↓

Client performs readiness check

↓

Client synchronises

↓

Queue submits message

↓

Server stores one record

↓

Recipient receives one event
```

---

# Chapter 2739 — Client Crash Recovery Test

```text
Server accepts queued message

↓

Client crashes before local completion commit

↓

Client restarts

↓

Action appears pending

↓

Client replays same idempotency key

↓

Server returns existing result

↓

Client marks completed

↓

No duplicate message exists
```

---

# Chapter 2740 — Long Offline Period Test

Test:

```text
Client remains offline beyond normal event-retention window.

Many messages and membership changes occur.

Client reconnects.

Event cursor is rejected.

Client performs full affected-scope resync.

Pending valid actions are preserved.

Invalid actions are blocked.

Final cache matches server state.
```

---

# Chapter 2741 — Offline Expiry Test

Where maximum offline duration is enabled:

```text
Access before expiry succeeds.

Access after expiry requires reconnection.

Clock rollback is detected where supported.

Drafts remain encrypted and preserved.

Successful reconnection restores access.
```

---

# Chapter 2742 — Queue Performance Tests

Test datasets:

```text
100 queued messages
500 preference updates with coalescing
20 prepared attachments
1,000 completed action records
```

Measure:

```text
Startup loading time
Queue processing throughput
Local database write latency
Memory use
UI responsiveness
```

---

# Chapter 2743 — Synchronisation Performance Tests

Test:

```text
1,000 conversations
100,000 cached messages
10,000 missed durable events
500 membership updates
Large key-cache refresh
```

Measure:

* Incremental sync duration.
* Full resync duration.
* Local transaction time.
* Search-index update time.
* GUI responsiveness.

---

# Chapter 2744 — Manual Offline Acceptance Tests

User shall complete:

```text
Open cached conversation offline
Write draft
Queue message
Edit queued message
Cancel queued message
Prepare attachment offline
Reconnect
Observe synchronisation
Confirm one message sent
Resolve one blocked action
Search cached messages
```

---

# Chapter 2745 — Failure Recovery Acceptance Tests

Tester shall verify:

```text
Server unavailable message is clear
Draft remains after restart
Pending message remains after restart
Membership conflict does not lose text
Expired edit becomes draft
Failed attachment can be removed
Diagnostics show last sync and queue state
```

---

# Chapter 2746 — Offline UI Requirements

The UI shall display:

```text
Current connectivity state
Last synchronised time
Pending-message state
Failed-message state
Queue count
Blocked-action warning
Transfer-resume state
Conflict-resolution actions
```

It shall not represent local pending data as server-confirmed.

---

# Chapter 2747 — Offline Status Banner

Example:

```text
You are offline.

Cached messages remain available. New messages will be sent after BlueBubbles reconnects.
```

Actions:

```text
Retry now
View pending actions
Run diagnostics
```

---

# Chapter 2748 — Synchronising Banner

Example:

```text
Connection restored.

BlueBubbles is updating conversations and checking unsent messages.
```

The composer may remain available, but queued actions shall wait until security-relevant sync completes.

---

# Chapter 2749 — Conflict Notification Centre

Unresolved conflicts shall appear in one visible location.

Categories:

```text
Messages requiring attention
Attachments requiring attention
Membership conflicts
Policy conflicts
Key warnings
```

The user shall not have to locate conflicts only through logs.

---

# Chapter 2750 — Offline Data Deletion

When the user clears local data:

* Stop queue processor.
* Cancel active workers.
* Confirm unsent work deletion.
* Delete drafts if confirmed.
* Delete queue payloads.
* Delete prepared attachments.
* Delete local database.
* Delete local keys as selected.
* Remove secure-store entries.
* Return to first-run or login state.

This action cannot be undone locally.

---

# Chapter 2751 — Logout with Pending Work

On logout, the client shall warn when pending work exists.

Example:

```text
You have 3 unsent messages and 1 unfinished upload.
```

Options:

```text
Log out and keep encrypted pending work
Cancel
Delete pending work and log out
```

Keeping pending work shall preserve it for the same user profile only.

---

# Chapter 2752 — Different User Login After Logout

If another user signs in:

* Their profile uses a separate local database and keys.
* Previous user drafts remain inaccessible.
* Previous queue does not process.
* Notifications do not reveal previous user content.
* Shared cached files remain isolated by profile.

---

# Chapter 2753 — Queue Security After Password Change

A directory password change shall not itself invalidate encrypted local pending work.

After successful reauthentication:

* The same user profile may resume.
* Session tokens are replaced.
* Local cryptographic identity remains unless policy changes.
* Queue actions are revalidated normally.

---

# Chapter 2754 — Device Revocation and Local Data

If a device is explicitly revoked through a future device-management feature:

* The server rejects new sessions or key use for that device.
* On reconnection, the client clears active tokens.
* Local profile may be locked.
* Automatic local deletion shall occur only if implemented and documented.
* Previously offline access cannot be prevented until reconnection or local expiry.

Version 1.0 may treat session revocation rather than full device revocation.

---

# Chapter 2755 — Version 1.0 Offline Scope

Version 1.0 shall implement:

```text
Cached conversation access
Cached message access
Encrypted drafts
Encrypted offline action queue
Queued encrypted message sending
Queued read updates
Queued conversation preferences
Queued announcement acknowledgements
Stable idempotency keys
Serial queue processing
Connectivity monitoring
WebSocket reconnection
Incremental event recovery
Expired-cursor full resynchronisation
Conversation and membership reconciliation
Public-key refresh
Message version conflict detection
Membership conflict handling
Attachment upload resume
Attachment download resume
Prepared offline attachments
Local tombstones
Queue management interface
Conflict notification interface
Offline diagnostics
```

---

# Chapter 2756 — Deferred Offline Features

The following may be deferred:

```text
Fully independent offline password authentication
Multi-device queue merging
Cross-device draft synchronisation
Automatic plaintext conflict merging
Peer-to-peer message delivery
LAN discovery without configured server
Distributed event logs
Remote deletion of permanently offline devices
Collaborative editing
Background Windows service queue processing
```

---

# Chapter 2757 — Offline and Synchronisation Summary

BlueBubbles shall remain useful during temporary LAN or server outages without pretending that stale client data is authoritative.

The client may display encrypted local cache data, preserve drafts, prepare attachments and queue selected actions.

Every queued write shall use a stable idempotency key.

The server shall remain authoritative for:

* Authentication.
* Membership.
* Permissions.
* Key state.
* Policy.
* Message versions.
* Deletion state.
* Read positions.

After reconnection, the client shall refresh policy, user state, membership and public keys before submitting queued messages.

Missed durable events shall be recovered incrementally where possible.

Expired event cursors shall trigger targeted or full resynchronisation.

Local pending state shall remain separate from server-confirmed state.

Version conflicts shall never silently discard edited text.

Membership conflicts shall block messages rather than send them to an incorrect recipient set.

Attachment transfers shall resume using verified encrypted chunks.

Queue, draft and transfer records shall remain encrypted locally.

Client crashes after server success shall not create duplicate messages because replay uses stable idempotency identifiers.

Revocation cannot erase or instantly disable a permanently offline device, and this limitation shall be documented honestly.

---

# End of Part 26

Part 27 will define the complete implementation roadmap and development-phase plan, including:

* Project milestones.
* Dependency order.
* Prototype stages.
* Database implementation sequence.
* Authentication milestone.
* Messaging milestone.
* Encryption milestone.
* Attachment milestone.
* Client-interface milestone.
* Administration milestone.
* Testing gates.
* Documentation gates.
* Risk-controlled feature prioritisation.
* Version 1.0 completion criteria.


# Part 27 — Implementation Roadmap and Development-Phase Plan

---

# Chapter 2758 — Roadmap Purpose

This section defines the order in which BlueBubbles shall be designed, implemented, tested and documented.

The roadmap shall ensure that:

* High-risk architecture decisions are tested early.
* Security-critical foundations exist before dependent features.
* Each milestone produces a working result.
* Database migrations remain controlled.
* Client and server contracts stay aligned.
* Cryptography is tested before production messaging depends upon it.
* Automated tests grow with the implementation.
* Documentation is updated continuously.
* Deferred features do not delay Version 1.0.
* The final application can be demonstrated reliably.

The coding AI shall follow the dependency order defined here rather than implementing visually impressive but unsupported features first.

---

# Chapter 2759 — Development Principles

Development shall follow these principles:

```text
Build vertically in tested increments.

Implement foundations before presentation.

Keep the application executable throughout development.

Write tests with each subsystem.

Do not postpone security until the end.

Do not postpone database migrations until the end.

Do not generate every file before running the project.

Do not build the full interface against imaginary services.

Do not optimise before measuring.

Do not add deferred features during Version 1.0 stabilisation.
```

---

# Chapter 2760 — Development Environments

The project shall use separate environments:

```text
Development

Automated testing

Demonstration

Production
```

Development:

* Local or isolated VM.
* Debug logging.
* Synthetic users.
* Mock or test directory provider.
* Replaceable test data.
* No real organisational data.

Testing:

* Disposable PostgreSQL database.
* Disposable Redis instance.
* Temporary filesystem storage.
* Deterministic configuration.
* Automated setup and cleanup.

Demonstration:

* Stable sample data.
* Production-like deployment.
* Clearly labelled as demonstration.
* No real credentials.
* Reliable scripted scenarios.

Production:

* Secure configuration.
* Real TLS.
* Protected secrets.
* Restricted logging.
* Controlled deployment.

---

# Chapter 2761 — Source-Control Strategy

The project shall use Git.

Recommended branches:

```text
main

Stable and releasable code.

development

Integrated development work where used.

feature/<name>

One feature or subsystem.

fix/<name>

One correction.

release/<version>

Optional release stabilisation.
```

For a single-developer NEA, a simpler structure is acceptable:

```text
main
feature branches
```

The main branch shall remain buildable.

---

# Chapter 2762 — Commit Requirements

Commits shall:

* Contain one coherent change.
* Use clear descriptions.
* Include tests for changed behaviour.
* Avoid committing secrets.
* Avoid committing generated build output unnecessarily.
* Avoid combining unrelated refactoring and features.
* Record migration files with matching model changes.

Example messages:

```text
Add typed server configuration loader

Implement user and session schema

Add message envelope canonicalisation tests

Fix upload resume conflict handling
```

---

# Chapter 2763 — Quality Gate Definition

A development phase shall not be considered complete until its gate passes.

A typical quality gate requires:

```text
Formatting passes

Linting passes

Type checking passes

Unit tests pass

Relevant integration tests pass

Database migrations apply from empty state

Application starts where applicable

Documentation reflects the implementation

No known critical security defect remains
```

---

# Chapter 2764 — Continuous Verification Command

The repository should provide one command or script such as:

```text
python scripts/development/run_quality_checks.py
```

or:

```text
make check
```

It shall run the standard local checks in a deterministic order.

Suggested sequence:

```text
Format verification

Linting

Type checking

Unit tests

Selected integration tests

Architecture boundary tests

Secret-pattern scan
```

---

# Chapter 2765 — Roadmap Phase Overview

The Version 1.0 roadmap shall use these phases:

```text
Phase 0 — Requirements and architecture confirmation
```
